context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus alone as the way to the Father establishes the non-negotiable doctrinal position that must be held even as believers adjust their disposition in how they communicate the gospel.

context: for God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son that whosoever believeth in Him should not perish but have everlasting life
point_summary: John 3:16 is cited as the most familiar verse in John's Gospel before being set aside to highlight how John's entire Gospel is saturated with the theme of divine love.

context: as he writes about the miracle that we read of in John chapter 11 when Lazarus is brought up from the dead
point_summary: In John 11, the language surrounding Lazarus's illness and death repeatedly frames Jesus's relationship to Mary, Martha, and Lazarus in terms of love, with even onlooking Jews declaring, "How He loved him."

reference_text: He whom thou lovest is sick
context: when those messengers come to Jesus, they announce it this way, He whom thou lovest is sick
point_summary: John 11:3's phrasing — "He whom thou lovest is sick" — is cited as evidence that John, as eyewitness and human penman, consistently frames Jesus' relationships in the language of love.

reference_text: Jesus loved Martha, and her sister, and Lazarus
context: The Bible then declares in that text of Scripture that Jesus loved Mary and Martha and Lazarus
point_summary: John 11:5 is cited as one of several Gospel of John texts that shaped the Apostle John's firsthand witness to Christ's love, here specifically noting that Jesus loved Mary, Martha, and Lazarus by name.

context: The text declares in John chapter 11 as Jesus wept
point_summary: John 11:35 is cited as evidence, observed even by onlooking Jews, that Jesus genuinely loved Lazarus, illustrating the reality of divine love expressed in human emotion.

reference_text: Then said the Jews, Behold how he loved him!
context: even those Jews that were there, they said, oh, how He loved Him
point_summary: John 11:36 is cited as evidence that even onlooking Jews recognized the love of Christ for Lazarus, illustrating the visible, observable nature of divine love as John the Apostle witnessed and recorded it.

context: John was there sitting as Jesus in John chapter number 13 displayed His humility in washing the feet of the disciples
point_summary: John 13 is cited to show that Jesus concluded his act of servant-humility in washing the disciples' feet by issuing a new commandment—to love one another as he had loved them.

reference_text: A new commandment I give unto you
context: He ends that whole period of time with the disciples at the end of John chapter number 13 by giving them a new commandment
point_summary: John's new commandment in John 13:34 — to love one another as Christ loved — is presented as the culminating charge Jesus gave His disciples, distinguishing it from the older Mosaic command to love.

context: to start John 13, in verse number 1, the Bible says that Jesus loved them until the end.
point_summary: John 13:1's declaration that Jesus loved His disciples "until the end" is presented as the experiential foundation that shaped John's later theological affirmation that "God is love."

context: We already have quoted John 3, in verse number 16.
point_summary: God's love is not merely declared but *manifested*—made visible and knowable—through the sending of His only begotten Son into the world, as 1 John 4:9 confirms what John 3:16 already established.

context: It's in John 17, in verse number 18, that Jesus is praying there before He would go to the cross of Calvary, and we find that He's given great truth in that 17th chapter.
point_summary: Christ's being sent into the world by the Father is cited as the definitive act by which God's love is manifested and made known to mankind.

reference_text: Jesus, He whom thou lovest is sick
context: We dealt with that in John 11 in Sunday school several weeks ago. Jesus, He whom thou lovest is sick.
point_summary: Even when God's love seems absent in suffering—as when Jesus delayed coming to Lazarus—He acts with purposeful intent, and believers must never let difficulty cause them to doubt that love.

context: if you love me, keep my commandments
point_summary: Genuine love for God is authenticated not by verbal profession or merchandise slogans but by obedience to His commandments, as Christ Himself declared in John 14:15.

context: Jesus said to the disciples that you love one another as I have loved you
point_summary: Love for fellow believers, rooted in Christ's command in John 13:34, serves as a visible testimony to the watching world that distinguishes genuine disciples.

context: by this shall all men know that you're my disciples if you love one to another
point_summary: Brotherly love among believers functions as visible testimony to the watching world, distinguishing Christ's disciples by what outsiders can observe and recognize as genuinely different.

context: he said that he came not only to give us eternal life, but to give us abundant life in John chapter number 10 and verse number 10.
point_summary: Christ's gift of abundant life in John 10:10 is cited as the basis for living both through him and for him in grateful response to God's love.

context: Without me, you can do nothing, John 15.
point_summary: Attempting to serve God independently of Christ guarantees weariness, because "without me, you can do nothing" means believers must rely entirely on his power rather than their own strength.

context: for God so loved the world
point_summary: God's universal love for all humanity is affirmed through John 3:16, paired with the sobering truth that those who perish do so not from lack of divine love but from rejecting salvation's free gift.

context: my sheep hear my voice and I know them
point_summary: Christ's promise that His sheep hear His voice and receive eternal life grounds the assurance that God preserves believers, securing their future inheritance in heaven.

context: I give unto them eternal life and they shall never perish
point_summary: God's preservation of believers is grounded in Christ's own promise to give eternal life to His sheep, so that they shall never perish.

context: The Bible uses the term to be born again.
point_summary: Born again is not a gradual process but a miraculous moment of salvation, as Jesus declared to Nicodemus and as the preacher explained to a woman who had never understood the term.

context: Nicodemus comes to Jesus by night. And Jesus says, marvel not that I said to thee, you must be born again.
point_summary: Being born again is not a process but a miracle of a moment—trusting Christ alone as Savior—grounded in Jesus's own words to Nicodemus in John 3.

context: Jesus said in chapter number 10 that my sheep hear my voice and I know them and I give unto them eternal life and they shall never perish.
point_summary: Christ's gift of eternal life to His sheep is irrevocable—good works neither secure salvation nor maintain it, since believers are kept by God's power alone.

context: The Bible tells us in John 8, he's a thief and he's a murderer.
point_summary: Satan's character as thief, murderer, and father of lies—drawn from John 8—is cited to establish that he imitates rather than creates, stealing and corrupting the truths God gives his people.

reference_text: He must increase, but I must decrease.
context: But the reality this morning is that He must increase and I must decrease.
point_summary: Pride's danger to the discipleship mandate is exposed by John the Baptist's principle—"He must increase, I must decrease"—showing that investing in others requires subordinating personal importance to God's greater work.

context: Jesus said in John 14, I am the way, and as I got right there, he said the truth and the life, no man cometh unto the Father but by me
point_summary: Sincerity alone is insufficient for salvation — Jesus declared Himself the exclusive way to the Father, a truth the man had memorized but ultimately rejected.

context: But we find that in John chapter number 15, we often reference the beginning of that portion of Scripture. If you want to take a moment to turn there, in John chapter number 15, I mentioned it actually this morning, how that we are the branches, but he is the vine, and the importance
point_summary: Rebekah's barrenness and Isaac's prayer for her to bear fruit opens into the spiritual parallel of John 15, where believers as branches depend on Christ the vine to produce fruit through prayer.

context: I am the true vine, and my Father is the husbandman.
point_summary: Fruit-bearing is God's will and plan for every believer, but it is only possible through abiding in Christ the vine, since apart from him we can do nothing.

context: Every branch in me that beareth not fruit, he taketh away. And every branch that beareth fruit, he purgeth it, that it may bring forth more fruit.
point_summary: God's purging of fruit-bearing branches in John 15:2 illustrates that bearing spiritual fruit is God's will and plan, requiring believers to pray and depend on him rather than produce fruit independently.

context: now ye are clean through the word which I have spoken unto you
point_summary: Spiritual fruitbearing is impossible apart from abiding in Christ, the vine, because the branch has no capacity to produce fruit on its own.

context: Abide in me, and I in you, as the branch cannot bear fruit of itself
point_summary: Abiding in Christ as the vine is the indispensable condition for bearing spiritual fruit, since apart from him the branch can do nothing.

context: I am the vine, ye are the branches. He that abideth in me, and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit
point_summary: Bearing spiritual fruit is impossible apart from abiding in Christ, the vine, because without him believers can do nothing—making prayer for fruitfulness a request God is delighted to answer.

context: If a man abide not in me, he is cast forth as a branch, and is withered
point_summary: Bearing spiritual fruit is God's will and plan for every believer, and abiding in Christ as the vine is the indispensable condition for producing it, since apart from Him nothing can be accomplished.

context: If ye abide in me, and my words abide in you, ye shall ask what ye will, and it shall be done unto you
point_summary: Prayer for fruitfulness is one God delights to answer, because bearing much fruit is explicitly his will and glorifies the Father.

context: herein is my Father glorified that ye bear much fruit. So shall ye be my disciples.
point_summary: Bearing much fruit glorifies the Father, and this is possible only through abiding in Christ the vine—the branch itself produces nothing apart from that connection.

context: God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son
point_summary: God's universal love expressed in John 3:16 stands as a counter-witness against any reading of Romans 9 that would make God the author of individual damnation.

context: what Jesus said to Nicodemus, ye must be born again
point_summary: Regeneration produces two natures within the believer — an older "old man" and a younger "new man" — making Christ's command to Nicodemus the interpretive key for the elder-serves-younger symbolism in Genesis 25.

context: But before we get there, I'm going to be reading a passage from John 17. And so perhaps you want to start in John chapter number 17.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And now I am no more in the world, but these are in the world. So I want you to pay attention as we read through and notice this word world that is used so often. It says, And I come to thee, Holy Father, keep through thine own name those whom thou hast given me, that they may be one as we are.
point_summary: Christ's high-priestly prayer establishes that believers remain in the world but are not of it, providing the theological backdrop for John's stern warning against loving the world in 1 John 2:15–17.

context: While I was with them in the world, I kept them in thy name. Those that thou gavest me I have kept, and none of them is lost, but the son of perdition, that the Scripture might be fulfilled.
point_summary: In Jesus' high-priestly prayer, the repeated contrast between believers who are *in* the world yet *not of* the world frames the stern warning of 1 John 2:15–17 against loving that same world.

context: Verse 13, And now come I to thee, in these things I speak in the world, that they might have my joy fulfilled in themselves.
point_summary: Christ's prayer that believers have his joy fulfilled in them, spoken while still in the world, frames the stern warning of 1 John 2:15–17 within his deliberate intention for those who remain in but are not of the world.

context: I have given them thy word, and the world hath hated them, because they are not of the world, even as I am not of the world.
point_summary: Believers are hated by the world precisely because, having received God's Word, they do not belong to it—just as Christ himself did not belong to it.

context: I pray not that thou shouldest take them out of the world, but that thou shouldest keep them from the evil.
point_summary: Christ's prayer in John 17:15 reveals that believers are intentionally left in the world not to escape it, but to serve as God's representatives and witnesses.

context: Sanctify them through thy truth, thy word is truth.
point_summary: Christ's prayer in John 17 establishes that believers are left in the world not to be removed from it but to serve as His representatives and witnesses while being kept from evil.

context: As thou hast sent me into the world, even so have I also sent them into the world.
point_summary: Believers are left in the world not to escape it but to serve as Christ's sent representatives and witnesses, mirroring the Father's sending of the Son.

context: And for their sakes I sanctified myself, that they also might be sanctified through the truth.
point_summary: Christ's prayer in John 17 establishes that believers are left in the world not to escape it but to serve as His representatives and witnesses within it.

context: I pray not that thou shouldest take them out of the world, but that thou shouldest keep them, notice, from the evil.
point_summary: Believers are left in the world not to be removed from it but to serve as Christ's sent representatives, kept from evil rather than extracted from it.

context: as thou hast sent me into the world, even so have I also sent them into the world.
point_summary: Believers are sent into the world as Christ's representatives and witnesses, yet kept in it by divine design rather than removed at salvation, creating the tension addressed throughout this passage.

context: we're in the world, but we're not of the world
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus said that he left us in this world, but we're not of the world
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: passage of scripture in John 4, when Jesus has interaction with the woman at the well
point_summary: Jesus's question to the woman at the well — "If thou knewest the gift of God" — is introduced against the backdrop of a commercialized Christmas culture that fixates on earthly gifts while missing the supreme gift Christ offers.

context: And he must needs go through Samaria.
point_summary: Christ's divine necessity to pass through Samaria sets the scene for his encounter with the woman at the well, where he reveals himself as the giver of living water.

context: Now Jacob's well was there. Jesus therefore being wearied with his journey sat thus on the well, and it was about the sixth hour.
point_summary: Christ's genuine weariness at Jacob's well establishes the human frailty of the incarnate Son before the sermon's central contrast—that this same weary traveler offers the gift of living water.

context: There cometh a woman of Samaria to draw water. Jesus saith unto her, give me to drink.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: For his disciples were gone away unto the city to buy meat.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Then saith the woman of Samaria unto him, how is it that thou, being a Jew, askest drink of me? Which I'm a woman of Samaria. For the Jews have no dealings with the Samaritans.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: As Jesus responds to this woman at the well,
point_summary: Christ himself is the gift of God referenced in John 4:10, and the woman's ignorance of his identity meant she had not yet asked him for the living water he alone could give.

context: he answered and said unto her, if thou knewest the gift of God, and who it is that saith to thee, give me to drink, thou wouldest have asked of him, and he would have given thee living water.
point_summary: Jesus's offer of "living water" to the Samaritan woman reveals that the greatest gift ever given is Christ himself — the gift of God that most people, then and now, do not recognize.

context: when Jesus says to the woman at the well, if thou knewest the gift of God
point_summary: The "gift of God" in John 4:10 is identified as eternal life through Jesus Christ (Romans 6:23), received not by human merit but through Christ's substitutionary atonement and resurrection.

context: they're like that woman at the well that came to simply draw water and had no idea that she was gonna meet Jesus on that particular day. And Jesus said, if thou knewest the gift of God.
point_summary: Identifying Jesus's words to the woman at the well with Romans 6:23, the sermon frames "the gift of God" as eternal life through Christ—the greatest and most perfect gift, unknown to many who remain spiritually unaware like the woman herself.

context: He says in John, it is finished. It's paid for, paid in full.
point_summary: Christ's cry "It is finished" in John 19:30 is presented as the moment the gift of salvation was paid in full through the shedding of His blood, completing the entire sacrificial typology from Eden through the tabernacle.

context: when Jesus says to the woman at the well, if thou knewest the gift of God
point_summary: Unlike consumer goods subject to supply-chain shortages, the gift Christ offered the woman at the well—eternal life purchased by His shed blood—is available to every person without exception.

context: And Jesus says to the woman at the well, if thou knewest the gift of God, if you knew it. You've got to understand that in John chapter 4, if you've held your place there, or if not, turn back there and notice what is happening here.
point_summary: Unlike seasonal gifts that go out of stock, the gift Jesus offers the Samaritan woman—crossing every ethnic and social barrier to reach her—is universally available to all who would ask.

context: ...through John 4, that God has a gift for us...
point_summary: Salvation is presented in John 4 as a gift of eternal life that cannot be earned or worked for, only personally received or rejected.

context: And we know this gift of God that is spoken of in John chapter number 4.
point_summary: Eternal life through Christ cannot be earned but only received or rejected, as John 4's "gift of God" is freely available to all yet never forced upon anyone.

context: remember, he says in verse number 10, the beginning of their interaction, if thou knewest the gift of God.
point_summary: Knowing the gift of God transforms the recipient into a witness, as Christ's words to the Samaritan woman—she did not yet know the gift, but it was about to be revealed—demonstrate.

context: He said, if you knew what it was, you would have asked me, and I would have given to thee living water.
point_summary: Christ's offer of living water in John 4:10 presupposes the woman's ignorance of the gift, which the sermon identifies as eternal life—a spiritual reality she initially misunderstood by thinking on the natural plane.

context: The woman saith, in verse number 11, Sir, thou hast nothing to draw with. The well is deep. From whence then hast thou that living water?
point_summary: The woman's puzzled question about drawing water reveals she had not yet grasped the spiritual nature of the gift Jesus offered, still thinking entirely on the natural plane.

context: And art thou greater than our father Jacob? Yes, absolutely, he is God. Which gave us the well, and drank there of himself, and his children, and his cattle.
point_summary: The woman's question about Jacob in John 4:12 illustrates that she remained on a natural, physical plane of thinking, not yet grasping the spiritual reality of the gift Christ was offering.

context: Jesus answered and said unto her, whosoever drinketh of this water shall thirst again.
point_summary: Earthly water satisfies only temporarily, a contrast Jesus uses to expose the woman's still-physical thinking before revealing the spiritual gift of living water.

context: Notice verse number 14, but whosoever drinketh of the water that I shall give him shall never thirst, but the water that I shall give him shall be in him a well of water springing up into everlasting life.
point_summary: Contrasted with the physical water that leaves one thirsting again, the living water Christ offers satisfies permanently—a spiritual gift the woman had not yet grasped, still thinking on the natural plane.

context: Jesus saith unto her, woman, believe me, the hour cometh when ye shall neither in this mountain nor yet at Jerusalem worship the Father.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Ye worship, ye know not. What? know what we worship, notice this, for salvation is of the Jews.
point_summary: Salvation being "of the Jews" is explained as the fulfillment of messianic promise through the Abrahamic line, through which Christ came into the world.

context: But the hour cometh, verse 23, and now is when the true worshippers shall worship the Father in spirit and in truth, for the Father seeketh such to worship him.
point_summary: True worship of the Father is no longer tied to a geographic location but is now offered in spirit and in truth — a transition the woman begins to grasp as she recognizes Jesus as Messiah.

context: God is a spirit. They that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth.
point_summary: God being spirit requires that true worship be offered in spirit and in truth — a condition the woman begins to grasp as she moves from physical thinking toward recognizing the Messiah before her.

context: the woman saith unto him, I know that Messiah is cometh, which is called Christ. When he is come, he will tell us all things.
point_summary: The woman's confession that she knows Messiah is coming becomes the very opening through which Jesus declares his identity directly: "I that speak unto thee am he."

context: And Jesus saith unto her, I that speak unto thee am he.
point_summary: Christ's self-disclosure — "I that speak unto thee am he" — is the moment the woman recognizes the gift of God as Messiah himself, moving her immediately to evangelize her city.

context: the disciples were gone away into the city to buy meat
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: they marveled that he talked to the woman
point_summary: The disciples' silent astonishment at Jesus speaking with the Samaritan woman (v. 27) frames her immediate departure to share the gift she had just received, illustrating that true encounter with Messiah compels witness.

context: But I want you to notice in verse 28 that the woman then left her water pot and went her way into the city, and saith to the men, Come, see a man which told me all things that ever I did. Is not this the Christ?
point_summary: The woman's abandonment of her water pot to proclaim "Is not this the Christ?" illustrates that genuine recognition of the gift of God naturally compels the recipient to share it with others.

context: Come, see a man which told me all things that ever I did. Is not this the Christ?
point_summary: Having come to recognize Jesus as the Christ, the woman's immediate response is to share the gift she has received, testifying to others and drawing many Samaritans to believe.

context: if thou knewest the gift of God, she didn't know it, now as her eyes have been opened, as Jesus deals with her, she now has her eyes opened, and she says, Is not this the Christ?
point_summary: Once ignorant of "the gift of God," the Samaritan woman's eyes are opened to Christ's identity and she immediately becomes a witness, illustrating the natural impulse to share the gift once received.

context: and many of the Samaritans of the city believed on him for the saying of the woman, which testified he told me all that ever I did.
point_summary: The woman's testimony about Christ—once she herself received the gift of God—became the instrument through which many Samaritans believed, illustrating that understanding the gift naturally produces a desire to share it.

context: So when the Samaritans were come unto him, they besought him that he would tarry with them, and he abode there two days.
point_summary: The woman's testimony drew the Samaritans to Christ, who then stayed two days among them — illustrating how receiving the gift of God naturally compels its bearer to share it with others.

context: And many more believed because of his own word,
point_summary: The woman's testimony drew many to Christ initially, but John 4:41 marks a deeper work: the Samaritans' faith moved from secondhand witness to personal encounter with his own word.

context: And said unto the woman, Now we believe, not because of thy saying: for we have heard him ourselves, and know that this is indeed the Christ, the Saviour of the world.
point_summary: The woman's testimony sparked initial belief, but John 4:42 marks the Samaritans' faith maturing beyond her witness to personal encounter with Christ as Savior of the world.

context: John the Baptist says, behold the Lamb of God.
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration "Behold the Lamb of God" is cited as the proclamation that prompted disciples to follow Jesus and, in turn, bring others to him, illustrating the gift's redemptive purpose through substitutionary atonement.

context: Jesus said, what seek ye? And they say, where dwellest thou?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And he said, come and see.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And the Bible says that in verse number 40, one of the two which heard John speak and followed him was Andrew, Simon Peter's brother.
point_summary: Andrew's response to encountering Christ—immediately finding his brother Simon and bringing him to Jesus—illustrates the natural impulse of one who has received the gift of God to testify and lead others to him.

context: He first findeth his own brother Simon and saith unto him, we have found the Messiah, which is being interpreted, the Christ.
point_summary: Andrew's immediate response upon finding the Messiah was to bring his brother Simon to Jesus, illustrating the natural impulse to testify and lead others to Christ after receiving the gift of God.

context: And I want you to notice this phrase in verse number 42, and he brought him to Jesus.
point_summary: Andrew's act of bringing his brother Simon to Jesus illustrates the believer's response to receiving Christ: knowing the gift of God compels testimony and bringing others to him.

context: This morning, if thou knewest the gift of God, we consider his gift to us, it's eternal life.
point_summary: Recognizing Jesus as the gift of God—the Messiah who came in the fullness of time to redeem through substitutionary atonement—should move recipients to testify of him and bring others to him.

context: He said, if you knew it, you'd ask for it and I'd give it to you.
point_summary: The gift of God in John 4:10 — salvation purchased by Christ's blood and proven through His resurrection — is freely available to any who, like the woman at the well, will simply receive it.

context: This morning, he is the Savior of the world.
point_summary: Christ's identity as "the Savior of the world" (John 4:42) grounds the closing appeal: the gift available to every listener is the same gift offered to the woman at the well.

context: convict and convince of sin and of Christ's righteousness and of judgment to come
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's convicting work—bringing sinners to awareness of sin, righteousness, and judgment—is invoked as the necessary divine agent drawing unbelievers to receive the gift of salvation.

context: We're familiar with that when we read John 3, 16, for God so loved the world.
point_summary: The word "so" in "the famine was so sore in all lands" is linked to the same word in John 3:16 to underscore the intensity of emphasis God places on both the famine's severity and His love.

context: And we come to the second chapter, and we find that this is the first miracle of the Lord Jesus Christ. In verse 11, it says, this beginning of miracles did Jesus in Cana of Galilee.
point_summary: Pharaoh's command to the famished Egyptians—"whatsoever he saith to you, do"—is read as a typological anticipation of Mary's identical instruction to the servants at Cana, linking Joseph's role as bread-provider to Christ's first miracle.

context: his mother saith unto the servants, whatsoever he saith unto you, do it.
point_summary: Mary's instruction to the servants at Cana — "whatsoever he saith unto you, do it" — is drawn as a parallel to Pharaoh's command, illustrating the timeless call to simple, unqualified obedience to Christ's word.

context: For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son, right?
point_summary: Missions is the heartbeat of God because, as John 3:16 states, God so loved the world that He gave His Son to save sinners—making world evangelism an expression of God's own heart.

context: my sheep hear my voice and I know them and I give unto them eternal life and they shall never perish
point_summary: Eternal security, grounded in Christ's promise that his sheep receive eternal life and shall never perish, is cited as assurance that believers can confidently call others to salvation.

context: but as many as received him in verse number 12, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name
point_summary: Receiving Christ grants the power to become sons of God, making all believers genuine brothers and sisters in one family — the basis on which John identifies himself as brother to suffering saints.

context: The Word was made flesh and dwelt among us, John one in verse 14.
point_summary: The title "Word of God" applied to Christ in Revelation 1:16 is grounded in John 1:14's declaration that the Word became flesh, identifying Jesus as the living Word distinct from, yet related to, the written Word.

context: Jesus said this in verse 19 of John 2 he said destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up
point_summary: Jesus' declaration "destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up" is understood as a reference to his own body—his death and resurrection—not the physical structure.

context: I am the way the truth and the life
point_summary: The tabernacle's single entrance typologically illustrates John 14:6's claim that Jesus is the only way to heaven, with no alternative access to God.

context: therefore doth my father love me because I lay
point_summary: Christ's willing self-offering on the cross was not coerced but sovereign—he possessed authority both to lay down his life and to take it again in resurrection.

context: he said, it is finished
point_summary: Christ's declaration "It is finished" is presented as the completed, sufficient offering that grounds both the believer's assurance and the open invitation for the unsaved to be redeemed by grace alone.

context: What was Peter saying? Well, he was saying the same thing that Jesus said in John 14. I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus Christ is the exclusive way to the Father—a claim the sermon grounds in both John 14:6 and Peter's parallel declaration in Acts 4:12 that no other name saves.

context: Father, I pray that if there's one that's not sure of heaven, it's our prayer this morning that the Holy Spirit of God would do the work that was outlined in John 16.
point_summary: John 16's teaching on the Holy Spirit's convicting work—of sin, righteousness, and judgment—is invoked as the divine means by which the unsaved may come to assurance of salvation.

context: the Bible says in the beginning was the word, and the word was with God, and the word was God
point_summary: Christ's identity as Creator is confirmed by linking John 1:1's "Word" who was God to Hebrews 1:2's declaration that the Son made the worlds.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me
point_summary: Christ's exclusive role as Savior—"the way, the truth, and the life, no man cometh unto the Father but by me"—grounds the sermon's claim that salvation comes through him alone.

context: He said, Father, the hour is come. In John chapter number 17, He knew that it was exactly why He came to this earth.
point_summary: John 17 is cited briefly to show that Christ understood the cross was the precise fulfillment of his redemptive purpose in coming to earth.

context: he obeyed always
point_summary: Christ's own unbroken obedience—"he obeyed always"—is held up as the pattern that a life genuinely shaped by considering Him will continuously follow.

context: The world hates you? Don't be surprised by that. They hated Jesus.
point_summary: Faithfulness unto death, even amid the world's hatred, is not surprising—for Christ himself warned his disciples that the world's rejection of them follows from its prior rejection of him.

context: understanding that Jesus did come, that he did complete the work of redemption. The plan of redemption has been completed. He said it is finished
point_summary: Christ's declaration "It is finished" signals the completed plan of redemption, placing believers on the far side of Calvary looking back, unlike Old Testament saints who looked forward to Messiah.

context: And you'll remember in John chapter number 3 and verse number 2, Jesus is there and Nicodemus comes to Jesus by night, and he makes the statement, We know that thou art a teacher sent from God.

context: God is a spirit, and they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth.

context: Jesus was the one that said in John four, thy must needs go through Samaria.

context: I was reading this morning in John 14, where Jesus said, my peace I give unto you. Not as the world giveth.

context: when He, the Spirit of truth, has come, that He will lead you into all truth

context: He's a liar, John 8, the father of it, right?
point_summary: Satan is identified as a liar and the father of lies (John 8:44), establishing his fundamental character as the one who desires to deceive and lead believers astray through false teaching.

context: Behold, the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world.

context: He said on the cross of Calvary it is Finished

context: I will come again and receive you unto myself that where I am, there ye may be also.

context: And we'd understand that even as Jesus said to the disciples in John 11 that Lazarus was asleep, he said he was dead.

context: He came to his own, but his own received him not.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.

context: God sent not a son into the world to condemn the world, but the world through him might be saved

context: But we find that the Bible tells us that we're condemned already if we don't believe. And so we understand that we look through, in verse 18 of John three, he that believeth on him is not condemned, but he that believeth not is condemned already.

context: Remember in the Gospel of John when Jesus healed the man that was born blind and there's all kinds of stirring that takes place and the religious leaders at the end of the text, they say this in John nine, are we blind also?
point_summary: The Pharisees' question "Are we blind also?" (John 9) illustrates that Israel's leadership could see physically yet remained spiritually blind—a condition Paul identifies as the veil still resting on their hearts in the reading of the Old Testament.

context: He that believeth not is condemned already.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Sanctify them by thy word. Thy word is truth, John 17 in verse 17.

context: sanctify them through thy Word. Thy Word is truth.

context: we're in the world but not of the world

context: what Jesus said once again in John 17 that we're in the world, but we're not of the world

context: These things I have spoken unto you, that in me you might have peace. In the world you shall have tribulation, but be of good cheer, I have overcome the world.

context: fear not daughter of Zion, behold thy King cometh sitting on an ass's colt

context: from John chapter 12 and the triumphal entry of the Lord Jesus Christ

context: in John chapter 13, God provides us with the record of Jesus washing the feet of the disciples

context: after that portion of scripture in John 13

context: to conclude the 13th chapter, chapter 14

context: chapter 14, 15 and 16

context: Jesus in the gospel of John regularly refers to his hour. And he speaks about in John chapter number two that his hour is not yet come

context: As we look at John 16 and verse number 33, I find that there's something for us to embrace, something for the disciples to embrace, but this morning for each of us

context: He begins chapter 14 with that wonderful promise that he's going to prepare a place for us and he's going to come again and what joy that is. But if you look in chapter 14 and verse 25, notice what Jesus says. He says, these things have I spoken unto you, being yet present with you.

context: but the comforter, which is the Holy Ghost, whom the Father will send in my name, he shall teach you all things and bring all things to remembrance, whatsoever notice I have said unto you.

context: I will not leave you comfortless. I will come to you.

context: These things have I spoken unto you, that my joy might remain in you, and that your joy might be full.

context: Look at verse 17 here in John 15. He says, these things I command you that you love one another.

context: John 16 and verse number 1, these things have I spoken unto you that you should not be offended.

context: Look in verse number four of John 16, but these things have I told you that when the time shall come, you may remember that I told you of them.

context: We find in verse seven of John 16, Jesus says, nevertheless, I tell you the truth. I tell you, he's speaking to them. It is expedient for you that I go away.

context: And Jesus concludes once again in chapter 16 and verse number 33, these things I have spoken unto you.

context: these things I have spoken unto you.

context: my peace give I unto thee.
point_summary: Christ's peace is distinguished from worldly peace as uniquely his own—"my peace"—a gift that surpasses understanding and is meant to rule the believer's heart amid tribulation.

context: in me, you might have peace, but he follows it up and says this, in the world, you shall have tribulation.

context: In the world, you shall have tribulation. But in me, you might have peace.

context: Jesus says, I've spoken these things, these things I have spoken unto you that in me, you might have peace. In the world, you shall have tribulation.

context: Jesus says let not your heart be troubled.

context: In me, Jesus said, you might have peace. In the world, there's going to be tribulation.

context: I find in John 16 and verse 33, there's something to embrace, there's something to expect, but I find there's someone to exalt. In John 16 and verse 33, there's someone to exalt. He says, in the world you shall have tribulation, but be of good cheer. Why? I have overcome the world.

context: But Jesus says, Be of good cheer, I have overcome the world

context: Jesus Christ said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the father but by me.

context: Jesus Christ said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.

context: Because Jesus said I am the way, the truth, and the life, no man cometh unto the Father but by me.

context: after these things came Jesus and His disciples into the land of Judea. And there he tarried with them and baptized.

context: for John was not yet cast into prison.

context: and they came unto John and said unto him, Rabbi, he that was with thee beyond Jordan, to whom thou bearest witness, behold, the same baptizeth, and all men come to him.

context: He must increase, but I must decrease.

context: He must increase, I must decrease

context: In our text of Scripture here in John chapter number 6, and I'm going to jump into the end of the passage. The Bible says this in John chapter 6 and verse number 66,

context: Verse 67, Peter said, Jesus unto the twelve, his twelve disciples, he says this, Will ye also go away?

context: Notice what he says, Lord, to whom shall we go? Thou hast the words of eternal life.

context: And we believe and are sure that thou art that Christ, the Son of the living God.

context: in the beginning of the passage of Scripture, in John chapter number 6, that God records for us and provides us with the record, the account, the miracle by which we refer to as the feeding of the 5,000

context: And the Bible tells us in verse number 25, notice actually verse 24, the Bible says, when the people therefore saw that Jesus was not there,

context: neither his disciples, they also took shipping and came to Capernaum seeking for Jesus. When they had found him on the other side of the sea, they said unto him, Rabbi, when camest thou hither?

context: we see this disturbing trend where the Bible says that many, many of his disciples, notice where it says went back. Went back. Went back to what? Their own lives. point_summary: Many disciples turning back to their former lives and walking no more with Jesus is framed as a disturbing, heart-breaking trend of abandonment rather than mere disagreement.

context: Jesus answered them and said, Verily, verily, I say unto you, ye seek me, notice, not because ye saw the miracles, but because ye did eat of the loaves and were filled.

context: It is the spirit that quickeneth, the flesh profiteth nothing. The words that I speak unto you, they are spirit and they are life.

context: From that time many of his disciples went back and walked no more with him.
point_summary: Many who initially sought and followed Jesus ultimately turned away when his teaching demanded personal faith rather than material benefit, illustrating that not all who seek Jesus are truly interested in living for him.

context: Jesus then focuses his attention in verse 67 back in John chapter number six to the 12, to his disciples, and notice it's a very direct question.

context: then said Jesus under the 12, will you also go away?

context: Simon refers to Jesus as Lord in verse number 68

context: and we believe and are sure that thou art that Christ

context: John six, it's a sad record of many that departed.

context: he said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Christ alone is the source of eternal life — the only one who can provide salvation — as affirmed by his own words in John 14:6 and echoed in Peter's declaration in Acts 4.

context: And when again into the judgment hall and saith unto Jesus, whence art thou? But Jesus gave him no answer.
point_summary: Pilate's boast of power over Christ in John 19:9–10 is used to contrast human presumption with God's omnipotence, which Christ's silent witness before Pilate actually demonstrates.

context: Knowest thou not that I have power to crucify thee and have power to release thee?
point_summary: Pilate's claim to hold power over Jesus is corrected by Christ himself, who declares that all earthly authority exists only because it is granted from above.

context: thou couldest have no power at all against me except it were given thee from above. Therefore he that delivered me unto thee hath the greater sin.
point_summary: All earthly authority—even Pilate's power to crucify or release—is derivative, granted from above, so that no human power operates independently of God's sovereign allowance.

context: As he hung upon that cross, several things that he cried out, but one was this, it is finished.
point_summary: Christ's declaration "It is finished" from the cross is presented as the completion of his willing, substitutionary sacrifice — the act that alone secured humanity's only hope of salvation.

context: When you come to John chapter number 11, and both Mary and Martha say, Jesus, if you had been here, our brother had not died.
point_summary: Christ's power over death, displayed when he raised Lazarus after four days in the tomb, is cited as evidence that he alone possesses immortality and authority over death itself.

context: The fact that He said in John chapter number 14, let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in Me. In My Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you. And then He says this, I go to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto Myself that where I am, there ye may be also.
point_summary: Christ's promised return in John 14 is presented as an unalterable certainty—no person, situation, or circumstance can prevent God from keeping His word to come again and receive His own.

point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: let not your heart be troubled

context: John 2 and chapter 3, I do not desire to be ashamed of him at his appearing.

point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life
point_summary: God's benevolent, giving nature—illustrated by the greatest gift of His Son in John 3:16—grounds the charge to trust in the living God rather than uncertain riches.

context: I mean, John 15, if you don't bear fruit, they're gonna cut the tree down.
point_summary: Fruitlessness invites judgment: John 15 is invoked to show that God's expectation of spiritual growth carries consequence, as branches that bear no fruit are cut down.

context: John chapter number 1 as we read this very important passage of Scripture dealing with the truth of the Incarnation and the eternality of the Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: John 1:1 is cited to establish the Incarnation and eternality of Christ as the theological ground for understanding how divine strength operates through human weakness.

context: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
point_summary: John 1:1, read alongside verse 14, establishes Christ's eternality and incarnation as the theological ground from which His fullness of grace flows to believers.

context: The same was in the beginning with God.
point_summary: John 1:2 is cited as part of a passage establishing Christ's eternality and incarnation, grounding the sermon's claim that grace flows from One who is both fully God and "full of grace and truth."

context: All things were made by Him. Without Him was not anything made that was made.
point_summary: John 1:3 is cited as part of a broader reading of John 1:1–18 to establish Christ's eternality and incarnation as the foundation for understanding Him as the source of grace.

context: In Him was life, and the life was the light of men.
point_summary: In Him was life, and that life was the light of men — John 1:4 is read here as part of the Incarnation passage establishing that Christ, the eternal Word made flesh, is the source of grace received by believers.

context: And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us.
point_summary: John 1:14 is read to establish that the incarnate Word is "full of grace and truth," grounding the sermon's claim that all grace flows from Christ himself.

context: John bare witness of Him, and cried, saying, This was He of whom I spake. He that cometh after me is preferred before me.
point_summary: John 1:15's testimony that Christ "was before" John establishes the eternality of Christ, grounding the fullness of grace believers receive in One who is eternal and preexistent.

context: received, and grace for grace.
point_summary: Grace flows from Christ's fullness to believers continuously — John 1:16's "grace for grace" grounding the present-tense sufficiency of "My grace is sufficient for thee."

context: For the law was given by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Grace and truth came by Jesus Christ, establishing Him as the gracious one whose sufficiency for believers in the present moment is grounded in who He is by nature.

context: No man hath seen God at any time. The only begotten Son, which is in the bosom of the Father, He hath declared Him.
point_summary: John 1:18's identification of Christ as the one who "declared" the Father grounds the claim that He is "full of grace," establishing the divine source of the sufficient grace promised in 2 Corinthians 12:9.

context: In the beginning was the word, and the word was with God, and the word was God
point_summary: John 1:1, read alongside John 1:14, grounds the apostle's eyewitness testimony in 1 John 1 by establishing that the eternal Word who is fully God also became fully human flesh — directly countering Docetist denial of Christ's humanity.

context: and the word was made flesh and dwelt among us
point_summary: John 1:14's declaration that "the Word was made flesh" is cited as direct scriptural confirmation of the incarnation — that Jesus Christ is fully God and fully man — against Docetist denial of his humanity.

context: Notice in John 1, back in the Gospel of John,
point_summary: The eyewitness credibility of 1 John rests on John's repeated, cumulative claims to have heard, seen, and handled the Word of life — sensory testimony that distinguishes his account from secondhand report or conjecture.

context: The Bible says in John 1 in verse number 14, and the Word was made flesh and dwelt among us.
point_summary: John 1:14's declaration that the Word was made flesh and dwelt among us grounds John's eyewitness credibility, as his proximity to the incarnate Christ meant he personally observed both Christ's human limitations and his divine power.

context: He saw the same God who was wearied with his journey that was looking for water that gave freely of the living water that we look at in John 4.
point_summary: John 4's account of Jesus, wearied from his journey yet offering living water to the woman at the well, is cited as evidence that John witnessed firsthand both the human limitations and divine power of the God-man.

context: And John says that he was made flesh and he dwelt among us. Notice the next phrase. And we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.
point_summary: The incarnation—God dwelling among humanity in flesh—established John's eyewitness credibility, as he personally observed both Christ's human attributes and His divine glory.

context: Notice in John one, skip down if you would, to verse number 35. The Bible says, again, the next day after John stood and two of his disciples...
point_summary: John 1:35 is cited to identify the Apostle John as one of the two disciples who responded to John the Baptist's declaration and followed Jesus, establishing his eyewitness credentials from the very beginning of Christ's ministry.

context: ...this is speaking of John the Baptist, it says this in verse number 36, and looking upon Jesus as he walked, notice what John the Baptist says, behold, the Lamb of God.
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration "Behold, the Lamb of God" in verse 36 is presented as the moment John the Apostle himself first encountered and began following Jesus, grounding his eyewitness testimony in personal experience.

context: ...Notice what Jesus says, the invitation that is extended in verse number 39. He saith unto them, come and see. They came and saw where he dwelt and abode with him that day, for it was about the 10th hour.
point_summary: The invitation "come and see" in John 1:39 marks the moment John the Apostle personally witnessed Christ firsthand, establishing the eyewitness foundation underlying his later testimony in 1 John.

context: Andrew in verse number 40, and it says that he first findeth his own brother and saith unto him, notice what he says, and no doubt John would have come to the same conclusion, we have found the Messiah, which is being interpreted, the Christ.
point_summary: Andrew's declaration "We have found the Messiah" in John 1:41 is cited to show that John, as one present at that moment, was an eyewitness from the very beginning of Jesus's ministry.

context: John chapter number 15, turn over, if you will, several chapters, and you note that in this particular section of the Gospel of John, that the hour is nigh, it is not yet come,
point_summary: John 15:27 is cited to establish that the disciples' eyewitness role was not incidental but divinely appointed, grounding the apostolic testimony of 1 John in a direct commission from Christ himself.

context: he makes that statement in John 17, the hour is now come,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: notice what Jesus says in John 15 and verse number 27, and ye also shall bear witness, why? Because ye have been with me from the beginning.
point_summary: Jesus's command in John 15:27 that the disciples bear witness "because ye have been with me from the beginning" is shown to be the direct commission fulfilled in 1 John 1:1–3's eyewitness testimony.

context: Peter, in verse number 20, turning about, seeth the disciple whom Jesus loved, following
point_summary: John 21:20 identifies "the disciple whom Jesus loved" as John, establishing him as an eyewitness present with Jesus and thus qualified to bear the testimony recorded in 1 John 1:1–4.

context: in verse number 24, as the book concludes, the Bible says, this is the disciple which testifieth of these things
point_summary: John 21:24 is cited as the Gospel's own closing attestation that the disciple who was present from the beginning both wrote and testified truthfully, corroborating John's eyewitness authority in 1 John 1.

context: Notice verse 25, and there are also many other things which Jesus did
point_summary: John 21:25 is cited to underscore that John's Gospel records only a selective portion of Jesus's deeds, implying the vastness of what the eyewitness could have testified to.

context: We looked at this verse of scripture, John 21 and verse 25, coupled with the truth in John 20, verse 30 and 31, speaking about the things that were written versus the things that were not written
point_summary: John's closing remark that the world could not contain all that Jesus did establishes the vast, unrecorded scope of his ministry, lending weight to the eyewitness credibility John claims in 1 John 1:1–4.

context: We looked at this verse of scripture, John 21 and verse 25, coupled with the truth in John 20, verse 30 and 31, speaking about the things that were written versus the things that were not written
point_summary: John 20:30–31, paired with John 21:25, establishes that the written Gospel records only a fraction of what Jesus did, making John's eyewitness credibility in 1 John all the more weighty.

context: We looked at this verse of scripture, John 21 and verse 25, coupled with the truth in John 20, verse 30 and 31, speaking about the things that were written versus the things that were not written
point_summary: John 20:31's record of selected signs is paired with John 21:25 to underscore that the written testimony represents only a fraction of what John witnessed, magnifying the credibility of his eyewitness account in 1 John 1.

context: How similar that is to John 1 and verse number 14. He says this, and bear witness and show unto you that eternal life which was with the Father and was manifested unto us.
point_summary: The content of John's eyewitness testimony centers on eternal life—a life that was with the Father and then manifested, paralleling John 1:14's declaration of the Word made flesh.

context: I know my sheep and they hear my voice
point_summary: John's firsthand witness of Jesus declaring "I give unto them eternal life, and they shall never perish" grounds 1 John's repeated emphasis on knowing one's salvation as absolute security, not temporary or probationary.

context: I give unto them eternal life, and they shall never perish
point_summary: John 10:28's promise that Christ's sheep "shall never perish" is presented as an absolute guarantee of eternal security—not temporary or probationary life—which John personally heard Jesus speak.

context: and many other signs truly did Jesus in the presence of His disciples, which are not written in this book
point_summary: John's own stated purpose in 20:30–31 — that readers might believe Jesus is the Christ and receive life through His name — is set alongside 1 John's parallel purpose of full joy, showing each book's distinct but complementary aim.

context: But these are written. Why? That you might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that believing you might have life through His name

context: No man taketh your joy, right? It's what the Word of God tells us.
point_summary: Joy, unlike circumstantial happiness, is an inward, God-given reality that no man can take away—grounded in salvation and undiminished even amid the severest trials.

context: Notice if you will, we'll quickly look at a few verses of Scripture. Turn to John 15.
point_summary: Obedience to Christ's commandments is the condition for abiding in His love, grounding the sermon's point that fullness of joy flows from fellowship and godly living, not salvation alone.

point_summary: Keeping Christ's commandments and abiding in His love is the condition Jesus himself names for fullness of joy—the same fullness John declares as his purpose in writing.

context: These things have I spoken unto you, that my joy might remain in you, and that your joy might be full.
point_summary: Fullness of joy, as Jesus promises in John 15:11, is conditioned on keeping His commandments and abiding in His love—making obedient fellowship, not merely salvation, the pathway to complete joy.

context: By the way, that's what Jesus tells him at the end of John 15. We looked at it earlier in verse number 27.

context: Notice in verse number 12, this is my commandment, that ye love one another as I have loved you.

context: Notice verse number 14, ye are my friends if you do whatsoever I have commanded you.
point_summary: Obedience to Christ's commands is the condition of friendship with Him, and that abiding fellowship is what produces fullness of joy.

context: Notice in John 16, in verse number 24, hitherto have ye asked nothing in my name. Ask and ye shall receive, that your joy may be full.

context: in the New Testament with John three in verse number 30, where John the Baptist said, he must increase and I must decrease.

context: Paul understands, just as we should understand this evening, the truth of John 15, when Jesus said, I am the vine, ye are the branches. Without me, how many things can we do? Nothing.
point_summary: Humility before God rests on Jesus's own declaration that apart from him believers can do nothing, making self-exaltation impossible for those who understand their total dependence on the vine.

context: who is it that sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind?

context: neither this man nor his parents, but for the glory of God

context: He must increase and I must decrease
point_summary: John the Baptist's willingness to surrender his following and position to Christ illustrates the sermon's call to abandon self-centered living in favor of living wholly for Jesus.

context: behold the Lamb of God which taketh away the sin of the world
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration—"Behold the Lamb of God which taketh away the sin of the world"—is cited as the defining moment when he willingly surrendered his own following and prominence to point others entirely to Christ.

context: He must increase, but I must decrease.
point_summary: John the Baptist's willingness to surrender his following and position to Christ illustrates the sermon's call to decrease self so that Christ may increase in one's life.

context: We find that for God so loved the world
point_summary: God's love for the world, cited from John 3:16, is presented as the motivating force that sent and kept Christ on the cross, and that holds believers together rather than letting them fall apart.

context: You read John 17, our memory verse for the month of March

context: but as many as received Him, to them gave He power to become the sons of God
point_summary: Receiving Christ as Savior is a one-time, decisive choice—some reject Him, but those who receive Him are given power to become sons of God.

context: Let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God. Believe also in me.

context: In my Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you.

context: I go to prepare a place for you that, and if I go, I'll come again and receive you. What does it say? And to myself, that where I am, there ye may be also.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:3 to return and receive believers unto himself grounds the assurance that every saved person will live with Christ forever.

context: And so then we find that he, Paul, continues to teach them here, they were in need of instruction, they were in need of encouragement. We find that Jesus, you remember after he told the disciples that he was going to go to the cross of Calvary, he'd been telling them that for many times, but John 13 makes it abundantly clear, and he says in John 14, let not your heart be troubled. And we know that verse of Scripture, that text of Scripture, and it's interesting as he uses that phraseology, it carries with it that word troubled in John 14, one, to take away calmness of mind.
point_summary: The word "troubled" in John 14:1 carries the meaning of having one's calmness of mind taken away—rendered anxious or distressed—the same condition Paul addresses in the Thessalonian church shaken by false teaching.

context: Jesus wept. I memorized John 11.35.
point_summary: Memorizing a verse as short and simple as John 11:35 illustrates the wrong approach to Scripture memory—what matters is knowing verses directly relevant to the specific temptations one faces.

context: The beginning was the Word, the Word was with God, and the Word was God.

context: The Word was made flesh and dwelt among us, and so we know that one of the names of Jesus is the Word,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Look at John 17. We'll start first with the Father. In John chapter number 17, we often refer to this portion of Scripture in John 17 as the Lord's Prayer.
point_summary: John 17 is introduced as the true Lord's Prayer—Christ's own prayer before the cross—to establish that the Father is distinctly addressed and identified as God within the Trinitarian Godhead.

context: These words spake Jesus and lifted up His eyes to heaven and said, Father, the hour has come. Glorify Thy Son that Thy Son also may glorify Thee.
point_summary: In John 17:3, Jesus refers to the Father as "the only true God" while distinguishing Himself as the One sent, establishing the Father's deity as the sermon's first New Testament proof of Trinitarian persons.

context: And Thou hast given Him power over all flesh that He should give eternal life to as many as Thou hast given Him.
point_summary: In John 17:3, Jesus distinguishes the Father as "the only true God" while referring to Himself separately, a distinction the sermon uses to introduce the Father's identity within the triune Godhead.

context: And this is life eternal that they might know Thee, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom Thou hast sent.
point_summary: In John 17:3, Jesus addresses the Father as "the only true God," establishing the Father's identity as God—the first person of the Trinity—while the surrounding verses also affirm Christ's own eternality and deity.

context: I have glorified Thee on the earth. I have finished the work which Thou hast given me, which Thou gavest me to do.
point_summary: In John 17:3, Jesus refers to the Father as "the only true God" while distinguishing Himself as the One sent, establishing the Father's identity as God within the Trinitarian framework being developed.

context: And now, O Father, glorify Thou me with Thine own self, with the glory which I had with Thee before the world was.
point_summary: Christ's pre-creation glory, shared with the Father before the world existed, establishes His eternality and refutes any claim that He is a created being.

context: verse number five when He speaks about the fact that He would have, once again, the glory that He had with the Father before the world was.
point_summary: Christ's pre-existence before creation is affirmed in John 17:5, where Jesus asks to be restored to the glory He shared with the Father before the world was, refuting the claim that He is a created being.

context: But we saw in verse number three where that Jesus says, and this is life eternal, that they might know Thee, the one true, or excuse me, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom Thou hast sent.
point_summary: In John 17:3, Jesus distinguishes the Father as "the only true God" while identifying Himself separately as "Jesus Christ whom Thou hast sent," demonstrating that both the Father and the Son are each rightly called God.

context: in verse number one, as Jesus is praying, He says, Father, the hour has come. Glorify Thy Son.
point_summary: In Jesus's high-priestly prayer, the Father is addressed as God and the Son requests mutual glorification, establishing that both the Father and the Son are distinctly yet equally God.

context: And John chapter number one, a portion of Scripture that I quoted a part of it when we looked at first John 5 and verse number 7, it said the Word and how
point_summary: John 1:1 is cited to establish that the Word—identified as Jesus Christ—is distinct from the Father ("with God") yet fully divine ("was God"), confirming Christ's eternal deity rather than a created nature.

context: in the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
point_summary: John 1:1 is cited to establish that Jesus Christ, identified as "the Word," is explicitly called God and existed before creation, directly refuting the claim that He is a created being.

context: The same was in the beginning with God.
point_summary: John 1:2's affirmation that the Word was in the beginning with God establishes Christ's eternality and deity, countering the claim that He is a created being.

context: All things were made by Him, and without Him was not anything made that was made.
point_summary: John 1:3's declaration that all things were made by the Word establishes that Christ is not a created being but is himself the Creator God.

context: and the Word was made flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.
point_summary: John 1:14's identification of "the Word" made flesh connects directly to John 1:1's declaration that "the Word was God," confirming Jesus Christ's full deity against those who deny it.

context: my sheep hear my voice and I know them and they follow me
point_summary: Christ's holding of the seven stars in his right hand (Rev. 2:1) is interpreted through John 10:27–29 as a picture of the eternal security and protection God provides his people.

context: I give unto them eternal. and they shall never perish, neither shall any man pluck them
point_summary: Christ's holding of the seven stars in his right hand (Rev. 2:1) is interpreted through John 10:28 as a picture of the eternal security and protection believers have in God's hand.

context: My father which gave them me is greater than all, and no man is able to pluck them out of my father's hand
point_summary: Christ's holding of the seven stars in his right hand (Rev. 2:1) is interpreted through John 10:29 as a picture of the believer's security and protection in the hand of God.

context: I and my father are one
point_summary: Christ's declaration "I and my Father are one" in John 10:30 grounds the believer's security, showing that the same hand holding the seven stars is the hand from which no one can pluck God's sheep.

context: we share with somebody that they're condemned already. That's what the Bible teaches us in John 3.
point_summary: John 3's declaration that the unbeliever "is condemned already" illustrates that no human effort is required to face judgment — condemnation is the default human condition apart from the revealed plan of redemption.

context: It's interesting in John chapter number one,
point_summary: John 1:14 is cited to ground the call to "love mercy" in the incarnation of Christ, whose coming in flesh displayed the glory of the Father full of grace.

context: and the word was made flesh and dwelt among us. We beheld His glory. The glory is of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.
point_summary: The incarnation of Christ — the Word made flesh, full of grace and truth — is presented as the supreme demonstration of God's merciful kindness toward humanity.

context: for the law was given by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Grace and truth coming by Jesus Christ in John 1:17 is presented as the expression of God's merciful kindness incarnate, connecting the New Testament fulfillment to Micah's call to love mercy.

context: it was there in John 13 that He washed the feet of the disciples.
point_summary: Jesus washing the disciples' feet in John 13 is presented as the defining historical illustration of the Philippians 2 call to Christlike humility—serving rather than being served.

context: He gave them, in John chapter number 13
point_summary: Christ's deliberate movement toward the cross—born to die, purposeful in servanthood, and personally caring for individuals—sets the context for His farewell discourse beginning in John 14.

context: Jesus begins this way in John 14. In verse number one, He says, Let not your heart be troubled.
point_summary: Amid the disciples' coming grief over Christ's departure, Jesus commands an untroubled heart by grounding their confidence in belief in Him—the same faith they already placed in God.

context: Let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God? Believe also in Me.
point_summary: Christ's command "Let not your heart be troubled" is presented as the direct pastoral response to the disciples' distress over coming events, grounding the promise of His return in the certainty of belief in God and in Him.

context: In My Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.
point_summary: Christ's promise of "many mansions" in the Father's house is presented as one of Scripture's most enduring sources of hope and assurance for believers across the centuries.

context: And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto Myself, that where I am, there ye may be also.
point_summary: Christ's use of "I will come again" — not "I might" or "I may" — is presented as the grammatical ground for absolute certainty in His promise of return.

context: John, who by the way, heard the words of Jesus spoken in John 14 that we referenced a moment ago. John was literally there and he heard Jesus say, let not your heart be troubled. He heard Jesus say, I'm going to prepare a place for you. He heard Jesus say, I will come again and receive you unto myself that where I am there you may be also.
point_summary: The promise of Christ's return in John 14—"I will come again and receive you unto myself"—is presented as divine truth meant to shape daily behavior, not merely inform doctrine.

context: In the gospel of John, Jesus mentions how that He was sent and that He now sends us
point_summary: Christ's post-resurrection commissioning in John 20:21—"as the Father sent Me, I send you"—grounds the church's evangelistic mandate as urgent and personal, to be fulfilled before rapture or death.

reference_text: He came to his own, but his own received him not
context: He came to his own, but his own received him not, and they rejected Messiah
point_summary: Israel's rejection of Messiah—"He came to his own, but his own received him not"—is cited to explain why God shifted focus to the church age, with Israel's restoration deferred until after the rapture.

context: what did Jesus say in John 14, I will come again
point_summary: The angels' declaration in Acts 1 that "this same Jesus" shall return "in like manner" is presented as the fulfillment-in-promise of Christ's own words in John 14:3, reinforcing the disciples' mission-driving certainty of his return.

context: John 17 is a great passage of Scripture dealing with the prayer of the Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: as thou hast sent me into the world, even so have I also sent them into the world
point_summary: Christ's prayer in John 17:18 grounds the church's mission in His own sending, establishing that believers are dispatched into the world as His representatives—to be light, salt, and witnesses.

context: ...as He said there in John 17, the hour has come...
point_summary: Christ's declaration in John 17 that "the hour has come" is cited as evidence that He understood and embraced the redemptive purpose for which He entered the world.

context: Jesus said to Nicodemus in John chapter number three he must be born again
point_summary: New birth is the only escape from spiritual death's finality, since being "born twice" means dying only once, while those born only physically face both physical and eternal death.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life.
point_summary: Jesus's promise in John 14:6 and the surrounding passage—"I will come again"—is presented as the doctrinal anchor for the disciples' expectant witness in Acts, connecting the Upper Room pledge directly to the church's anticipation of Christ's return.

context: Let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in me.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:1—"let not your heart be troubled"—is presented as the foundational assurance the disciples received before the cross, anticipating his return to receive them unto himself.

context: In my Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:2 to prepare a place and return for his own is presented as the foundational pledge that grounds the believer's blessed hope in his second coming.

context: And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto myself.

context: Jesus said, no man cometh unto the father but by me.

context: I am the vine, ye are the branches, he that abideth in me and I in him, the same bringeth forth. What does it say? Much fruit, for without me ye can do nothing.

context: If ye abide in me and my words abide in you, ye shall ask what ye will and it shall be done unto you.

context: God is a spirit, according to John chapter number four, and they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth.

context: He said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.

context: Let me remind you, as we think about this finish line of what Jesus said in John 14 as He's speaking to His disciples, and we find that they were troubled at the message that they had received in chapter 13 with regard to Christ

context: In my Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you.

context: I go to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto myself that where I am, there ye may be also.

context: Notice what the Bible says in John chapter number 10.

context: My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me.

context: And I give unto them eternal life, and they shall never perish, neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand.
point_summary: John 10:28 is cited to establish the surety of the believer's inheritance—that eternal life is Christ's gift and no one can remove his sheep from his hand.

context: My Father, which gave them me, is greater than all; and no man is able to pluck them out of my Father's hand.

context: I and my Father are one.
point_summary: Christ's declaration "I and my Father are one" anchors the surety of the believer's inheritance in the unified, unbreakable grip of both the Son and the Father.

context: Remember, Jesus said in John 8, in verse number 58, when He's speaking to the religious leaders of the day,

context: He said that Abraham rejoiced to see his day.
point_summary: Jesus's claim "Before Abraham was, I am" is presented as a direct appropriation of the divine name revealed to Moses, which the religious leaders immediately recognized as a claim to deity warranting stoning for blasphemy.

context: And He said, Before Abraham was, I am.
point_summary: Jesus's claim "Before Abraham was, I am" is presented as a deliberate invocation of the divine name revealed to Moses, which the religious leaders immediately recognized as a claim to be the self-existent God.

context: In John 8, they were contending with Jesus, and they say in verse 53, art thou greater than our father Abraham?
point_summary: The religious leaders' dismissive question — "Art thou greater than our father Abraham?" — is cited to show that they had no idea they stood before one who, moments later, would declare "Before Abraham was, I am," establishing His eternal deity and the grounds for calling Him precious.

context: In just a little while, He would say, before Abraham was, I am!
point_summary: Christ's self-declaration "Before Abraham was, I am" (John 8:58) is cited as proof that Jesus is God, grounding the meaning of "precious" in His divine rank and office.

context: When he says the Word, we're speaking of the Lord Jesus Christ. That is a title, a name that is ascribed to Jesus Christ, John 1.

context: Go to John 17, if you will, John chapter number 17.
point_summary: John 17 is introduced to demonstrate that the three distinct persons of the Godhead are co-equal yet hold distinct roles, as seen in Jesus's words on the eve of the crucifixion.

context: And we find here in John chapter number 17, we come to this portion of Scripture, and Jesus, of course, would soon go to the cross of Calvary.
