point_summary: In John 17:1, Jesus addresses the Father as a distinct person, illustrating the co-equal yet role-differentiated relationship within the Trinity as the Son prepares to glorify the Father through the cross.

context: If you study throughout the book of John, beginning in the second chapter, you'll find reference to the hour. And He says in the second chapter, mine hour is not yet come, the miracle of the marriage there at Cana.
point_summary: The phrase "mine hour is not yet come" in John 2:4 is traced as the first of a recurring thread through John's Gospel, culminating in Jesus' declaration in John 17 that the hour of His redemptive purpose has arrived.

context: as thou hast given Him power over all flesh, that He should give eternal life to as many as thou hast given Him.

context: And this is life eternal, that they might know Thee the only true God.
point_summary: Eternal life is defined here as knowing the Father as the only true God, with Jesus praying to Him as such, while simultaneously revealing His own pre-creation glory shared with the Father.

context: I have glorified Thee on the earth. I have finished the work which thou gavest Me to do.

context: And now, O Father, glorify thou Me with thine own self. Notice what He says, with the glory which I had with Thee before the world was.

context: This here in John 17 is really the Lord's Prayer.

context: We find here that John 17 is giving us the record of the Lord's Prayer, and He's praying.

context: In John chapter 1, in verse number 1, we find that here in these passages we looked at, the Father is referred to and acknowledged as being God.

context: in the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.

context: In Him was life; and the life was the light of men

context: according to what John writes to us, and you read in chapters 15 and 16 as the Lord Jesus Christ is giving this truth to the disciples
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's role as divine teacher, promised by Christ in John 15–16, grounds the believer's ability to discern truth from error without dependence on any human instructor.

context: I have yet many things to say unto you, but you cannot bear them now.

context: And we find that as we come to the word of God, it's interesting as we think about the question that was asked this evening, and we notice in our text of scripture in John chapter number 10, beginning in verse number 22, the Bible says, and it was at Jerusalem, the feast of the dedication, and it was winter.
point_summary: The feast of Dedication setting in John 10:22 frames the Jews' direct challenge—"Tell us plainly"—as the occasion for Jesus to address his identity as the Christ, the promised Messianic anointed one.

context: And Jesus walked in the temple in Solomon's porch.
point_summary: The setting of Jesus walking in Solomon's Porch during the Feast of Dedication establishes the historical and physical context for the Jews' direct challenge to declare his identity plainly.

context: And notice in verse 24, that the Jews come, that says, then came the Jews round about him. And so he was often interacting with the people of the day. And we find that they ask him a question here in verse number 24. And notice what they say. How long dost thou make us to doubt? If thou be the Christ, tell us plainly.
point_summary: The Jews' demand that Jesus declare himself plainly as the Christ frames the entire sermon's inquiry into why Scripture sometimes appears to veil rather than state outright his deity.

context: Jesus answered them, I told you, and ye believed not. The works that I do in my Father's name, they bear witness of me.

context: But ye believe not, because ye are not of my sheep, as I said unto you.

context: My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me,
point_summary: Jesus's declaration that his sheep hear his voice, know him, follow him, and receive eternal life serves as his plain answer to the Jews' demand that he identify himself as the Christ.

context: and I give unto them eternal life, and they shall never perish, neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand.

context: My Father which gave them me is greater than all, and no man is able to pluck them out of my Father's hand.

context: I and my Father are one.

context: When the Jews say to Jesus, if thou be the Christ, tell us plainly.
point_summary: The Jews' demand that Jesus speak "plainly" — meaning without concealment — about whether He is the Christ frames the entire passage as a question about His identity as the promised, anointed Messiah.

context: and the question that was asked, and we begin to look at the truth of John chapter number 10 when they say, tell us plainly, and Jesus says, I have, I've told you, but you believe not.

context: but I know ye that ye have not the love of God in you.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I am come in my Father's name, and ye receive me not. If another shall come in his own name, him ye will receive.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: How can you believe which receive honor one of another and seek not the honor that cometh from God only?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Do not think I will accuse you to the Father. There is one that accuseth you, even Moses, in whom ye trust.
point_summary: Moses' failure to convince Israel indicts them, since his writings testified of Christ — meaning rejection of Jesus is rejection of the very Scriptures they claimed to trust.

context: For had ye believed Moses, you would have believed me. For he, Moses, wrote of me.
point_summary: Moses' writings testify of Christ, so rejecting Jesus while claiming to follow Moses is self-contradictory—unbelief in one entails unbelief in the other.

context: But if ye believe not his writings, how shall ye believe my words?
point_summary: Rejection of Moses' writings is presented as the root cause of unbelief in Jesus, since Moses himself wrote of Christ.

context: Jesus heard that they had cast him out; and when he had found him, he said unto him, Dost thou believe on the Son of God?
point_summary: Jesus's self-revelation to the man born blind—culminating in the man's confession and act of worship—is cited as scriptural evidence that Jesus receives worship, which belongs to God alone.

context: The woman saith unto him, I know that Messiah cometh, which is called Christ. When He is come, He will tell us all things.
point_summary: Jesus' plain declaration "I that speak unto thee am He" to the Samaritan woman is cited as direct evidence that Jesus explicitly claimed to be the Messiah.

context: Jesus saith unto her, I that speak unto thee am He.
point_summary: Jesus' plain declaration to the Samaritan woman — "I that speak unto thee am He" — is cited as direct, unambiguous evidence that Jesus explicitly claimed to be the Messiah.

context: Jesus talks about the fact that Abraham rejoiced
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: thou art not yet fifty years old and hast thou seen Abraham
point_summary: Jesus's claim "before Abraham was, I am" — using God's covenant name from Exodus — provoked a stoning attempt, demonstrating that his hearers understood him to be claiming full deity.

context: verily, verily, I say unto you, before Abraham was, I am
point_summary: Jesus's claim "before Abraham was, I am" is presented as an unmistakable divine self-identification, confirmed by the leaders' murderous response as proof they understood exactly what He was claiming.

context: then took they up stones to cast at Him, but Jesus hid Himself
point_summary: The crowd's attempt to stone Jesus after his "I Am" declaration confirms they understood his claim to deity — a plain assertion of divine identity, not a misunderstanding.

context: I and My Father are one.
point_summary: Jesus's claim "I and My Father are one" is shown to be an unambiguous assertion of deity, confirmed by His accusers' own charge of blasphemy for "making himself God."

context: Then the Jews took up stones again to stone Him.
point_summary: The Jews' response to "I and My Father are one" — taking up stones for blasphemy because Jesus, being a man, made Himself God — is presented as plain confirmation that His audience understood exactly what He was claiming.

context: Many good works have I showed you from My Father; for which of those works do ye stone me?
point_summary: The Jews' own accusation — "thou makest thyself God" — confirms that Jesus' claim "I and My Father are one" was understood by His opponents as an unambiguous assertion of deity.

context: For a good work we stone thee not; but for blasphemy; and because that thou, being a man, makest thyself God.
point_summary: The Jews' accusation of blasphemy in John 10:33—that Jesus, being a man, made himself God—is presented as their own plain acknowledgment of exactly what Jesus was claiming when he said "I and my Father are one."

context: Judas knew the place, according to verse number 2, he knew the place, why? For Jesus oft times resorted thither with His disciples.
point_summary: Judas's familiarity with the garden, cited from John 18:2, establishes the setting for Jesus's self-disclosure—"I am"—which the sermon treats as a divine claim requiring the italicized "he" to be recognized as an addition.

context: Notice in verse number 4, Jesus therefore knowing all things that should come upon Him, went forth and said unto them, Whom seek ye?
point_summary: Jesus's sovereign foreknowledge at His arrest—"knowing all things that should come upon Him"—is presented as evidence of His divine nature, not the vulnerability of a mere man.

context: As soon then as He had said unto them, I am, they went backward and fell to the ground.
point_summary: When Jesus strips the italicized "he" from "I am he" in John 18:6, the divine name alone causes his arresters to fall backward—demonstrating his identity with the "I AM" of Exodus 3.

context: the disciples were gathered together according to verse number 19, but Thomas was not there.
point_summary: Thomas's absence from the disciples' gathering in John 20:19 is noted as the circumstance that set up his subsequent doubt and, ultimately, his confession of Christ's deity.

context: I will not believe at the end of verse number 25, because they told him that Jesus had came to them.
point_summary: Thomas's absence from the first resurrection appearance and his subsequent refusal to believe without physical proof set the stage for Christ's gracious self-revelation eight days later.

context: it says in verse 26, and after eight days again his disciples were within, notice it says expressly, and Thomas with them.
point_summary: After Thomas missed the first resurrection appearance, Jesus returned eight days later — doors shut — specifically to reveal Himself to Thomas, demonstrating God's gracious willingness to manifest Himself to doubting disciples.

context: He told them in John 10, He said, they said to Him, tell us plainly, He said, I've told you.
point_summary: Jesus's gracious self-revelation to doubting Thomas—meeting his exact conditions for belief—illustrates the same willingness to make Himself plainly known that He expressed in John 10.

context: Thomas had said to them in verse number 25, the other disciples therefore said unto Him, we have seen the Lord, but He said unto them, except I shall see in His hands the print of the nails, and put my fingers into the print of the nails, and thrust my hand into His side, I will not believe.
point_summary: Thomas's demand for physical proof of the nail-prints and wounded side is met by Jesus's gracious, direct response, illustrating that God actively condescends to reveal Himself to doubting hearts.

context: And He says to Thomas, be not faithless, but believing.
point_summary: Jesus's silence in response to Thomas's confession "My Lord and my God" is presented as implicit confirmation of His deity, since any mere man or angel would have corrected such worship.

reference_text: Thomas answered and said unto Him, my Lord and my God
context: Notice the response of Thomas in verse number 28, and I think that he says it with great excitement and emotion. Thomas answered and said unto Him, my Lord and my God.
point_summary: Thomas's confession "My Lord and my God" stands unrebuked by Jesus, and that silence is treated as decisive evidence that Jesus accepted the title of God as rightfully his own.

reference_text: because thou hast seen me thou hast believed, blessed are they that have not seen and yet have believed
context: Jesus answered him and says, Thomas, in verse 29, because thou hast seen me thou hast believed, blessed are they that have not seen and yet have believed.
point_summary: Jesus's silence in response to Thomas's confession — offering no correction — is presented as implicit confirmation that the title "my Lord and my God" was rightly applied to Him.

context: When Thomas says, my Lord and my God, he was rightly recognizing what Jesus had revealed to him
point_summary: Thomas's uncontested confession of Jesus as "my Lord and my God" is treated as decisive evidence of Christ's deity, since Jesus's silence constitutes implicit affirmation rather than the correction any mere man would owe.

context: in many other signs truly did Jesus in the presence of His disciples, which are not written in this book
point_summary: John's stated purpose in recording his Gospel — that readers might believe Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God — is presented as the culminating declaration toward which all the signs and evidence in the book point.

context: but these are written. Why are these written? That you might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that believing you might have life through His name
point_summary: John 20:31 is cited as John's own stated purpose — that the Gospel's signs were written to produce belief that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, confirming that the entire book builds toward this revelation.

context: They said in John 10, tell us plainly. He says, I have. I told you, and you believe not.
point_summary: Jesus's plain declaration in John 10 that He had already told them who He is underscores that unbelief, not insufficient evidence, is the barrier to recognizing His deity.

context: My sheep hear My voice, and I know them, and they follow Me.
point_summary: Christ's promise that His sheep receive *eternal* (not temporary or probationary) life and cannot be plucked from His hand grounds the believer's security in divine keeping, not personal effort.

context: I give unto them eternal life. And they shall never perish. Neither shall any man pluck them out of My hand.
point_summary: Christ's gift of "eternal life" — not temporary or probationary — guarantees that His sheep shall never perish and cannot be plucked from His hand, establishing security rooted in divine power rather than human effort.

context: My Father which gave them Me is greater than all, and no man is able to pluck them out of My Father's hand.
point_summary: The Father's surpassing greatness guarantees that no one can remove believers from His hand, grounding eternal security not in human effort but in divine power.

context: I and My Father are one.
point_summary: Christ's declaration "I and My Father are one" grounds the security of eternal life in the unified omnipotence of both the Father and the Son, ensuring no believer can be plucked from their keeping hands.

context: My sheep, they hear My voice, and I know them, they follow Me.
point_summary: Christ's promise that His sheep receive eternal life — not temporary or probationary — grounds the assurance that believers are kept by God's power, not their own effort.

context: Jesus said to Nicodemus in John chapter number three, He comes to Jesus by night.
point_summary: Being born again—the spiritual birth Jesus declared necessary to Nicodemus—is identified as the second birth that exempts the believer from the second death.

context: except you be born again, you cannot see the kingdom of God
point_summary: Being born again—the new birth Jesus declared necessary in John 3:3—is identified as the salvation event that exempts believers from the second death described in Revelation 20.

context: Marvel not that I said unto thee, you must be born again
point_summary: Being born again—the new birth Jesus declared necessary in John 3:7—is identified as the salvation event that exempts believers from the second death described in Revelation 20.

context: Jesus says, let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in me.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:1–3 is cited as assurance that He will personally return to receive believers unto Himself, grounding the Christian's hope in His guaranteed, though undated, coming.

context: In my Father's house. house or many mansions, if it were not so I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:1–3 — that He has gone to prepare a place and will return to receive believers unto Himself — is presented as one of the great pledges anchoring the Christian's hope.

context: and if I go and prepare a place I will come again and receive you unto myself that where I am there you may be also.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:1–3 to prepare a place and return for believers is presented as one of the great pledges God—who is not slack concerning His promises—will certainly fulfill.

context: this evening, John chapter 21. You say, pastor, I thought we were in the book of Revelation.
point_summary: John 21:15–19 is introduced as a case study in recovering first love, set against the Ephesian church's failure to sustain it across twenty centuries of Christian history.

context: Remember what the Bible says there in John 21
point_summary: The Ephesus church's failure to maintain its first love raises the broader question of whether believers today genuinely love God with all their heart, as John 21 will illustrate.

context: And in John 21, beginning in verse number 15, notice what the Bible says, I'll read verse 15 down through verse number 19,
point_summary: The post-resurrection exchange in John 21:15, where Jesus asks Peter "Lovest thou me more than these?", is introduced as the biblical lens through which the sermon examines what it means to love God with all one's heart.

context: In verse 15, the Bible says, so when they had dined, Jesus saith to Simon Peter, Simon, son of Jonas, lovest thou me more than these? He saith unto him, yea, Lord, thou knowest that I love thee. He saith unto him, feed my lambs.
point_summary: John 21:15–19 is introduced as the sermon's central text to frame the theme of loving God with all one's heart, using Christ's threefold question to Peter as the defining illustration.

context: He saith to him again the second time, Simon, son of Jonas, lovest thou me? He saith unto him, yea, Lord, thou knowest that I love thee. He saith unto him, feed my sheep.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He saith unto him, verse 17, the third time, Simon, son of Jonas, lovest thou me? Peter was grieved. It means to have a heavy, sorrowful, broken heart. He was grieved because he said unto him the third time, lovest thou me? And he said unto him, Lord, thou knowest all things.
point_summary: Peter's grief at the third questioning reveals that God's omniscience—"thou knowest all things"—renders human profession of love accountable to divine knowledge rather than mere self-declaration.

context: Jesus saith unto him, feed my sheep.
point_summary: Peter's grief at the third questioning exposes the convicting reality that God knows all things, making any profession of love either genuine or hollow before an omniscient Christ.

context: Verily, verily, I say unto thee, when thou wast young, thou girdest thyself and walkest whither thou wouldest, but when thou shalt be old, thou shalt stretch forth thy hands and another shall gird thee and carry thee whither thou wouldest not.
point_summary: Loving God wholeheartedly carries a cost, as Christ's words to Peter in verse 18 foretell that genuine discipleship would ultimately lead him where he would not choose to go—to a death that glorifies God.

context: This spake he, signifying by what death he should glorify God. And who had spoken this, he saith unto him, follow me.
point_summary: Following Jesus is framed as the call that bookends Peter's entire journey—from the first miraculous catch to the prophecy of his martyrdom—showing that discipleship unto death is the shape of loving God.

context: Lord, we know that you are the Christ. You have the words of eternal life
point_summary: Peter's bold confession in John 6:68 — that Christ alone holds the words of eternal life and there is nowhere else to go — establishes the depth of commitment from which his subsequent denial makes his restoration all the more striking.

context: Go back to the beginning of John 21. It might be on the same page for you. But we find that according to the opening statements of John chapter 21.
point_summary: After the resurrection appearances of John 20, Jesus deliberately showed himself again to a specific group of seven disciples at the Sea of Tiberias, establishing that this restoration encounter with Peter was intentionally personal.

context: Now remember in John 20, that's the truth of the resurrection, right? That's when they saw the resurrected Christ.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And the Bible says, after these things, Jesus showed himself again to the disciples at the Sea of Tiberias.
point_summary: After the resurrection appearances of John 20, Jesus' renewed encounter with the disciples at the Sea of Tiberias sets the stage for a restoration that moves from the group ("them") to the individual.

context: Notice verse two, there were together Simon Peter and Thomas called Didymus, and Nathanael of Cana in Galilee, and the two sons of Zebedee, and two other of his disciples.
point_summary: The personal nature of Christ's post-resurrection appearance is established by the specific naming of seven disciples in John 21:2, setting the stage for the individual, face-to-face restoration of Peter that follows.

context: The Bible tells us in verse number three, that Simon, notice it says, Simon Peter said unto them, I go a fishing.
point_summary: Peter's return to fishing, joined by six other disciples, sets the scene for a deeply personal encounter—Jesus addresses not the group collectively but moves toward individual restoration.

context: It was morning according to verse number four He stood on the shore
point_summary: Jesus appearing personally on the shore—unrecognized at first—sets up the sermon's point that His subsequent restoration of Peter was a deliberate, individual encounter, not a general address to the group.

context: notice in verse number five when Jesus shows up.
point_summary: Jesus addressing the disciples collectively as "them" in verse 5 establishes that this seaside encounter was a personal, direct interaction between Christ and His disciples as a group.

context: Jesus saith to Simon Peter according to verse number 15
point_summary: After addressing the group collectively, Jesus singles out Simon Peter by name, illustrating that God's love, though global, is also deeply personal and individual.

context: According to verse number 16 Jesus saith to Peter again
point_summary: Christ's repeated, personal address to Peter — "again" in verse 16 — illustrates that God's love is not merely global but individually directed toward each person.

context: according to verse number 17 He Jesus saith to him Peter the third time
point_summary: Christ's threefold, personal address to Peter by name illustrates that God's love is not merely global but individually directed toward each person.

context: And I'm thankful here as we have this account in John 21 That Jesus came to Peter personally
point_summary: Christ's threefold personal address to Simon Peter in John 21:15–17 illustrates that God deals with individual believers, not merely congregations, calling each to examine whether they have left their first love.

context: the interaction here in John chapter 21, though There were others that were gathered
point_summary: Restoration of an individual believer is the entry point for revival, illustrated by Christ's purposeful, personal examination of Peter in John 21:15.

context: I look at Peter here and Jesus says, Peter, do you love me? In verse 15, lovest thou me more than these, and he saith unto him, yea, Lord, thou knowest
point_summary: Christ's threefold question to Peter — "Lovest thou me more than these?" — is presented as a purposeful call to self-examination, paralleling the church of Ephesus being confronted with having left its first love.

context: And Jesus comes to Peter. He says Peter do you love me? Three times.
point_summary: Restoration after failure is the lens through which John 21:15 is read here: Christ's threefold question to Peter mirrors the threefold denial, framing the exchange as both examination and deliberate restoration of a broken disciple.

context: I think tonight that as I look at Peter in John chapter 21 it must have been so encouraging for the Lord to have this conversation with him. Yes it was difficult. He says he was grieved in verse 17.
point_summary: Though Peter was grieved when Jesus asked him a third time, the sermon frames that grief as part of a purposeful, restorative process that later became a source of peace and joy.

context: Notice in verse 15, he says, feed my lambs
point_summary: Genuine love for Christ, demonstrated by Peter's commission in verse 15 to "feed my lambs," produces willing obedience to whatever the Lord calls his people to do.

context: verse number 16 and 17, he says, feed my sheep
point_summary: Genuine love for Christ, demonstrated in Peter's repeated commission to "feed my sheep," produces willing obedience to whatever the Lord calls his people to do.

context: verse number 16 and 17, he says, feed my sheep
point_summary: Genuine love for Christ, expressed in Peter's restoration, produces willing obedience to his commands—feeding his sheep and following him—because love, not duty, motivates the heart.

context: And then we find in verse 19, he says, follow me
point_summary: Genuine love for Christ transforms obedience into delight, so that Christ's command "Follow me" in verse 19 is not burdensome but gladly embraced by the heart he possesses.

context: John 4 and v. 24 when Jesus is meeting with the woman of the well there when He says that He must needs go through Samaria, He's having that conversation. And in John 4 and v. 24, the Bible says that God is a Spirit. They that worship Him must worship Him in spirit and in truth.
point_summary: John 4:24's declaration that "God is a Spirit" is cited to establish why Christ as "the image of the invisible God" (Col. 1:15) is the visible manifestation of an otherwise unseen, incorporeal God.

context: See, we mentioned already that John 4 and verse 24, God is a spirit.
point_summary: John 4:24's declaration that God is a Spirit grounds the significance of Christ as the "image of the invisible God," since an invisible, spiritual God required incarnate manifestation to be made known and visible.

context: The Bible says in verse number 14, and the Word was made flesh.
point_summary: Christ's incarnation as the Word made flesh is presented as the moment God became visible and knowable to humanity, directly illustrating what Paul means by "image of the invisible God" in Colossians 1:15.

context: Now we know in the beginning of this chapter that the Bible tells us that in the beginning was the Word and the Word was with God and the Word was God.
point_summary: John 1:1's identification of the Word as God grounds the claim that at the incarnation Jesus did not become God but made the invisible God visible as the one who eternally is God.

context: And it says here, and we beheld His glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.
point_summary: The incarnation of the Word made flesh is presented as the moment Christ made the invisible God visible and beheld in glory, directly illuminating what it means for Jesus to be the image of the invisible God in Colossians 1.

context: Look at verse number 18. No man hath seen God at any time. The only begotten Son, which is in the bosom of the Father, He hath declared Him.
point_summary: Because no one has seen God directly, the incarnate Son — uniquely in the Father's bosom — declared and made the invisible God visible and knowable.

context: In John chapter number 17, we find that the Bible makes this truth known to us with regard to the day in which Jesus, He says the hour is now come.
point_summary: John 17:5 corroborates Colossians 1's twin claims about Christ — his eternality and his shared divine glory with the Father existing before the world was created.

context: Notice in verse number five, Jesus says this, and now, O Father, glorify Thou me with Thine own self, notice this, with the glory which I had with Thee before the world was.
point_summary: Christ's pre-incarnate glory shared with the Father "before the world was" is cited as evidence of His eternality, paralleling Colossians 1:17's declaration that He existed before all things.

context: and then we see the manifestation, look in verse number six of John 17, the Bible says this, Jesus said, I have manifested Thy name unto the men which Thou gavest me out of the world. Thine they were, and Thou gavest them me, and they have kept Thy word.
point_summary: Christ's self-declaration — "I have manifested Thy name" — is cited to show that Jesus actively made the invisible God known during His earthly ministry, paralleling Colossians 1:15's description of Him as the image of the invisible God.

context: we find the way in which He is described in John 1, we looked at a few verses, but in verse number 16,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: in the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God
point_summary: John 1:1 is presented alongside Colossians 1 as complementary divine revelation that supplies further detail about Christ's specific role as Creator, without contradicting Genesis 1.

context: The same was in the beginning with God
point_summary: John 1:2 is cited alongside Colossians 1 to show that both passages complement rather than contradict Genesis 1, providing further revealed detail about Christ's role as Creator of all things.

context: All things were made by Him
point_summary: John 1:3 is presented alongside Colossians 1:16–17 as complementary revelation that expands Genesis 1 by identifying Jesus Christ specifically as the Creator through whom all things were made.

context: without Him was not anything made that was made. It's obvious in John 1
point_summary: John 1:1–3 is presented as divine revelation that complements Genesis 1 by supplying further detail: the Word who was God is identified as Jesus Christ, the Creator of all things.

context: John the Baptist said in John 3 and verse 30, He must increase, I must decrease.
point_summary: John 3:30 is cited as the believer's fitting response to Christ's preeminence—self-decrease and Christ's increase as the logical, lived-out conclusion of understanding who Jesus truly is.

context: When we understand that without Him we can do nothing...
point_summary: Christ's lordship demands total surrender because, as John 15:5 underscores, without Him we can do nothing—making self's claim to the throne of the heart the central battle of the Christian life.

context: my sheep hear my voice and I know them
point_summary: Eternal security is affirmed from John 10:27 to clarify that Colossians 1:23's call to remain grounded addresses effectiveness, not the loss of salvation for true believers.

context: I give unto them eternal life and they shall never perish.
point_summary: Eternal security is affirmed from John 10:28 to clarify that Colossians 1:23's call to remain grounded warns against lost effectiveness, not lost salvation.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life, no man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Knowing that Jesus alone is the way, the truth, and the life is presented as essential doctrinal grounding whose loss to false teaching cripples a believer's effectiveness in evangelism.

context: One of the most known verses in all of the Bible, John 3.16, for God so loved the world.
point_summary: John 3:16 is introduced as Scripture's declaration and demonstration of God's love, distinguished from popular misuse of "God is love" by insisting that biblical vocabulary must be understood by biblical definitions.

context: For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in Him should not perish, but have everlasting life.
point_summary: John 3:16 is introduced as the scriptural declaration of God's love, which the sermon then distinguishes from popular misreadings by defining that love as protective rather than permissive.

context: John heard the Lord Jesus Christ speak those words in John 15 and how we need to abide in him.
point_summary: Abiding in Christ, the posture of the branch wholly dependent on the vine, is the foundation John calls believers to maintain in light of the Lord's imminent return.

context: Turn back to the book of John, if you will, and I want you to go back to John chapter number 14.
point_summary: John 14:1–3 is cited as Jesus' own direct promise of his return, grounding the certainty of the Second Coming in the eyewitness testimony John had already established in his epistle's opening verses.

context: let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in me.
point_summary: John's eyewitness presence at the Upper Room discourse grounds his apostolic authority to declare Christ's promised return as a settled, comforting fact rather than speculation.

context: In my Father's house are many mansions, if it were not so, I would have told you, I go to prepare a place for you.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:2—"I go to prepare a place for you"—is presented as words John personally heard, giving his eyewitness testimony in 1 John its grounding in firsthand comfort and confidence about the Lord's return.

context: if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto myself, that where I am, there ye may be also.
point_summary: Christ's personal promise—"I will come again and receive you unto myself"—is presented as the eyewitness foundation underlying John's confident declaration of the Lord's return in 1 John 2–3.

context: I give you a new commandment that you love, not as yourself, don't love your neighbors yourself, but love as I have loved, that you love also one another
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: by this truth, by this action, by this love, all men will know that you're my disciples.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: let not your heart be troubled
point_summary: Christ's promise "I will come again" in John 14:1–3 is presented as the source of comfort and confidence that John himself had heard firsthand and later drew upon when writing to his readers about the Lord's appearing.

context: I will come again
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:3 — "I will come again" — is presented as the direct source of the comfort and confidence John carried into his later writing about the Lord's appearing.

context: John was there, for John, as it says in John 21 and verse number 22
point_summary: John's presence at the exchange in John 21:22—where Jesus redirected Peter's curiosity about John's future—grounds John's firsthand witness to Christ's promise of return, lending personal weight to his later assurance in 1 John 2:28.

context: Peter seeing him, saith to Jesus, Lord, and what shall this man do?
point_summary: John 21:21 provides the narrative context in which John himself heard Jesus say "till I come," words that would have personally shaped John's confidence in Christ's return as he later wrote 1 John.

context: then Peter, turning about, seeth the disciple whom Jesus loved following
point_summary: John 21:20 identifies "the disciple whom Jesus loved" as John himself, establishing that John personally heard Jesus promise in verse 22 that He would come again.

context: Jesus saith unto him, if I will that he tarry, notice these words, till I come, what is that to thee? Follow thou me.
point_summary: John's firsthand memory of Jesus saying "till I come" (John 21:22) grounds his confident expectation in 1 John 2:28 in a direct, personal encounter with Christ's own promise to return.

context: Jesus says, what does it matter to you? You follow me.
point_summary: Christ's words "till I come" in John 21:22, heard personally by John, are presented as formative background to John's later writings about the Lord's return.

context: let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in me.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:1–3 — "I will come again" — is presented as the living hope John personally carried, shaping his understanding of Jesus's words in John 21 about tarrying "till I come."

context: In my Father's house are many mansions, if it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:2 — that He goes to prepare a place and will come again — is presented as a word John personally heard and carried, giving weight to his later writings about the Lord's return.

context: I will come again and receive you unto myself, that where I am, there you may be also.
point_summary: Christ's personal promise in John 14:3 — "I will come again" — is presented as the foundational word John himself heard, giving weight to every subsequent reference to the Lord's return.

context: if I will that he tarry till I come.
point_summary: Christ's words to Peter in John 21:22 are read here not only as a rebuke of distraction but as a promise John himself heard and carried — that Jesus would come again.

context: John hears the Lord Jesus Christ in John 14 give that great discourse and says, I will come again.
point_summary: John's promise "I will come again" (John 14) is cited as one of three firsthand witnesses John received that ground his unshakeable certainty of Christ's return.

context: Remember, as we mentioned there in John chapter 21, how was it that John referred to himself, the disciple whom the Lord loveth?
point_summary: John's self-identification in John 21 as "the disciple whom the Lord loveth" is invoked to anchor the believer's identity as one loved by God, paralleling the sonship declared in 1 John 3:2.

context: your father Abraham rejoiced to see my day, and he saw it and was glad
point_summary: Abraham's rejoicing to see Christ's day, cited alongside Jesus's "I am" declaration, establishes that Abraham himself recognized the Messiah's pre-existent glory across the ages.

context: Verily, verily, I say unto you, before Abraham was, I am
point_summary: Jesus's claim "before Abraham was, I am" is cited to demonstrate his eternal pre-existence, surpassing even the revered patriarch Abraham whom the Jews invoked against him.

context: John 4 talks about the truth that they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth.
point_summary: Genuine worship must be rendered on God's terms—in spirit and in truth—not defined by human preference, as John 4 declares.

reference_text: worship you as we should, in spirit and in truth
context: God, help us to worship you as we should, in spirit and in truth.
point_summary: Worship offered to God must be rendered in spirit and in truth, as the congregation is called to emulate Abraham's altar-building devotion in their own lives.

context: It's in John 14 and verse 15 that the Bible says, if you love Me, keep My commandments.
point_summary: Obedience to God's commandments flows from love, and keeping them is not burdensome because they come from a loving God given for our good.

context: he said that my joy might remain in you and that your joy might be full as I read that verse from John 15
point_summary: Joy rooted in Jesus never changes, unlike circumstances or possessions, so that his joy remaining in believers produces fullness—not fleeting happiness found in the world.

reference_text: before the feeding of the 5,000, He says that Jesus asked this question to prove Him. For He Himself already knew what He would do.
context: In fact, before the feeding of the 5,000, He says that Jesus asked this question to prove Him. For He Himself already knew what He would do.
point_summary: God's testing of His people — whether in the wilderness or before the feeding of the five thousand — is a proving of faith and obedience, revealing whether they truly believe He will do what He has said.

context: notice what Jesus says to His disciples in John 15. Beginning in verse number 18, notice what He says. If the world hates you, ye know that it hated Me before it hated you.
point_summary: World-hatred of believers is not incidental but expected, as Jesus explicitly prepared His disciples—and by extension all believers—for the same persecution He Himself endured.

context: If ye were of the world, the world would love His own. But because you're not of the world, but I have chosen you out of the world, therefore the world hateth you.
point_summary: Being chosen out of the world by Christ is the very reason believers face the world's hatred, a truth Jesus spoke to prepare His disciples—and preserved to prepare us.

context: Remember the word that I said unto you, the servant is not greater than his Lord. If they have persecuted Me, they will also persecute you.
point_summary: Persecution of Christ's followers is inevitable because the servant shares the same treatment as the Lord—a truth Jesus spoke to prepare His disciples and that still prepares believers today.

context: If they have kept My saying, they will keep yours also.
point_summary: Persecution of Christ's followers by the world is not unexpected but forewarned, since Jesus explicitly told His disciples that servants are not greater than their Lord and would face the same hatred He received.

context: If the world hates you, what do you say? You know that it hated Me before it hated you.
point_summary: Hatred of believers by the world is not incidental but expected, because Christ himself was hated first—a truth Jesus spoke to prepare his disciples, and which Scripture preserves to prepare us.

context: Notice in chapter 16 in verse number one, these things have I spoken unto you that you should not be offended.
point_summary: Jesus forewarns His disciples of coming persecution and offense so that, when it arrives, they will be spiritually prepared rather than caught off guard.

context: They shall put you out of the synagogues, yea, the time cometh that whosoever killeth you will think that he doeth God's service,
point_summary: Persecution of believers—even to the point of death by those who believe they serve God—is not a surprise but a forewarning Jesus gave so His followers would not be offended when it comes.

context: and these things will they do unto you. Why? Because they have not known the Father nor Me.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: But these things have I told you that when the time shall come,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: but now I go on My way to Him that sent Me, and none of you asketh Me whither goest now
point_summary: Jesus' forewarning that His disciples would face hatred, expulsion, and even death was spoken so that when those things came, they would not be caught off guard or fall away.

context: but because I have said these things unto you, sorrow hath filled your heart

context: The time's gonna come, they're gonna put you out of the synagogue, the one that kills you is gonna think that he does God's service

context: But notice what He says in verse number seven. Nevertheless, I tell you the truth, is that it is expedient for you that I go away

context: John heard Jesus speak those words that we referenced earlier in John chapter number 15.

context: Jesus is washing the feet of the disciples

context: But notice this truth that is given to us in John 13 and verse 34. point_summary: Jesus's command in John 13:34 to love one another as He loved them is framed as a new commandment rooted in His perfect love, which John himself heard and later reflected in his own writing.

context: when Jesus said, and John is there, I give you this new commandment. And he says in the text of Scripture, that by this, by your love one for another, will all men know that you're my disciples.
point_summary: John's command in John 13:34 — that love among believers would identify them as Christ's disciples — is presented as words John himself heard, grounding his later apostolic instruction on brotherly love in firsthand experience.

context: we see the command of the Lord Jesus Christ, all men are going to know you're my disciples as they see your love one for another.

context: According to John chapter number one, he is light. In him is no darkness at all.

context: the Lord used that in John chapter number 15 in speaking

context: ...as he's there with Jesus in John chapter number 13, in verse number 34...
point_summary: The newness of Christ's commandment in John 13:34 is that the standard shifts from loving one another as yourself to loving one another as Christ has loved you.

context: The Bible tells us that God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son that whosoever believeth in Him should not perish, but have everlasting life.
point_summary: God's agape love, displayed in Christ's earthly ministry and epitomized in John 3:16's self-giving sacrifice, set the new standard by which believers are now commanded to love one another.

context: A new commandment I give you, that you love one another.
point_summary: The newness of Christ's command in John 13:34 lies not in the duty to love but in its standard — "as I have loved you" — a measure of love humanity had never before witnessed or experienced.

context: John 13 and verse number 34 a few times, where the Bible says, as we've mentioned here in this point, that Jesus said, a new commandment I give unto you, that you love one another as I have loved you, that ye also love one another.
point_summary: The newness of Christ's command in John 13:34 lies not in the duty to love but in its standard—"as I have loved you"—which reframes the familiar commandment around Christ's own self-giving love.

context: But look over in chapter number 15, if you will, of the gospel of John. John chapter number 15. Look down at verse number 12. In John 15 and verse number 12, again, John, the apostle, would have been here and would have been hearing, receiving these words from Jesus Christ on the eve of Him going to the cross of Calvary. The greatest demonstration of the love of God Almighty was when He went to that cruel cross for your sin and for my sin. And we find on the eve of that that Jesus is telling His disciples, love as I have loved. In John 15 and verse number 12, this is my commandment, that ye love one another as I have loved you.
point_summary: Christ's command to love one another, spoken on the eve of the cross, carries the standard of His own self-giving love as its measure and model.

context: Look at verse number 17. These things I command you, that ye love one another.
point_summary: Christ's command to love one another, repeated in John 15:17, is framed as a divine imperative reinforcing the same standard introduced in John 13:34—love modeled after Christ's own self-giving love.

context: I want you to consider in John 13, we looked already at the end of the chapter when He's speaking to His disciples.
point_summary: John 13:1 is presented as the premier illustration of love-one-another truth, with the phrase describing Christ's awareness of His departing hour highlighted as theologically significant to understanding the love He displayed.

context: now before the feast of the Passover, when Jesus knew that His hour was come, that He should depart out of this world unto the Father
point_summary: Jesus's love for His own—illustrated in His enduring love for Peter despite denial—is captured in the phrase "He loved them unto the end," showing that His love persisted through failure and toward the cross.

context: Peter, do you love me?
point_summary: Christ's love for Peter—illustrated by the post-denial question "Do you love me?"—is offered as evidence that Jesus loved His own "unto the end," even through betrayal and failure.

reference_text: love one another as I loved you

context: Jesus says, a new commandment I give unto you, that you love one another, as I have loved you, that you also love one another.
point_summary: Mutual love among believers is the visible mark by which the watching world recognizes true disciples of Christ, making church division and animosity a direct failure of that witness.

context: By this shall all men know that ye are my disciples, if ye have love one to another.
point_summary: Mutual love among believers is the distinguishing mark by which all men recognize Christ's disciples, making the church's unity or division a visible testimony—or counter-testimony—to the watching world.

context: And we find that the Lord Jesus Christ said this in John 17. He said a lot of things, but one of the truths that we get as we understand the portion of Scriptures he's praying there is he reveals to us that we're in the world, but we're not of the world.

context: Jesus said this in John 13, if you turn back there, hold your spot here in 1 John

context: Jesus said, a new commandment I give unto you, that you love one another. And then he says this, as I have loved you, that you also love one another.
point_summary: Christ's command in John 13:34 to love one another "as I have loved you" is grounded in His own love demonstrated to the end, making mutual love among believers an expected mark of discipleship.

context: now before the feast of the Passover, when Jesus knew that His hour was come, that He should depart out of this world unto the Father, having loved His own which were in the world, notice this phrase, He loved them unto the end.

context: Notice what Jesus said. John, again, obviously would have heard these words, and he was used to pen them in John chapter number 15 and verses 18 and 19.
point_summary: Hatred from the world toward believers is expected, because Christ himself was hated first, and the world's rejection of his followers flows from their being chosen out of the world.

context: Notice what Jesus said. John, again, obviously would have heard these words, and he was used to pen them in John chapter number 15 and verses 18 and 19.
point_summary: Believers should expect the world's hatred because Christ has chosen them out of the world, making them no longer belong to it.

context: if the world hates you, you know that it hated me before it hated you
point_summary: Hatred from the world toward believers is to be expected, because the world's rejection of Christians ultimately stems from its prior rejection of Christ himself.

context: It's amazing to me that Jesus said this. We looked at it in John chapter number 13 as we looked at that text just a moment ago, but remember in verse number 35, Jesus said, by this shall all men know that you're my disciples, what? For your love one for another.
point_summary: Loving one another functions as a dual witness: outward-facing, it marks believers as Christ's disciples before the watching world, while inwardly it assures the believer of genuine conversion.

context: Most of us can quote John 3.16, for God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life.
point_summary: God's self-giving love in laying down His life for us (1 John 3:16) is presented as the defining example and standard that should shape how believers love one another.

context: Jesus says, listen, I give you a new commandment, that you love one another as I have loved you
point_summary: Jesus's command in John 13:34 to love one another as he loved us grounds the standard of brotherly love in the self-sacrificing agape love Christ himself demonstrated by laying down his life.

context: Jesus says, listen, I give you a new commandment, that you love one another as I have loved you
point_summary: John 13:34–35 is cited as the dominical command underlying John's exposition of brotherly love, with Christ's self-sacrificing agape established as both the standard and the supreme demonstration that love must move beyond declaration into action.

context: he said, I lay down my life in John chapter number 10. He's the good shepherd and he lays down his life.
point_summary: Christ's self-identification as the Good Shepherd who lays down His life (John 10) is cited as the defining act proving that genuine love expresses itself in sacrificial action, not mere words.

context: when we couple it with John 13 that those without will know that we're His disciples because of our love one for another.

context: I want to draw your attention to what the Lord Jesus Christ says to these disciples in John 15
point_summary: Just as God revealed His plans to Abraham His friend, Christ's words in John 15:14–15 show that believers are called friends precisely because He has made known to them what servants do not know.

context: In chapter number 13, he washed the feet of the disciples
point_summary: John 13's account of Jesus washing the disciples' feet and announcing his departure serves as contextual backdrop to John 15, where Christ calls his disciples friends and shares with them privileged information, paralleling Abraham's role as God's friend in Genesis 18.

context: And we find here in verse number 14 of John 15, notice what Jesus says to his disciples
point_summary: Just as God shared privileged information with Abraham His friend, Christ's words in John 15:14 show that believers are called friends precisely because He has made known to them what servants do not know.

context: A new commandment I give unto you, that ye love one another as I have loved you, that ye also love one another.
point_summary: Christ's command that believers love one another as he loved them is cited as the defining standard and visible mark of discipleship that corporate gathering is meant to provoke and sustain.

reference_text: by this, by our love one for another, all men will know that we're his disciples
context: by this, by our love one for another, all men will know that we're his disciples

context: let not your heart be troubled. He says, you believe in God, believe also in me.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:1–3 to receive believers unto himself is identified as the future gathering referenced in 2 Thessalonians 2:1, distinct from the present gathering of saints.

context: In my Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you.

context: I go to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again, notice where he says, and receive you unto myself.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:3 to "come again and receive you unto myself" is identified as the very gathering referenced in 2 Thessalonians 2:1, distinguishing that future reception from the present gathering of saints.

reference_text: he whom thou lovest is sick
context: They sent to Jesus to say he whom thou lovest is sick.
point_summary: When crisis strikes, the instinct of those who love Jesus is to bring their need directly to him, as Mary and Martha did by sending word that the one he loved was sick.

reference_text: if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died

context: Jesus would say Lazarus come forth.
point_summary: Lazarus had to die and remain four days in the tomb so that Christ's command "Lazarus, come forth" would produce a belief in resurrection that mere healing could never have generated.

context: For God so loved the world But he gave his only begotten Son that whosoever believeth in him should not perish but have everlasting life

context: The Bible says in verse number 35 the father loveth the Son and hath given all things into his hand

context: He that believeth on the Son hath everlasting life And he that believeth not the Son shall not see life, but the wrath of God abideth on him

context: ...as Thou hast sent Me into the world, even so have I also sent them into the world.

context: The wrath of God abideth upon you

context: What did Jesus say in John 13? He said that we're to love as Christ loved.

context: He came to His own, and His own received Him not
point_summary: Christ's rejection by His own people (John 1:11) illustrates that not everyone who hears the gospel will believe, grounding the reality that the saved bear witness to a message some will refuse.

context: but as many as received Him. To them gave He power to become the sons of God, even them that believe on His name

context: I said this back in December as I considered some of the truth from John 1, verses 11 and 12 about those that received Him not and those that did receive Him
point_summary: The tragedy of John 1:11—that people received Him not—is applied to the lost in the surrounding community who remain unaware they even have a choice to receive or reject Christ.

context: I said this back in December as I considered some of the truth from John 1, verses 11 and 12 about those that received Him not and those that did receive Him
point_summary: Receiving Christ, as contrasted with rejecting Him in John 1:11–12, frames the urgent evangelistic point that many in the community are unaware they even have a choice to receive Him.

context: Jesus would make reference to this Old Testament account when speaking to Nicodemus in John chapter number 3.

context: In John chapter number 3 and verse number 14, we find that Jesus would say this to Nicodemus. With regard to this very account that we're looking at here in Numbers chapter 21, we find that God, the Lord Jesus Christ, says to Nicodemus in verse number 14 of John 3, and as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, even so must the Son of Man be lifted up.
point_summary: Jesus's own words to Nicodemus—"as Moses lifted up the serpent, even so must the Son of Man be lifted up"—are presented as identifying the bronze serpent episode as a picture of Christ's substitutionary death on the cross.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.

context: without me you can do nothing
point_summary: Apart from human effort or ability, every good thing received traces back to God alone, as Christ's own words confirm that without Him, nothing can be accomplished.

context: Jesus Christ Himself said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: The exclusivity of salvation through Christ alone—"no man cometh unto the Father but by me"—grounds the singular way of conversion that Phoebe herself had experienced.

context: In John chapter number 10, He said, my sheep hear my voice and I know them and I give unto them eternal life and they shall never perish.

context: it was the Lord Jesus Christ in John 13 that said, a new

context: where He says, it is finished.
point_summary: Christ's death on the cross was complete and sufficient — He "gave up the ghost" voluntarily, fulfilling the prophecy that not one of His bones would be broken, before the soldiers could break them.

context: they found that He was dead already.
point_summary: The soldiers' discovery that Jesus was already dead — and their consequent decision not to break His legs — is presented as fulfillment of Old Testament prophecy and confirmation that He had voluntarily given up His life.

context: not one of His bones would be broken
point_summary: Scripture's prophecy that none of Christ's bones would be broken was fulfilled precisely because Jesus had already given up His life before the soldiers could break His legs.

context: Jesus answered and said unto them, destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up.
point_summary: Jesus predicted His own bodily resurrection at the outset of His ministry, and the angel's phrase "as He said" at the empty tomb confirms that He always does what He says He will do.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life.
point_summary: Jesus is identified as "the way" precisely because he made the only path for sinners to be reconciled to God, connecting his resurrection victory to the personal salvation he offers.

context: how Jesus was revealing these things to His disciples
point_summary: Jesus's foreknowledge of His coming death and glorification, revealed progressively to His disciples, frames the sorrow they experienced as part of God's sovereign plan.

context: let not your heart be troubled
point_summary: Christ's words "let not your heart be troubled" in John 14:1 are presented as His direct response to the disciples' sorrow upon hearing His predictions of betrayal and death.

context: mine hour is not yet come

context: the hour is come
point_summary: Jesus's declaration in John 17 that "the hour is come" marks the arrival of the long-anticipated moment of redemption, contrasting with His earlier statements throughout John that His hour had not yet come.

context: Turn to John 5 and verse number 35. John the Baptist is a man in which had a powerful ministry

context: he must increase. I must decrease.

context: Jesus said, except a man be born again, he cannot see the Kingdom of God.

context: He said, let not your hearts be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in me.

context: Nicodemus was in that situation. He came to Jesus by night in John chapter number three, and he was religious, but Jesus said, 'Ye must be born again, ye must be born again.'
point_summary: Nicodemus illustrates that religious standing offers no exemption from the necessity of new birth, as Jesus declared to him directly.

context: He said in John 15, without Me, ye can do nothing.
point_summary: Dependence on God alone—not self-reliance—drove the early church to prayer, grounded in Christ's own declaration that apart from Him nothing can be accomplished.

context: if the world hates you, you know that it hated Me before it hated you.

context: If you were of the world, the world would love His own. Because you're not of the world, but I have chosen you out of the world, therefore the world hateth you.

context: Remember the word that I said unto you, the servant is not greater than his Lord. If they have persecuted Me, they will also persecute you. If they have kept My saying, they will keep yours also.
