context: If ye know these things, happy are ye if ye do them.
point_summary: Knowing and doing God's will are inseparable: Jesus' words in John 13:17 ground the sermon's point that obedience to revealed truth—not mere knowledge—brings the blessing of God.

context: And He gave them an example that you should do as I have done to you is what He says in v. 15.
point_summary: Knowing Christ's example of servant-leadership is not enough; blessing comes only when that knowledge is translated into doing, as Jesus himself declared in John 13:17.

context: if you abide in me and my words abide in you, you shall ask what you will and it shall be done unto you
point_summary: Abiding in Christ and bearing much fruit glorifies the Father, connecting John 15:7 to Colossians 1:10's call to be fruitful in every good work as an expression of doing God's will.

context: Herein is my Father glorified that you bear much fruit
point_summary: Bearing fruit in every good work, as Paul prays in Colossians 1:10, glorifies the Father — a connection drawn directly from Jesus' words in John 15:8.

context: I am the vine, ye are the branches. He that abideth in me and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit, for without me ye can do nothing.
point_summary: Bearing fruit in God's will requires divine enablement, not human strength—because apart from Christ, as Jesus declared, we can do nothing.

context: The Bible says that you must be born again.
point_summary: Salvation is not a process but the miracle of a moment—a supernatural transaction in which the believer is instantly delivered from darkness and translated into Christ's kingdom, as Jesus declared one must be born again.

context: But Jesus in John chapter number 4 says, I must needs go through Samaria.
point_summary: Jesus' deliberate journey through Samaria, counter to Jewish cultural practice, illustrates his rejection of partiality and love for all people made in God's image.

context: The other is in John chapter two. And here's what they say. Well, Jesus turned water into wine.
point_summary: John 2's account of Jesus turning water into wine is cited only as one of two texts people commonly use to justify alcohol consumption, without any exposition of the passage itself.

context: In John chapter number three, the Bible tells us this in verse number 20 with regard to this thought of those that are despisers of that which is good
point_summary: Evil-doers despise those who are good because, as John 3:20 states, they hate the light and avoid it lest their wicked deeds be exposed and reproved.

context: number three, we're familiar with much of this chapter in the interaction with Nicodemus and the wonderful proclamation of John 3 16 and all that takes place
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: but notice in verse number 20, the Bible says, for everyone that doeth evil hateth the light. Why? And then it says, neither cometh to the light lest his deeds should be reproved.
point_summary: Evil-doers despise the godly not out of mere hostility but because the light of righteous living exposes and reproves their deeds, driving them to separate themselves from it.

context: I mentioned in John chapter number 3 when you think about Nicodemus, you know what Nicodemus was? He was a religious man, but you know religion couldn't save Nicodemus.
point_summary: Nicodemus, a religious man who came to Jesus by night, illustrates that outward religion cannot save—only the new birth can.

context: He comes to Jesus by night and Jesus says, you must be born again.
point_summary: Religion, no matter how devout its outward form, cannot save—as Jesus told the religious Nicodemus, "You must be born again."

context: We find that when we consider not only Nicodemus in John chapter number 3, but you could consider Cornelius in Acts chapter number 10.
point_summary: Nicodemus's encounter with Jesus in John 3 illustrates that religious observance, however sincere, cannot save—only the new birth can.

context: In John 8 in verse number 44, Jesus says this. He says to these religious leaders, and by the way, they're attacking him, and speaking of which he was born of fornication, according to verse number 41, and we know that that's not true, and we know the miracle of the birth, the incarnation of Christ, and he says in verse 44, ye are of your father the devil, and the lust of your father you will do. He was a murderer from the beginning, and a bode not in the truth, because there is no truth in him.
point_summary: Satan is identified as the originator and father of all lying and deception, explaining why those who deny Christ—the Truth—remain perpetually deceived despite increasing knowledge.

context: 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. 'No man cometh unto the Father but by me.'
point_summary: False teachers exploit shared biblical vocabulary—including the word *saved*—while emptying it of its true meaning: exclusive salvation through Christ alone, as Jesus himself declared in John 14:6.

context: Sanctify them through thy word. Thy word is truth.
point_summary: Sanctify them through thy word; thy word is truth — Scripture is presented as the reliable, enduring standard against which all teaching must be tested, and by which God's people are set apart amid pervasive deception.

context: What did Jesus say? John 10 we referenced this morning about the Good Shepherd. He says, I lay down my life for the sheep. No man taketh it from me.
point_summary: Jesus' voluntary surrender of his life — "no man taketh it from me" — is cited as evidence that he was in absolute control of his own death, not subject to Pilate's authority.

context: All I know is I was blind, and now I can see.
point_summary: The blind man's confession — "I was blind, now I see" — illustrates the spiritual blindness Paul describes in Romans 10:3, where Israel, ignorant of God's righteousness, pursues its own.

context: If I tell you again, are you gonna be his disciples also?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: are we blind also?
point_summary: The question of the Pharisees — "Are we blind also?" — illustrates the spiritual blindness Paul identifies in Romans 10:3 as ignorance of God's righteousness, driving people to establish their own.

context: By the way, He tells us that we're to worship Him in spirit and in truth. Amen? John 4.
point_summary: God's holiness defines the terms of worship, so that what He has prescribed—worship in spirit and truth (John 4:24)—is the standard, not whatever humans designate as worship.

context: Peter says to whom shall we go we know that now art the Christ and that thou hast the words of eternal life
point_summary: Peter's confession in John 6:68 is invoked to show that Christ alone holds the words of eternal life, making any return to a lesser system senseless.

context: In John 8, in verse number 32, the Lord Jesus Christ said this, and ye shall know the truth and the truth shall make you free.
point_summary: Truth is the remedy for deception, and knowing it—as Jesus declared in John 8:32—is what sets people free, making the consistent teaching of truth the essential defense against error.

context: Jesus declared in the 14th chapter of the book of John, I am the way, the truth, and the life.
point_summary: Truth itself is personified in Christ — who declared "I am the way, the truth, and the life" — making Him the ultimate answer to deception and error.

context: in John 17, in verse number 17, He says, sanctify them through Thy truth. Thy Word is truth.
point_summary: Sanctification through God's Word is the appointed remedy against deception, since Christ's own prayer identifies Scripture as truth and the means by which believers are set apart from error.

context: Jesus said, if they hate you, they hated me first.
point_summary: Suffering before glory is the pattern established by Christ himself, so believers should not be surprised when trials come, since Jesus warned that the world's hatred of him extends to his followers.

context: And He said in John 17 that He would receive the glory that was due, right?
point_summary: Christ's own pattern of suffering preceding glory—affirmed in John 17, where He anticipated receiving back the glory He had with the Father before the world—grounds the expectation that believers will likewise suffer before glory is revealed.

context: as Jesus said in John 14, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me
point_summary: Salvation is exclusively through Jesus Christ—who declared himself *the* way, not *a* way—making personal faith in him, not religious heritage or moral comparison, the only means of being saved.

context: He came to His own, but His own received Him not.
point_summary: Salvation is a gift that can be received or rejected, as John 1:11–12 frames Christ's coming to His own who rejected Him, yet granting power to become God's sons to all who receive Him.

context: But as many as received Him, to them gave He power to become the sons of God.
point_summary: Receiving Christ is framed as a personal, decisive act—accepting or rejecting a gift already paid for—by which one is given power to become a son of God.

context: Jesus said, marvel not that I say unto thee, singularly to Nicodemus, that ye all people must be born again.
point_summary: Being born again is not optional or metaphorical — Jesus declared to Nicodemus, and through him to all people, that this spiritual birth is a universal necessity.

context: And Nicodemus says, I don't understand what this thing means to be born again. Can I enter into my mother's womb a second time?
point_summary: Nicodemus's literal, confused question about re-entering the womb illustrates the natural mind's inability to grasp that the new birth Jesus requires is spiritual, not physical.

context: I was a child of the devil, according to John chapter number eight.
point_summary: Before salvation, every lost person—however respectable they may appear—is a child of the devil, as John 8 identifies, though they may be entirely unaware of that spiritual condition.

context: Nicodemus, a ruler of the Jews
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I lay down my life for the sheep.
point_summary: Christ's willingness to lay down his life for the sheep (John 10:15) is cited as proof that the cross was not accidental but the only sufficient means by which humanity could be saved.

context: And I taketh it up again.
point_summary: Christ's willingness to lay down his life and take it up again is cited as proof that salvation required the cross and could not be achieved by law-keeping or good works.

context: he's a liar, he's the father of it
point_summary: Satan's identity as a liar and the father of lies (John 8:44) is invoked to attribute the introduction of error into modern Bible translations to the enemy's ongoing deceptive work.

context: sanctify them by thy word. Thy word is truth.
point_summary: Knowing the truth of Scripture thoroughly equips believers to expose error without fear, because, as Jesus prayed, God's Word is truth and truth always exposes error.

context: John chapter number 8 deals with people bringing someone to Christ, a lady, that said she was caught in the very act of adultery. She should be stoned. The law condemns her.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: let him that hath no sin cast the first stone
context: He just starts writing. I think He's writing their sin. That's just what I believe. I have no idea what He's writing. It doesn't tell us. But He's just writing, and He says, let him that hath no sin cast the first stone.
point_summary: The Chinese government's rewriting of John 8:7's account—replacing Jesus' mercy with an execution—illustrates how false teaching distorts Christ's character and why doctrinal vigilance is urgent.

context: And then He says to her, hey, where are these nine accusers? They're all gone, because they were guilty too.
point_summary: A Chinese government rewriting of John 8 replaces Jesus's words of forgiveness with an account of Him stoning the woman, illustrating how false teaching distorts Christ's character and the gospel.

context: And then Jesus said to her, go and sin no more. Amen? John 8.
point_summary: The Chinese government's rewriting of John 8:11 — making Jesus stone the woman rather than forgive her — is cited as a vivid, contemporary example of how false teaching dangerously distorts the character of Christ.

context: he said I am the way the truth and the life no man cometh unto the Father but by me
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: in John chapter number 10 in verse number 10 the Bible says that he came as the Good Shepherd and he gives life and he gives life to the world He gives it more abundantly.
point_summary: Christ as the Good Shepherd gives life abundantly—a life qualitatively richer than existence before salvation, contrasting the believer's heaven-bound reality with merely living for the here and now.

context: Remember, it was the Lord Jesus Christ in John 10, I referenced a moment ago when He said that I give them life that they might have it more abundantly, that He says just a few verses after that, that without Me, ye can do nothing.
point_summary: Christ's gift of abundant life (John 10) is paired with His warning that apart from Him believers can do nothing, establishing that He is the sole source of life and fruitfulness.

context: let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in Me.
point_summary: Christ's promise to return, cited from John 14:1–3, grounds the certainty of Paul's "shall appear" in Colossians 3:4 — Jesus himself pledged his coming to troubled disciples, and God cannot fail his word.

context: In My Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:2–3 is cited as the scriptural foundation for the certainty of His return, underscoring that God's declared word cannot fail.

context: and if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto Myself that where I am, there ye may be also.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:3 — "I will come again" — is cited as the foundation of the "declared promise" that His return is certain, not merely possible.

context: Remember the truth of John 1, in the beginning was the Word, the Word was with God, the Word was God.
point_summary: The title "Word of God" given to the returning Christ in Revelation 19:13 is grounded in John 1:1, identifying the conquering rider as the eternal Word who became flesh.

context: The Word was made flesh and dwelt among us in verse number 14, and here his name is the Word of God.
point_summary: The incarnate Word of John 1:14 is identified as the same returning conqueror of Revelation 19, whose name "the Word of God" confirms his divine identity across both texts.

context: ...we see it in John 3 in verse 16 as well, for God so loved the world...
point_summary: God's love for the world in John 3:16 is invoked to illuminate the weight of the word "so" in 1 John 4:11, grounding the command to love one another in the magnitude of divine love already received.

context: He loved until the end.
point_summary: Christ's love "until the end" — extending even to Judas with the word "Friend" — is held up as the pattern believers are called to follow in loving one another.

context: If you love me, keep my commandments
point_summary: Obedience to Christ's commands is presented as the observable, verifiable proof of genuine love for him, exposing any claim of love unaccompanied by keeping his word as a lie.

context: by this shall all men know that you are my disciples if you have love one to another
point_summary: Love for fellow believers is the observable, outward proof by which the world identifies true disciples of Christ.

context: Think about the Lord Jesus Christ in John chapter number 13, a wonderful passage of Scripture that deals with not only the love, but also the humility of the Lord Jesus Christ being a servant.
point_summary: John 13's account of Jesus washing the disciples' feet is presented as the supreme demonstration that love and humility are inseparable from servanthood.

context: It begins in John 13 by saying he loved them until the end.
point_summary: Jesus's love for his disciples expressed through foot-washing—the lowest of tasks—demonstrates that genuine love for one another takes the form of humble servanthood.

context: And it ends in John chapter number 13 by Jesus saying, a new commandment have I given you that you love not as yourself, but you love as I have loved.
point_summary: Jesus's command in John 13 to love "as I have loved" is grounded in his servant example of washing the disciples' feet, establishing self-giving service as the pattern for loving one another.

context: let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in me.
point_summary: Certainty of heaven rests not on wishful thinking but on Christ's own promise to prepare a place and return for his own, making the believer's hope an assured reality.

context: In my Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you, I go to prepare a place for you.
point_summary: Jesus's promise in John 14:2 grounds the believer's hope of heaven as certain assurance, not mere wishful thinking, because Christ himself declared he goes to prepare a place for his own.

context: And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto myself, that where I am, there you may be also.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:3 grounds the believer's hope of heaven not in wishful uncertainty but in assured certainty, because Jesus himself declared he would return to receive his own.

context: Jesus said this in John 14 and verse number 15, if you love me, keep my commandments.
point_summary: Obedience to the Great Commission is framed as an expression of love for Christ, grounding missions in the principle that loving Jesus means keeping His commandments.

context: Jesus is speaking to a religious man by the name of Nicodemus. He was a Pharisee. He was a ruler of the Jews. The Bible tells us in verse number one, you find a man that is religious.
point_summary: Nicodemus's religious credentials illustrate that knowing about God is insufficient for salvation, since even demons acknowledge Christ's identity without being saved.

context: but as many as received Him to them gave He power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on His name
point_summary: Genuine belief, as drawn from John 1:12, means personally receiving Christ as Savior—not merely a mental acknowledgment that God exists.

context: Jesus responded and said, verily, verily, of a truth of a truth I say unto thee, except a man be born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God
point_summary: Even religious knowledge and moral standing leave a person lost until Christ declares the necessity of new birth, as illustrated by Nicodemus's encounter with Jesus.

context: He says this in verse number 4, how can a man be born when he's old? Can he enter the second time into his mother's womb and be born?
point_summary: Nicodemus's literal, flesh-bound misunderstanding of rebirth illustrates that physical birth alone is insufficient—every person must also be born of the Spirit to enter God's kingdom.

context: He says in verse number 5, I say unto thee, except a man be born of water and of the Spirit, he cannot enter into the kingdom of God.
point_summary: Jesus's words to Nicodemus establish that physical birth alone is insufficient—every person has been born of water, but entrance into God's kingdom requires a second, spiritual birth.

context: well, verse number 6, that which is born of the flesh is flesh and that which is born of the Spirit is Spirit.
point_summary: Born once in the flesh is universal but insufficient; Jesus tells Nicodemus a second birth—of the Spirit—is required to enter the kingdom of God.

context: And Jesus says in verse number 7, marvel not that I said unto thee, ye must be born again.
point_summary: Jesus' declaration "ye must be born again" is applied universally — physical birth is common to all, but spiritual rebirth is the necessary second birth that addresses the lost condition of every person.

context: Jesus said you must be born again
point_summary: Being born once physically is not enough—Jesus declares to the religious-but-lost Nicodemus that a second birth, of the Spirit rather than water, is the necessary condition for entering the kingdom of God.

context: marvel not at this truth
point_summary: Jesus's command "marvel not" in John 3:7 frames the new birth not as an incomprehensible mystery to be rejected but as a necessary spiritual reality every person must receive, distinct from physical birth.

context: verse 15, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish but have eternal life.
point_summary: Eternal life is the gift offered to all who believe in Christ, set in contrast to perishing — the spiritual death awaiting those born only once, never born of the Spirit.

context: verse 16, probably one of the most commonly known verses in all of the Bible, for God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish but have everlasting life.
point_summary: God's love for the world in John 3:16 is set against His greater attribute of holiness and justice, so that the gift of His Son satisfies divine judgment while offering eternal life to all who believe.

context: world that He gave His only begotten Son. What is He speaking of?
point_summary: God's holiness and justice required a sacrifice, and John 3:16 shows that His love for the world moved Him to provide that sacrifice in His Son so that believing sinners might escape condemnation and receive everlasting life.

context: in verse 14, He talks about Moses being lifted up in the wilderness, even so must the Son of Man be lifted up.
point_summary: The lifting up of Moses' bronze serpent in the wilderness is read as a direct foreshadowing of Christ's crucifixion, establishing that God's love required a just sacrifice at Calvary.

context: Notice what it says in verse 17, for God sent not His Son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world through Him might be saved.
point_summary: God's mission in sending the Son was not condemnation but salvation, since humanity was already condemned and in need of rescue.

context: He says that in verse 18. He that believeth on him is not condemned, but he that believeth not is condemned already, because he hath not believed in the name of the only begotten Son of God.
point_summary: Humanity stands already condemned apart from Christ, making belief in the only begotten Son the sole escape from a judgment that is not future but present.

context: He that believeth on the Son hath everlasting life, and he that believeth not the Son shall not see life, but the wrath of God abideth on him.
point_summary: Belief in the Son brings everlasting life, while unbelief leaves a person already under God's wrath—the default condemned condition of all humanity apart from Christ.

context: He that believeth not, according to verse number 18, is condemned already.
point_summary: Humanity's default condition apart from Christ is already one of condemnation, making unbelief not a neutral stance but the state every person is born into and must be rescued from.

context: Jesus said, you must be born again.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he gives them the command that they're love as he loved
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: let not your heart be troubled you believe in God believe also in me
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:1–3 to return and receive His disciples gave urgent motivation to obey the Great Commission, since the angels' confirmation of His coming reinforced the last command He had given them.

context: in my father's house or many mansions if it were not so I would have told you I go to prepare a place for you
point_summary: Christ's promise to return—echoed by the angels at the ascension—gave the disciples urgent motivation to obey the last command they heard from Jesus: go and preach the gospel.

context: go and prepare a place for you I will come again and receive you unto myself that where I am there you may be also
point_summary: Christ's promised return in John 14:3, heard firsthand by the disciples and confirmed by the angels at the ascension, is presented as the driving motivation behind the early church's urgent obedience to the Great Commission.

context: Jesus is truth he said I'm the way the truth in the life
point_summary: Jesus is identified as "the truth" from John 14:6 to ground the claim that rejecting the gospel means rejecting truth itself, leaving the lost without salvation.

context: he said if you love me keep my commandments
point_summary: Obedience to Christ's command is cited as the primary motivation for missions, with John 14:15 grounding the church's missionary investment in love expressed through keeping His commandments.

context: Notice what the Bible says in John chapter number five this morning in verse number 24.
point_summary: Believers will not face condemnation at the judgment seat of Christ because, as John 5:24 declares, those who hear and believe have already passed from death unto life.

context: John chapter five and verse number 24, the Bible says this, verily, verily, I say unto you, he that heareth my word and believeth on him that sent me hath everlasting life, notice this, and shall not come into condemnation, but is passed from death unto life.
point_summary: Because the believer has everlasting life and shall not come into condemnation, John 5:24 is cited as evidence that the Judgment Seat of Christ is not a judgment for sin.

context: Jesus said, my peace give I unto thee
point_summary: Christ's peace—described as "his peace," perfect and surpassing understanding—is offered to believers precisely amid turmoil and adversity, contrasting with the chaos surrounding Revelation's original recipients.

context: In the beginning was the Word and the Word was with God and the Word was God.
point_summary: John 1:1's declaration that "the Word was God" is cited alongside John 1:14–15 to establish Christ's eternality—that he existed before John the Baptist despite being born after him physically.

context: And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us.
point_summary: The Word's incarnation ("the Word became flesh," John 1:14) is cited as evidence of Christ's eternality, with John the Baptist's testimony in verse 15 immediately invoked to show that the one born after him nonetheless existed before him.

context: John bear witness of him.
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration that Jesus "was before me"—despite being born after him physically—confirms the eternal pre-existence of Christ that the sermon connects to Revelation 1:8's "which is and which was and which is to come."

context: John the Baptist is able to say in verse number 15 that he was before me
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration that Jesus "was before me"—despite being born after him physically—is cited as evidence of Christ's eternal pre-existence.

context: we find that in verse number 30 of John chapter number one, this reference is made again
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration that Jesus "was before me"—despite being born after him physically—is cited as evidence of Christ's eternal preexistence, reinforcing the "which is and which was and which is to come" of Revelation 1:8.

context: John is recording this prayer of the Lord in John 17
point_summary: John 17 is identified as the true Lord's Prayer—Jesus's own prayer to the Father—distinguishing it from the model prayer of Matthew 6, with verse 5 cited to develop Christ's eternal glory.

context: these words spake Jesus and lifted up his eyes to heaven. And he said, Father, the hour has come. Glorify thy son that thy son also may glorify thee.
point_summary: John 17:1 and 5 are cited together to establish that Christ's pre-incarnate glory confirms his eternality—the same eternality declared in Revelation 1:8's "Alpha and Omega."

context: and now, O Father, glorify thou me with thine own self, with the glory which I had with thee before the world was.
point_summary: Jesus's pre-incarnate glory, affirmed in his own prayer that he shared glory with the Father before the world existed, establishes the eternality that Revelation 1:8 later declares with "Alpha and Omega."

context: The Bible says in John three in verse 16, a verse that many of us are familiar with.
point_summary: Love as the motive for Christ's death is grounded in John 3:16, cited to confirm that the cross was an act of divine love toward the world.

context: For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish but have everlasting life.
point_summary: John 3:16 is cited as the scriptural ground for the love that motivated Christ's cross, directly linking "for God so loved the world" to Revelation 1:5's phrase "unto him that loved us."

context: having loved his own, which were in the world, he loved them unto the end
point_summary: Christ's love for his own is not temporary or conditional — having loved them, he loved them to the end, a truth the sermon connects to Revelation 1:5's phrase "unto him that loved us."

context: Jesus says I am the good shepherd. And he says that the good shepherd, what does the good shepherd do? He gives his life for the sheep.
point_summary: Christ's self-giving death as the Good Shepherd—voluntarily laying down his life as the required sacrifice for sin, with no man taking it from him—is presented as the supreme demonstration of God's love.

context: He says in John 10 that he not only is gonna lay his life down, but he's gonna take it up again.
point_summary: In John 10, Jesus as the Good Shepherd voluntarily laid down his life for the sheep and took it up again, with the resurrection foretold in that same passage.

context: Remember John one in verse number 11, he came to his own, but his own received him not.
point_summary: Those who receive Christ are granted the power to become sons of God, contrasted with the rejection he faced from his own people in John 1:11.

context: But verse number 12, but as many as received him to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name.
point_summary: Receiving Christ grants the believer power to become a son of God, a privileged adoption illustrated by Mephibosheth's undeserved welcome into David's household.

context: even as John is mindful in John 14, as he had the privilege of writing those words, let not your heart be troubled
point_summary: Zechariah's prophecy of Israel looking upon the pierced One connects to Revelation 1:7 and ultimately to Christ's physical return to the Mount of Olives, grounding John 14's "I will come again" promise in literal, geographical fulfillment.

context: In my Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you. And then he says this, I go to prepare a place for you, and if I go and prepare a place, I'll come again and receive you unto myself, that where I am, there ye may be also.
point_summary: Christ's promised return, voiced in John 14:2's "I will come again," is presented as the faithful witness's own guarantee that he is coming back.

context: In my Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you. And then he says this, I go to prepare a place for you, and if I go and prepare a place, I'll come again and receive you unto myself, that where I am, there ye may be also.
point_summary: Christ's own promise in John 14:3 — "I will come again" — is cited as the foundational pledge undergirding the believer's confidence in His certain return.

context: let not your heart be troubled.
point_summary: Christ's promise "I will come again" in John 14:1–3 is linked to His identity as the faithful witness, establishing His return as certain rather than conditional.

context: me, in verse number 21 he says, well what about this guy? He says, Lord and what shall this man do? What's he concerned about? He's concerned about John, right? And Jesus says, if I will that he tarry till I come, what is that to thee? Follow thou me.
point_summary: Peter's deflecting question about John — "what shall this man do?" — illustrates the danger of comparing oneself to other believers rather than answering Christ's personal call to follow Him.

context: when Jesus said, destroy this temple in three days, I'll rise it up
point_summary: Christ's declaration "destroy this temple" (John 2:19) is read as a reference to His bodily resurrection, which vindicated His claims and secured the believer's new and living access into God's presence.

context: Jesus said that to His disciples. He said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by Me.
point_summary: Christ's role as "the way" in John 14:6 is cited to establish that he is the exclusive means of access to the Father, illustrated by contrast with restricted-access buildings requiring proper credentials.

context: Jesus as He would wash the disciples' feet
point_summary: Jesus washing the disciples' feet in John 13 is cited as a picture of the cleansing required for service, paralleling the laver in the tabernacle and the call to draw near with purified hearts.

context: therefore when he was gone out Jesus said now is the Son of Man glorified and God is glorified in him
point_summary: Christ's command in John 13:34–35 to love one another as He loved sets a higher standard than loving one's neighbor as oneself, grounding Christian love in His sacrificial example.

context: if God be glorified in him God shall also glorify him in himself and shall straightway glorify him
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: a new commandment I give unto you that you love one another as I have loved you that you also love one another
point_summary: Christ's command in John 13:34 sets a higher standard than loving one's neighbor as oneself—believers are to love one another as Christ loved, with sacrificial love.

context: by this shall all men know that you're my disciples if you have love one to another
point_summary: Loving one another as Christ loved—sacrificially, not merely as neighbor-love—is the visible mark by which all men recognize true disciples.

context: God is a spirit they that worship him must worship him in spirit and truth John 4 and verse 24
point_summary: God's nature as spirit, cited from John 4:24, grounds his omnipresence — the truth that he is everywhere present — as the third attribute establishing his greatness.

context: as Jesus is praying, He says, He speaks about the glory that He had with God in eternity past
point_summary: In John 17, Jesus prays referencing the glory He shared with the Father in eternity past, confirming His pre-existent, eternal nature before creation.

context: Remember Jesus said in John 8, in verse number 58, when He's speaking to the religious leaders of the day, He's speaking about Abraham rejoices to see his day. And they said, man, you're not even 50 years old, what are you talking about? And He says, before Abraham was, I am.
point_summary: Jesus's claim "before Abraham was, I am" is connected to Exodus 3's divine name, establishing that Christ shares the eternal "I AM" identity of the God who spoke to Moses.

context: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God
point_summary: John 1:1 is cited to establish Christ's full deity as the grounding truth behind Philippians 2:5–8—that the one who humbled himself unto death was none other than God incarnate.

context: And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us
point_summary: The Incarnation—the Word becoming flesh and dwelling among humanity—is cited as the supreme demonstration of divine humility, grounding the call to serve Christ with a lowly mind.

context: You read John 17, and you read about the glory that He had with the Father, and eternity passed, and yet He left the glories of heaven, the splendor of heaven, to come to this sin-cursed world so they could die for us.
point_summary: John 17's depiction of Christ's pre-incarnate glory with the Father is cited as evidence that He willingly left heaven's splendor to humble Himself and die for sinners.

context: as he's praying there in John chapter number 17, he mentions this very truth with regard to his ministry and finishing the work that he'd been sent to do
point_summary: Christ's prayer in John 17 grounds the believer's dual identity—not of the world yet sent into it—as the basis for understanding the Christian's ambassadorial mission.

context: I have given them thy word and the world hath hated them because they are not of the world even as I am not of the world.
point_summary: Believers are kept in the world rather than removed from it because Christ has sent them into it as his ambassadors, to shine as lights amid a crooked and perverse world.

context: I pray not that thou shouldest take them out of the world, but thou shouldest keep them from the evil.
point_summary: Believers are kept in the world rather than removed from it because Christ has sent them into it as his ambassadors and representatives, to shine as lights amid a crooked and perverse world.

context: They are not of the world even as I am not of the world.
point_summary: Believers are not removed from the world at salvation but are intentionally left in it as Christ's sent ambassadors, mirroring the mission Christ himself was given by the Father.

context: Sanctify them through thy truth, thy word is truth.
point_summary: Sanctify them through thy truth — John 17:17 is cited to establish that believers, sent into the world as Christ's ambassadors, are kept and set apart by God's Word rather than removed from the world.

context: as thou hast sent me into the world, even so have I also sent them into the world.
point_summary: Believers are not removed from the world at salvation but are deliberately sent into it as Christ's ambassadors, mirroring the Father's sending of the Son.

context: In John 16 and verse number 13, the Bible plainly tells us as Jesus is preparing to go to the cross of Calvary and He's giving the disciples some instruction with regard to the days ahead and the ministry that they would have to fulfill. He says this in verse number 13 of John 16, how be it when He, He's speaking of the Holy Spirit, He refers to Him as the Spirit of truth. We understand that He's referred to Him as the Comforter, even within the same context of this passage with the instruction that He's giving, and He calls Him here the Spirit of truth. And He says that when He, the Spirit of truth, has come, notice what it says, He will guide you into all truth.
point_summary: Because the Holy Spirit indwells believers at salvation, He functions as their divine teacher, guiding them into all truth in a way He does not for the unregenerate.

context: For He shall not speak of Himself, but whatsoever He shall hear, that shall He speak, and He will show you things to come.
point_summary: The Holy Spirit, identified as the Spirit of truth, is the divine teacher who indwells believers and guides them into all truth—a capacity the unregenerate, lacking His presence, do not possess.

context: And we find that in the same text of Scripture in John chapter number 16, when he speaks about how the Holy Spirit of God, the Spirit of Truth would come and He would guide us
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's role as guide into all truth (John 16) is invoked to contrast the Spirit's indwelling teaching ministry in believers with its absence in the unregenerate, who therefore cannot receive scriptural argument.

context: it says in John 16 in verse number 8, speaking of the Holy Spirit, the Spirit of Truth, the Comforter, that when He has come, He will reprove the world of sin and of righteousness and of judgment.
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's specific ministry toward the unregenerate natural man is to convict the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment—not to grant comprehension of Scripture, which belongs to the regenerate.

context: of Christ's righteousness, and of judgment to come. That's from John 16, verses 8 through 11.
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's ministry toward the unregenerate—convicting of sin, of Christ's righteousness, and of coming judgment—is the only means by which the natural man can be reached, since biblical truth alone cannot penetrate without it.

context: of Christ's righteousness, and of judgment to come. That's from John 16, verses 8 through 11.
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's specific work of reproving the world "of sin" (John 16:9) is identified as conviction of unbelief, marking this as His distinct ministry toward the unregenerate natural man.

context: of Christ's righteousness, and of judgment to come. That's from John 16, verses 8 through 11.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: of Christ's righteousness, and of judgment to come. That's from John 16, verses 8 through 11.
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's convicting work toward the unregenerate—of sin, righteousness, and judgment—is identified as the specific ministry described across John 16:8–11, applied to the lost man's inability to receive biblical truth.

context: In John chapter nine, we won't take the time to go there, but there's a wonderful account of a miracle that took place of the man that was born blind.
point_summary: John 9's account of the man born blind illustrates that spiritual blindness—not physical—was the true condition of the religious leaders who rejected Christ.

context: But in John 1 and verse number 12, the Bible says, but as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name.
point_summary: Receiving Christ grants believers the power to become sons of God, establishing the adoptive relationship that makes calling God "Father" in prayer both possible and meaningful.

context: The Bible says Jesus wept.
point_summary: Christ's weeping at Lazarus's tomb (John 11:35) is cited as one of three occasions where Jesus prayed with tears, illustrating the genuine compassion and humanity he displayed alongside his deity.

context: He says as He makes the great declaration in John chapter number 10, I am the Good Shepherd.
point_summary: Christ's willingness to die is underscored by His own declaration in John 10:11 that as the Good Shepherd He lays down His life for the sheep, showing Gethsemane was not a prayer to escape death.

reference_text: chapter number 17 of the book of John
context: He says in chapter number 17 of the book of John, the hour is come.
point_summary: Christ's declaration in John 17 that "the hour is come" is cited as evidence that He went to the cross willingly and with full foreknowledge, not reluctantly.

context: I have finished the work
point_summary: Christ's being "made perfect" (v. 9) means He completed the mission He was sent to accomplish, as He himself declared in John 17 — not that He lacked moral perfection.

context: Jesus says this in John chapter number 10, I've come to give you life and that you might have it more abundantly.
point_summary: Abundant life in Christ, drawn from John 10:10, is realized not merely at salvation but through ongoing daily fellowship and walk with Him.

context: I'm the good shepherd, he says
point_summary: Christ's declaration "I am the good shepherd" is applied to Joseph's providential care of his family, illustrating that God supplies, protects, and leads His people as their shepherd.

context: he says in John 17 that that he experienced that glory that he had from the beginning
point_summary: John 17 is cited as evidence that Christ possessed and consciously relinquished a pre-incarnate glory, illustrating the self-emptying humility Paul commands in Philippians 2.

context: we understand that in John chapter one as John the Baptist is there
point_summary: John the Baptist's acknowledgment that Christ "is before me" is cited as evidence of Christ's eternality despite being physically younger than John.

context: says this in John 8 and verse number 44 as Jesus is speaking and he's speaking to these religious leaders and he says, you're of your father the devil and the lust of your father you will do. He was a murderer from the beginning and abode not in the truth because there is no truth in him. When he speaketh a lie, he speaketh of his own for he's a liar and the father of it.
point_summary: Satan's nature as a liar and the father of lies, cited from John 8:44, grounds the warning that false teachers—active in Paul's day and now—use familiar vocabulary while meaning something entirely different.

context: Jesus said in John 17, sanctify them through Thy Word. Thy Word is truth.
point_summary: Sanctification through the Word—cited from Jesus' high-priestly prayer—is presented as the scriptural basis for spiritual growth as the primary defense against false teaching.

context: Remember what Jesus said in John chapter number 13 as he would give instruction to the disciples. He says a new commandment I give you that you love one another as as I have loved you.
point_summary: Christ's command in John 13:34 to love one another is invoked to show that being "knit together in love" produces the unity that results from embracing truth, not from avoiding division.

context: By this, by what? What? Our love one for another. Shall all men know that You are My disciples?
point_summary: Christian unity—being "knit together in love"—is the visible mark of discipleship that Jesus commanded, and Paul echoes this as the antidote to the division false teachers create.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth, and the life.
point_summary: Christ's exclusive claim in John 14:6 — "I am the way, the truth, and the life" — is cited as evidence that truth is absolute, not relative, grounding the unity Paul calls for in objective divine truth.

context: Thy Word is truth.
point_summary: Unity in the body is grounded in embracing objective truth, with "Thy Word is truth" (John 17:17) cited to refute the notion that truth is relative.

context: And yes, we must deal with and we must acknowledge the fact that God so loved the world and I'm thankful for God's love.
point_summary: God's love in John 3:16 must be held alongside His justice, since sin required a payment that sent Christ to the cross.

context: We find that there, as you read the fourth chapter of the book of John, he says, I must needs go through Samaria.
point_summary: Christ's deliberate journey through Samaria to reach the woman at the well illustrates that Jesus purposefully sought out sinners in need of salvation.

context: And in John chapter number two, when it talks about this is the beginning of miracles, right after the miracle at the marriage at Cana, you read about Jesus going into the temple and turning over the tables and driving people out.
point_summary: The cleansing of the temple in John 2, positioned at the very outset of Jesus' public ministry, signals—consistent with Old Testament revival patterns under Hezekiah and Josiah—that genuine renewal begins with the purging of defilement from God's house.

context: destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up
point_summary: Christ's citation of raising the temple in three days (John 2:19) is presented as pointing to His bodily resurrection, framed within the broader sermon argument that the temple—now the believer's body—must not be defiled.

context: But as many as received Him, to them gave He power to become the sons of God.
point_summary: Receiving Christ brings a new identity and new name through adoption into God's family, as John 1:12 grounds the promise of the white stone's "new name" in the believer's legal standing as a son of God.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth and the life. No man, that means there's no exceptions, cometh unto the Father, but by me.
point_summary: John 14:6 is cited as the scriptural ground for remaining unwavering against cultural pressure to treat Christ as merely one path among many, with the definite article—"the way," not "a way"—bearing the full exclusivity of the claim.

context: Jesus Christ before he went to the cross of Calvary he said sanctify them through thy word thy word is truth John 17 and verse 17
point_summary: Scripture itself is the standard of sanctifying truth, cited here to anchor the sermon's call for fathers and mature believers to impart biblical truth to the next generation.

context: Jesus says this, and of course we know that He is perfect and that He is the perfect example. He says in John 8, in verse number 29, for I do always those things that please Him.
point_summary: Christ's own declaration in John 8:29 — "I do always those things that please Him" — is cited as the supreme model of embodying and not merely teaching truth, surpassing even Paul's example.

context: we've referenced John 13 a number of times
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: in John 15, in verse 12, he says, this is my commandment that you love one another as I have loved you.
point_summary: Christ's repeated command that believers love one another—cited here alongside John 13 and 1 John 3—is presented as the clear, simple will of God for his people.

context: He says in verse 17, these things I command you that you love one another.
point_summary: Loving one another is not a suggestion but a repeated, emphatic command of Christ, cited here alongside John 15:12 to underscore that mutual love is God's clear will for his people.

context: abide in Me, and I in you
point_summary: Obedience to God's commandments produces the blessing of His abiding presence, grounded in Christ's own teaching that branches must remain in the vine or accomplish nothing apart from Him.

context: I am the vine, Jesus says, but you're the branches
point_summary: Obedience and abiding are inseparable: John 15:5 grounds the promise of 1 John 3:24 by showing that the believer's fruitfulness and the indwelling presence both depend entirely on remaining in Christ.

context: for without Me you can do nothing
point_summary: Obedience to Christ's commandments produces His abiding presence, grounded in the vine-and-branches truth of John 15 that believers can do nothing apart from Him.

context: it's expedient for you that I go away
point_summary: Christ's departure was necessary so that the Comforter could be sent, and it is by that indwelling Holy Spirit that believers know God abides in them.

context: Jesus said in John 3, you must be born again.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he came to his own, his own received him not
point_summary: John 1:11–12 is cited to show that confidence in heaven is not pride, because sonship belongs to those who received Christ, making it entirely about him and nothing about the believer.

context: but as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God
point_summary: Certainty of heaven is not prideful presumption but a confidence grounded entirely in Christ, since sonship itself—receiving him per John 1:12—is God's gift, not human achievement.

context: We find that Jesus, John 14, you're familiar with the passage of Scripture.
point_summary: Jesus's words to his troubled disciples in John 14—"let not your heart be troubled" and "I go to prepare a place for you"—ground the sermon's claim that heaven's certainty transforms present perspective.

context: for we find in John chapter number 16 and verse number 33, He says, these things have I spoken unto you that you might have peace.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 16:33 — that tribulation is certain but He has overcome the world — grounds the disciples' troubled hearts in peace, setting the context for His "let not your heart be troubled" assurance in John 14.

context: In the world you shall have tribulation, but be of good cheer, I have overcome the world.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 16:33 — that He has overcome the world — is cited as the ground for the "let not your heart be troubled" command, assuring believers of peace amid tribulation.

context: let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in Me.
point_summary: Jesus's promise in John 14:1-3 that He goes to prepare a place for believers and will return so they may be where He is grounds the sermon's call to confident, joyful expectation of heaven.

context: in My Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you.
point_summary: Christ's personal promise — "where I am, there ye may be also" — grounds the sermon's case that heaven is a certain, joyful expectation for believers, not a vague hope.

context: I go to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:3 — that where He is, believers will also be — is presented as the foundation for the Christian's confident expectation of and excitement about heaven.

context: that where I am, there ye may be also.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:3 — that where He is, believers will also be — is presented as the foundation for the Christian's confident expectation of and excitement about heaven.

context: according to John 16, one of His ministries is to reprove the world of sin and of righteousness and of judgment to come
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's ministry of convicting the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment (John 16) is what makes gospel preaching effective—not human wisdom or eloquent words.

context: the good shepherd, he gave his life for the sheep and that we, according to verse number 10, might have life more abundantly
point_summary: Christ's promise of abundant life in John 10:10 serves as a personal starting point for gospel presentation, grounded in the Good Shepherd giving his life so that people might truly live.

context: without him we can do nothing that he is the vine and we're simply the branches
point_summary: Apart from Christ believers can accomplish nothing, as John 15:5 is briefly invoked alongside 2 Corinthians 12:9 to frame human dependence on God before the sermon's main text.

context: Jesus Christ said I am the way, the truth, and the life.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The Holy Spirit, one of the names ascribed to the Holy Spirit is Comforter.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son that whosoever believe it than him should not perish, but have everlasting life
point_summary: God's love in John 3:16 is understood only through its full cost — the precious blood of Christ — which many in the world fail to grasp when they hear the familiar words.

context: John 3, 16, for God so loved the world
point_summary: John 3:16 is cited as the scriptural foundation children instinctively grasp to prove that Jesus's love is grounded in God's Word, not merely human testimony.

context: Jesus said to the disciples often, turn back to John 13 if you will, often we are mindful of this truth in John 13 when the Lord Jesus Christ would give this new commandment
point_summary: Christ's command to love one another is grounded in His own prior love as the standard and source—"as I have loved you"—making His love both the model and the measure for believers.

context: Jesus said, by this, by our love one for another, shall all men know that ye are my disciples
point_summary: Loving one another as Christ loved is the mark by which all people will recognize his disciples, with Christ's own love as both the standard and the source.

context: A new commandment I give unto you, that ye love one another, as I have loved you, that ye also love one another.
point_summary: Christ's command to love one another is grounded in His own prior love as the standard and source — "as I have loved you" — making His love both the model and the motivation.

context: This is my commandment, that ye love one another as I have loved you.
point_summary: Christ's command in John 15:12 repeats and reinforces John 13:34, grounding the believer's call to mutual love in the prior, certain love Jesus himself has shown them.

context: I'll just read the end of verse number 1, that it says this, He loved them unto the end.
point_summary: John 13:1's closing phrase, "He loved them unto the end," is introduced as the scriptural backdrop establishing that John the apostle was personally confident Jesus loved him.

context: and we come to verse number 23.
point_summary: John's self-description as the disciple "whom Jesus loved" illustrates the kind of personal, confident assurance of Christ's love that every believer is called to possess.

context: Jesus said in verse 21, verily, verily, I say unto you that one of you shall betray me.
point_summary: John's declaration that one of the disciples would betray Him (v. 21) provides the narrative context for identifying the beloved disciple in v. 23, whom the sermon uses to establish John's confident, personal assurance of Christ's love.

context: In the Bible, look at verse 22, it says, then the disciples looked one on another, doubting of whom He spake.
point_summary: John 13:22's context of mutual suspicion among the disciples frames the contrast with verse 23, where John identifies himself as "the disciple whom Jesus loved," demonstrating his personal confidence in Christ's love.

context: And look at verse 23, now there was leaning on Jesus' bosom, one of His disciples, notice this phrase, whom Jesus loved.
point_summary: John's self-identification throughout his Gospel as "the disciple whom Jesus loved" reflects his full assurance and personal confidence that Christ's love was directed specifically toward him.

context: In chapter number 20. Think about this, in verse number 1, it was the first day of the week, and here comes Mary Magdalene early to the tomb, and of course it's after the crucifixion and burial. The Bible says that she seeth the stone taken away from the sepulcher.
point_summary: John's self-identification as "the other disciple whom Jesus loved" in the resurrection account illustrates the personal assurance of Christ's love that marked his entire apostolic witness.

context: It says in verse number 2, then she runneth and cometh to Simon Peter, and to the other disciple whom Jesus loved. The other disciple whom Jesus loved. Who is that other disciple? John.
point_summary: John's self-identification as "the other disciple whom Jesus loved" in John 20:2 illustrates that he lived with full, unshaken assurance of Christ's personal love for him.

context: he said unto them, cast the net on the right side of the ship, and ye shall find. They cast therefore, and now they were not able to draw it for the multitude of fishes.
point_summary: John's obedience to Christ's command to cast the net on the right side—resulting in an overwhelming catch—illustrates the confidence and assurance that comes from knowing the Lord is present and directing His disciples.

context: Therefore that disciple whom Jesus loved saith unto Peter, it is the Lord. Now when Simon Peter heard that it was the Lord, he girded his fisher's coat unto him for he was naked and he did cast himself into the sea.
point_summary: John's recognition of the risen Lord on the shore—"It is the Lord"—illustrates the personal assurance of Christ's love that marked the beloved disciple throughout the Gospel.

context: Jesus tells him in verse 19 to follow me
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Peter in verse number 20, it says, then Peter turning about, seeth the disciple whom Jesus loved following.
point_summary: John's self-identification as "the disciple whom Jesus loved" illustrates the personal assurance and confidence in Christ's love that the sermon holds up as a model for believers.

context: I want to go over to the book of John in the 11th chapter.
point_summary: John 11 is introduced as the biblical case study for the sermon's second point: that believers must never allow difficult circumstances to cause them to doubt God's love.

context: We see in John 11, the reality is this passage of Scripture is a really exciting passage of Scripture. It's in this passage that Jesus stands at the tomb of Lazarus and says, Lazarus, come forth!
point_summary: John 11 is introduced as the scriptural setting for the sermon's second point: that believers must never allow difficult circumstances to make them doubt God's love.

context: therefore his sisters, we know those are Mary and Martha, the sisters of Lazarus, and they are going to send to Jesus
point_summary: Even as Lazarus lay sick and dying, his sisters appealed to Jesus not on the basis of their need alone but on the basis of Jesus' own love—"he whom thou lovest is sick"—demonstrating that confident faith rests on God's declared love, not on favorable circumstances.

context: it was that Mary which anointed the Lord with ointment, wiped His feet with her hair, whose brother Lazarus was sick
point_summary: John 11:2 is cited to identify Mary and Martha as the sisters of Lazarus, establishing the relational context for why they appeal to Jesus on the basis of his love rather than merely reporting Lazarus's illness.

context: they say, Lord, behold, He whom thou lovest is sick.
point_summary: Mary and Martha's appeal—"he whom thou lovest is sick"—illustrates the confidence believers can have that Jesus loves specific individuals personally, not merely humanity in the abstract.

context: when Jesus heard that, He said, this sickness is not unto death, but for the glory of God, the Son of God might be glorified thereby.
point_summary: Jesus' declaration that Lazarus's sickness is "for the glory of God" frames the entire Lazarus narrative as a demonstration that divine love does not preclude suffering but works through it toward God's glorification.

context: Now, Jesus loved Martha and her sister.
point_summary: John 11:5's explicit naming of Martha, Mary, and Lazarus as individuals whom Jesus loved grounds the move from God's general love for the world to His personal, knowing love for each individual believer.

context: we can quote John 3.16 and we can speak about the fact that God so loved the world
point_summary: God's love in John 3:16 becomes personally meaningful only when its universal scope—"the world"—is applied to the individual, despite God's complete knowledge of that person.

context: Our friend Lazarus sleepeth; but I go, that I may awake him out of sleep.
point_summary: Jesus' use of the word "sleepeth" for Lazarus in John 11:11 illustrates how the disciples misunderstood His meaning, requiring Him to speak plainly that Lazarus was dead.

context: Then said his disciples, Lord, if he sleep, he shall do well.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Howbeit Jesus spake of his death: but they thought that he had spoken of taking of rest in sleep.
point_summary: The disciples' misunderstanding of Jesus's sleep metaphor for death (v. 13) sets up His plain declaration in v. 14, illustrating that His deliberate delay served a purposeful plan to deepen their belief.

context: Then said Jesus unto them plainly, Lazarus is dead.
point_summary: Jesus speaks plainly to His disciples—"Lazarus is dead"—cutting through their misunderstanding to set the stage for a miracle designed to deepen their belief.

context: And I am glad for your sakes that I was not there, to the intent ye may believe; nevertheless let us go unto him.
point_summary: Jesus' deliberate absence from Lazarus's deathbed, though an expression of love, served the purpose of deepening the disciples' faith—a truth the sermon acknowledges but sets aside to focus on love.

context: when Mary was come where Jesus was and saw Him, she fell down on His feet saying unto Him, Lord, if Thou hadst been here, my brother had not died.
point_summary: Even when circumstances tempt believers to doubt God's love, Mary's grief-stricken words at Jesus' feet—echoing Martha's—and Jesus' own weeping confirm that His delay was not indifference but purposeful, love-driven development.

context: When Jesus therefore saw her weeping and the Jews also weeping which came with her, He groaned in the Spirit and was troubled.
point_summary: Seeing Jesus groan in spirit and be troubled at the tomb of Lazarus demonstrates that His delay was never indifference, but love actively at work amid human grief.

context: And the Bible says in v. 34 that Jesus said, where have you laid Him? And they said unto Him, Lord, come and see.
point_summary: Jesus's question "Where have you laid him?" leads directly into his weeping, which the mourners themselves interpreted as proof of his love for Lazarus.

context: And then the Bible says here in v. 35, Jesus wept.
point_summary: Even amid divine purpose and delay, Jesus wept at Lazarus's tomb—demonstrating that His love for those He is developing is real, not absent.

context: Jesus wept. And then the Bible says this in v. 36, Then said the Jews, these mourners that were there, Behold, how He loved Him.
point_summary: Jesus's weeping at Lazarus's tomb is presented as visible, undeniable proof of His love—so certain that even the mourning Jews declared, "Behold, how He loved Him."

context: Could not this man, speaking of Jesus, which opened the eyes of the blind, and you would look back in John 9 and see that great miracle.
point_summary: John 9 is referenced only as background evidence that Jesus had power to heal the blind, cited by the mourning Jews to argue He could have prevented Lazarus's death.

context: But remember what He said back in the 15th verse, I'm glad for your sakes that I was not there to the intent you may believe.
point_summary: Jesus' deliberate absence from Lazarus's death, explained in v. 15 as being "for your sakes," is presented as purposeful love designed to produce belief through resurrection rather than mere healing.

context: Jesus will stand at that tomb. And He says, take ye away the stone. In v. 39. Of course, there's an objection. He hath been dead for four days and surely by now he stinketh.
point_summary: The objection at the tomb—that Lazarus had been dead four days—frames Jesus' command to roll away the stone as a deliberate test of faith before the miracle of resurrection that would produce belief.

context: In v. 43, it says, when He thus had spoken, He cried with a loud voice, Lazarus, come forth.
point_summary: Christ's deliberate delay and then public command — "Lazarus, come forth" — is presented as the demonstration of love that moved witnesses from acknowledging Jesus' power to full belief in Him.

context: And the Bible says, he that was dead came forth, bound hand and foot with grave clothes. And his face was bound about with a napkin. Jesus saith unto them, loose him and let him go.
point_summary: The resurrection of Lazarus is presented as the culminating demonstration of Christ's love, designed by Jesus to produce belief in those who witnessed it.

context: And I want you to notice in v. 45, the Bible begins with this word, then. Then. Then. Many of the Jews which came to Mary and had seen the things which Jesus did, what's it say? Believed on Him.
point_summary: The resurrection of Lazarus produced saving faith — "then" many Jews who witnessed it believed on Jesus, demonstrating that Christ's miraculous works are designed to move observers from partial acknowledgment to full belief.

context: Remember, the Bible said in v. 4 that this sickness is not unto death but for the glory of God that the Son of God might be glorified thereby.
point_summary: Lazarus's sickness and death were not divine failure but divine design—Jesus himself declared the entire episode was purposed for the glory of God and the glorification of the Son.

context: the Bible says that the disciples asked Him, saying, Master, who did sin? This man or his parents who was born blind?
point_summary: Suffering and divine delay, as in both the raising of Lazarus and the healing of the blind man, serve not as evidence against God's love but as occasions for His glory to be manifested.

context: And Jesus said neither hath this man sinned nor his parents, but that the works of God should be made manifest in him so that God would be glorified.

point_summary: The universal scope of Christ's atoning love—"the whole world" and "whosoever"—is cited as direct scriptural refutation of the Calvinist doctrine of limited atonement.

point_summary: John 3:16's "whosoever" and "world" are cited as direct scriptural evidence against limited atonement, confirming that Christ's propitiatory death was made available to all mankind without exception.

context: I am the good shepherd: the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep.
point_summary: Christ as the Good Shepherd is the basis for divine protection, with John 10:11–14 invoked to show that the shepherd actively guards his sheep, often from dangers they never perceive.

context: But he that is an hireling, and not the shepherd, whose own the sheep are not, seeth the wolf coming, and leaveth the sheep, and fleeth: and the wolf catcheth them, and scattereth the sheep.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The hireling fleeth, because he is an hireling, and careth not for the sheep.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I am the good shepherd, and know my sheep, and am known of mine.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Look at John 10 and verse number 11. The Bible says this, I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep, but he that is an hireling and not the shepherd, whose own the sheep are not, seeth the wolf coming, and what does he do? He leaves the sheep.
point_summary: Christ's identity as the Good Shepherd is contrasted with the hireling who flees danger, establishing that genuine care and self-giving protection—not abandonment—define the shepherd's relationship to his sheep.

context: But he that is an hireling, and not the shepherd, whose own the sheep are not, seeth the wolf coming, and leaveth the sheep, and fleeth: and the wolf catcheth them, and scattereth the sheep.
point_summary: Unlike the hireling of John 10:12 who flees at the wolf's approach, the Good Shepherd stays to protect his sheep—even shielding them from dangers, and from themselves, they never perceive.

context: The hireling fleeth, because he is an hireling, and careth not for the sheep.
point_summary: Unlike the hireling who flees danger because he has no true care for the sheep, the Good Shepherd stays and protects—even shielding his sheep from themselves.

context: I am the good shepherd, and know my sheep, and am known of mine.
point_summary: The Good Shepherd's intimate knowledge of His sheep, contrasted with the hireling's indifference, grounds the claim that Christ actively protects His own rather than fleeing when danger comes.

context: And then other times you read Peter and you kind of scratch your head and say, what was he thinking? What was he doing? I mean, it was great when Peter said, Lord, if it's thee, bid me come to thee. And he walked on the water, what great faith! But then he says to the other disciples in John 20, he says, I'm going fishing. You say, what's going on, Peter?
point_summary: Peter's declaration "I'm going fishing" in John 20 is cited as an example of impulsive, directionless action that contrasts with Christ's ongoing intercessory prayer on behalf of his stumbling disciples.

context: John 14, Jesus had just spent time with the disciples
point_summary: Christ's intercession and eternal preparation of a heavenly dwelling are presented as enduring, spiritual blessings that surpass temporal prosperity and cause the believer's cup to overflow.

reference_text: the 13th chapter, now is the Son of Man glorified
context: he says at the end of the chapter, the 13th chapter, now is the Son of Man glorified
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in me.
point_summary: Jesus's command "let not your heart be troubled" is presented as a direct, personal word to disciples—and to believers today—grounded in his promise to prepare a heavenly place for them.

context: In my father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.
point_summary: Jesus' promise in John 14:2 that he goes to prepare a place for his people is presented as the shepherd's ongoing heavenly work on behalf of those whose cup already overflows with earthly blessing.

context: In the same passage of scripture in John 14, we see in verse 2, he says, I go to prepare a place for you.
point_summary: Christ's promised return in John 14:3 grounds the believer's present endurance, assuring that the same Shepherd preparing heaven's place will personally come back to receive his own.

context: But he says this in verse number 3, and if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again.
point_summary: Christ's personal promise to return and receive believers unto himself means that no circumstance, however terrible, can diminish the overflowing cup of one who knows his Shepherd is coming back for him.

context: Notice this, we've seen it not only in John 14
point_summary: Christ's promise "I will come again" in John 14 is presented as both a comforting and motivating truth that drove first-century disciples to fill Jerusalem with doctrine and spread the gospel through the known world.

context: I will come again to receive you unto myself that where I am there you may be also
point_summary: Christ's personal return—"I will come again"—is presented as both a comforting and motivating promise, with the Lord himself coming rather than sending another in his place.

context: The Lord himself, he said I will come again.
point_summary: Christ's personal return — "I will come again" — is corroborated by 1 Thessalonians 4:16's declaration that "the Lord himself shall descend," emphasizing that Jesus comes for his own rather than sending a representative.

context: as Jesus said in John without him I can do nothing
point_summary: Fruitfulness in ministry belongs entirely to God, such that any ability or gifting a believer exercises must be credited to Him alone, not to the individual.

context: if thou knewest the gift of God

context: Jesus says this. Let me back up to verse 31 and catch it in context
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.
