context: if God be glorified in Him. but God shall also glorify Him in Himself and shall straightway glorify Him.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 'Little children, yet a little while I am with you. Ye shall seek Me, and as I said unto the Jews, whither I go ye cannot come.
point_summary: Jesus's "new commandment" in John 13:33–35 is cited as the scriptural foundation for the love among believers that the Colossian church was visibly displaying toward all the saints.

context: a new commandment I give unto you, that ye love one another, as I have loved you, that ye also love one another.
point_summary: Love among believers is the mark of true discipleship, as Jesus commanded in John 13:34, and the Colossians' love for all the saints visibly confirmed they were living out that command.

context: By this shall all men know that ye are My disciples, if ye have love one to another.
point_summary: Love for fellow believers is the mark Jesus himself designated as the distinguishing evidence of true discipleship, which the Colossians' love "to all the saints" visibly fulfilled.

context: Of loving one another as Jesus commanded in John 13.
point_summary: Mutual love among believers is the distinguishing mark of Christ's disciples, by which all men recognize genuine discipleship.

context: I hope that we're following the instruction of Christ in John 13.
point_summary: Love for all the saints—not selective or personality-driven—is the mark Christ commanded in John 13, by which observers should recognize genuine Christian community.

context: Jesus is not a way to heaven, he is the way to heaven.
point_summary: Jesus is not merely one path among many but the exclusive way to heaven, making correct knowledge of his identity a matter of eternal consequence.

context: Can I tell you this morning, the truth of John 15, when he stood with his disciples and said to them, I am the vine, ye are the branches. And he said to them, without me, ye can do nothing.
point_summary: Apart from human strength, Christ's words "without me ye can do nothing" ground the enabling for ministry entirely in divine grace, not personal power.

context: he came to his own, but his own received him not
point_summary: The rejection in John 1:11 illustrates that the lost must first be confronted with the gospel before they can make the decision to receive or reject Christ.

context: but as many as received him, to them gave he power
point_summary: John 1:12 is cited to show that receiving Christ requires a conscious decision, and that the lost cannot make that decision if no one brings them the gospel.

context: In the reality in John chapter number one, as you read verses 11 and 12, it says, 11 and 12, you find that they had an opportunity, there's a decision that has to be made.
point_summary: John 1:11–12 is read as presenting two possible responses to Christ—rejection or reception—to show that the lost cannot make that decision if they have never been confronted with the gospel.

context: In the reality in John chapter number one, as you read verses 11 and 12, it says, 11 and 12, you find that they had an opportunity, there's a decision that has to be made.
point_summary: Receiving Christ requires a decision, and John 1:12 shows that many lost people never make that decision simply because no one has confronted them with the gospel.

context: John 15 plainly tells us that when we bear fruit, we bring glory to his name.
point_summary: Bearing fruit, according to John 15, is the God-ordained means by which believers left on earth bring glory to His name.

context: how can we trust John 3.16? Oh, but we want to trust John 3.16, because for God so loved the world
point_summary: John 3:16's trustworthiness depends on Genesis's reliability, since a redeemer presupposes a creator, and undermining one collapses confidence in the other.

context: Jesus, who is the way, the truth and the life
point_summary: Against the relativism that says "your truth" and "my truth," Jesus himself—the way, the truth, and the life—stands as the embodiment of absolute truth, which Pilate tragically walked away from without receiving.

context: Pilate says what is truth
point_summary: Pilate's question "What is truth?" illustrates the world's rejection of absolute truth, contrasted with Jesus' own declaration that God's Word is truth and stands on its own.

context: sanctify them by thy word. Thy word is truth
point_summary: Sanctify them through thy truth: thy word is truth (John 17:17) is cited to establish that Scripture is absolute, transforming truth—not relative—and that it is the standard by which believers know and live.

context: to as many as as we've believed on the Lord Jesus Christ as many as received him to them gave he power to become the sons of God according To John 1 in verse number 12
point_summary: Receiving Christ grants the believer sonship, giving him the relational standing to address God as Father in prayer.

context: I pray not that thou shouldest take them out of the world, but that thou shouldest keep them from the evil.
point_summary: Jesus' prayer in John 17:15 is cited to establish that believers are called to be kept from evil while remaining in the world, not removed from it.

context: They are not of the world, even as I am not of the world.
point_summary: Believers are in the world but not of it—a distinction drawn from John 17:16 to ground the call to separate from worldly influence without physical withdrawal.

context: Sanctify them through thy Word. Thy Word is truth.
point_summary: Sanctification through God's Word (John 17:17) grounds the call to be "in but not of" the world, reinforcing Psalm 1's portrait of the blessed man who separates from worldly influence.

context: I read already in John 17, he talked about the fact that he's praying not that we'd be delivered from the world, that we're in the world, but we're not of the world.
point_summary: Sanctification from worldly influence comes not by physical removal from the world but by immersion in God's Word, which Jesus himself identified as truth in his high-priestly prayer.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth, and he said, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father, but by me.
point_summary: Exclusive salvation through Christ alone—"I am the way, the truth, and the life; no man cometh unto the Father, but by me"—was the doctrinal anchor the man already knew yet had drifted from through outside influences.

context: For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son
point_summary: God's love for the Son—declared at Jesus's baptism in Matthew, Mark, and Luke—is the same love expressed in John 3:16, making the Father's willingness to give that beloved Son the measure of His love for mankind.

context: He was able to say, it is finished.
point_summary: Christ's declaration "It is finished" on the cross signifies the complete, willing payment for mankind's sin, accomplished only through Calvary and no other means.

context: It's amazing love that God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son. Why? That whosoever believeth in him should not perish but have everlasting life.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus Christ himself said, I am the way, the truth and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus Christ's own declaration — "I am the way, the truth, and the life; no man cometh unto the Father but by me" — is cited as the exclusive ground of salvation that the next generation must receive through the gospel.

context: And Jesus said, without me, you can do nothing. In John 15.
point_summary: John 15's "without me you can do nothing" is invoked to explain why God reduced Gideon's army—ensuring the victory could have no human explanation and no room for self-glorying.

context: John 15, we find that Jesus teaches the disciples, and of course for us to have the understanding of that teaching, that without Him we can do nothing.
point_summary: Human inability apart from God is the ground for total dependence on Him, as John 15:5's "without Him we can do nothing" is invoked to reinforce Jehoshaphat's declaration that his eyes are upon God alone.

context: a familiar verse of Scripture, but once again, my attention was focused upon that great declaration of John the Baptist in John
point_summary: God alone receives glory when believers, like Jehoshaphat, fix their eyes on Him rather than their own solutions—illustrated by John the Baptist's declaration that He must increase and I must decrease.

context: he must increase and I must decrease
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration that "he must increase and I must decrease" is cited as the theological ground for abandoning self-reliance and redirecting all attention and glory to God alone.

context: But Jesus said this, if the world hates you, just remember that they hated me first. If you read through the Gospel of John, you find that God is preparing His servants. Look over, if you will, at John 15, and notice what the Bible says as you begin to look through this portion of scripture and begin to understand as the Lord Jesus Christ is trying to prepare His children for their service to Him. Listen, if the world loves us, there's probably something wrong. Honestly. Look at John 15 and verse 18. If the world hates you, you know that it hated
point_summary: Hatred from the world toward God's people is expected and normal, as Christ himself was hated first—making worldly rejection a mark of faithful standing for truth.

context: me before it hated you. If ye were of the world, the world would love his own.
point_summary: The world's hatred of believers is not incidental but expected, because Christ himself was hated first, and faithfulness to truth will cost popularity with the world.

context: Remember the word that I said unto you, the servant is not greater than his Lord. If they have persecuted me, they will also persecute you.
point_summary: Servants who stand for truth should expect the world's hatred, because Christ himself warned that persecution follows his name—the servant is not greater than his Lord.

context: He is the way, the truth, and the life. And he said, no man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Access into God's presence is possible not by human merit but exclusively through Christ, who declared himself the way, the truth, and the life, and who opened the path to the Father.

context: Jesus said, Before Abraham was, I am.
point_summary: Jesus's declaration "Before Abraham was, I am" is cited as confirmation that the divine name revealed to Moses—the self-existent, eternal I AM—belongs to Christ himself.

reference_text: Without me you can do nothing
context: Without me you can do nothing is what Jesus said. The danger comes in the life of a believer when we think, I got this, I can handle this.
point_summary: Human inadequacy before God is the very condition that makes dependence possible, as John 15:5's "without me ye can do nothing" is invoked to warn against self-sufficiency and commend total reliance on God's strength.

reference_text: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by Me
context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by Me.
point_summary: Salvation comes exclusively through Jesus Christ — not through human effort or moral improvement — because he alone is the way, the truth, and the life.

context: where Jesus taught in John 13, a new commandment, 'Give I unto you that you love one another 'as I have loved you.
point_summary: Paul's tearful letter to Corinth is grounded in Christ's command in John 13:34, which established love for one another as the defining mark of discipleship that Paul carried into his own ministry relationships.

context: the Bible tells us in verse number 19 of John chapter number 20 that the same day evening, being the first day of the week when the doors were shut, where the disciples were assembled for fear of the Jews, came Jesus and stood in the midst and saith unto them, Peace be unto you.
point_summary: John 20:19 is cited as narrative context establishing that the risen Christ appeared to the assembled disciples on resurrection day, setting the scene from which Thomas's absence and subsequent doubt arise.

context: Thomas was not there, according to verse number 24, the Bible says, but Thomas, one of the 12, called Didymus, was not with them when Jesus came.
point_summary: Thomas's absence from the disciples' first post-resurrection gathering, noted in passing as context for his subsequent doubt and eventual confession, sets the scene for verses 28–29.

context: And after eight days again, His disciples were within, and Thomas with them, then came Jesus, the doors being shut, and stood in the midst and said, Peace be unto you.
point_summary: Eight days after the first resurrection appearance, Jesus returned to the assembled disciples—this time with Thomas present—passing through shut doors to confront Thomas's unbelief directly.

context: Then saith He to Thomas, Reach thither thy finger, and behold My hands, and reach hither thy hand, and thrust it into My side, and be not faithless, but believing.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And Thomas answered and said unto Him, My Lord and my God.
point_summary: Thomas's confession "My Lord and my God" is presented as the definitive answer to the question of who Jesus is, serving as the sermon's launching point for establishing Christ's identity.

context: Jesus saith unto him, Thomas, because thou hast seen Me, thou hast believed. Blessed are they that have not seen, and yet have believed.
point_summary: Blessed are those who believe without physical sight — a beatitude Jesus pronounces over Thomas's confession that frames the sermon's exploration of who Jesus is to the believer.

context: Jesus says, because thou hast seen me, thou hast believed. And then think of this, the latter part of verse number 29. Blessed are they that have not seen and yet have believed.
point_summary: Believers today, who have not seen Christ yet trust the scriptural record of Him, stand within the blessing Jesus pronounced on all who believe without sight.

context: In the beginning was the word, and the word was with God, and the word was God.

context: The same was in the beginning with God.
point_summary: John 1:2 is cited alongside John 1:1 to establish that the Word—identified as Jesus—was eternally with God before the Incarnation, grounding the claim that Jesus is God in the flesh.

context: All things were made by him, and without him was not anything made that was made.
point_summary: John 1:3's declaration that all things were made by Christ establishes His identity as God in the flesh — the foundational reason, in this sermon's first point, why Jesus is all the world to the believer.

context: the word was made flesh and dwelt among us. And we beheld his glory. The glory is of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.
point_summary: The eternal Word who was God took on human flesh — the Incarnation establishing that Jesus is God in the flesh, the first reason he is all the world to the believer.

context: in John chapter 4 verses 6 & 7 the Bible tells us that he was thirsty
point_summary: John 4:6's account of Jesus at the well is cited as evidence of Christ's genuine humanity, specifically that he experienced thirst as a real man who took on human likeness to die as an acceptable sacrifice for redemption.

context: in John chapter 4 verses 6 & 7 the Bible tells us that he was thirsty
point_summary: John 4:7's account of Jesus asking the Samaritan woman for water is cited as evidence of Christ's genuine humanity—specifically that he experienced thirst as a real man.

context: my mind races to the one that's probably the most familiar to us in John the the Gospel of John in chapter 3 11 you remember that Lazarus was sick and we look at the passage of Scripture we find that he Jesus was made aware of the sickness of Lazarus
point_summary: The raising of Lazarus in John 11 is presented as evidence of Christ's deity, with his deliberate delay explained by his own words that the disciples might believe and grow in faith.

context: after Jesus wept these Jews say it's recorded in verse number 37 they say amongst themselves could not this man which opened the eyes of the blind have caused that even this man should not have died
point_summary: The crowd's reference to Jesus healing the blind man (John 9) reveals that even skeptical onlookers recognized His miraculous power, yet that recognition fell short of understanding He intended not to heal Lazarus but to raise him from the dead.

context: the miracle that had taken place back in John chapter number nine with regard to the healing of the man that was born blind
point_summary: The healing of the man born blind in John 9 is referenced as a miracle so well-known that bystanders at Lazarus's tomb invoked it as evidence of Jesus's power over death.

context: take away the stone in verse number 39
point_summary: Martha's objection at the tomb — "he stinketh" — is cited to underscore the undeniable, four-day-old death that made Christ's subsequent raising of Lazarus a demonstration of divine omnipotence.

context: Jesus will proclaim in verse number 43 with a loud voice Lazarus come forth
point_summary: Jesus's command "Lazarus, come forth" over a four-days-dead man is presented as a demonstration of Christ's omnipotence, establishing why he is worthy of total devotion.

context: he that was dead came forth bound hand and foot with grave clothes
point_summary: Lazarus's resurrection after four days—despite objection and physical decay—demonstrates Christ's omnipotent deity, confirming that Jesus is God in flesh with all power over death.

context: we mentioned John chapter 9 with the man that was born blind
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: you think about John chapter number 5 I've often looked at John chapter number 5 and in reference that portion of Scripture is when the impotent man meets the omnipotent God
point_summary: John 5's healing of the lame man illustrates Christ's power over disease, framed as the impotent man encountering the omnipotent God after 38 years of infirmity.

context: Jesus says in verse number 6 wilt thou be made whole
point_summary: Christ's question to the man lame for thirty-eight years — "Wilt thou be made whole?" — illustrates His power over disease and His initiative toward one who had no human help or expectation of healing.

context: the impotent man answered him sir I have no man when the water is troubled to put me into the pool
point_summary: John 5:7 illustrates the impotent man's utter helplessness after 38 years of infirmity, contrasted with Christ's omnipotent command that immediately made him whole.

context: Jesus saith unto him, rise, take up thy bed, and walk
point_summary: John 5:8 is cited as a demonstration of Christ's power over disease, illustrated by the healing of a man who had been lame for thirty-eight years and was instantly made whole at Jesus' command.

context: demonstrated over demons, over disease, over death. We mentioned it already in John chapter 11, and you see his ability to raise them from the dead.
point_summary: John 11 is cited as evidence of Christ's power over death, illustrating that he not only raised the dead but conquered sin, death, and the grave entirely.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus is the exclusive way of salvation — the only name under heaven by which men must be saved — as affirmed by both Christ's own words and Peter's declaration in Acts 4:12.

context: In John chapter number 10, and we'll actually look at some of those verses this morning, the Bible teaches us in that portion of scripture
point_summary: John 10's portrait of Jesus as the good shepherd who lays down his life and gives eternal life to his sheep grounds the claim that the Lord can only be *your* shepherd through personal saving faith.

context: that Jesus is the good shepherd. And the good shepherd layeth down his life for the sheep.
point_summary: Jesus as the good shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep establishes the personal saving relationship that makes it possible to call the Lord "my shepherd."

context: And he says this in verse number 27, as you go through the end of that chapter, he says this, my sheep hear my voice and I know them and I give unto them eternal life and they shall never perish.
point_summary: Jesus's words "my sheep hear my voice" are set alongside David's "the Lord is my shepherd" to show that claiming Christ as one's personal shepherd requires accepting him as Lord and Savior.

context: But notice in John chapter number three, and I want you to read it this morning. For so many of us this morning, we could quote John 3.16. It's a very familiar verse of scripture.
point_summary: God's declared love for every believer in John 3:16 is presented as sufficient reason alone for the child of God to say "my cup runneth over," regardless of trial or hardship.

context: The Bible says this in verse number 16. For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son that whosoever believeth in him shall not die. who believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life.
point_summary: God's declaration in John 3:16 that he loved the whole world—including the unlovable—grounds the sermon's first reason why the believer's cup overflows.

context: Notice verse 17, For God sent not his Son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world through him might be saved.
point_summary: God's purpose in sending his Son was not condemnation but salvation, so that believers who were already condemned stand condemned no more.

context: For God so loved the world.
point_summary: God's love for the world—declared in John 3:16 and demonstrated in the gift of his Son—is the foundational reason the believer's cup runs over, even for those who feel unlovable.

context: Jesus begins in verse number one of John 10, and he says, verily, verily, this is of a truth of a truth. God's word is truth. Jesus Christ himself is truth. And he says this, I say unto you, he that entereth not by the door into the sheepfold, but climbeth up some other way, the same as a thief and a robber, but he that entereth in by the door is the shepherd of the sheep.
point_summary: Jesus' declaration in John 10:1 introduces the sheepfold imagery that the sermon connects directly to Psalm 23, establishing that Christ is both the door and the shepherd who died for his sheep.

context: I am the door of the sheep.
point_summary: Jesus as the door of the sheep is expounded as the exclusive means of salvation and abundant life, contrasting the thief's destruction with the Good Shepherd who dies for his own.

context: All that ever came before me are thieves and robbers, but the sheep did not hear them.
point_summary: Christ's identity as the true shepherd is contrasted with all prior false claimants—thieves and robbers whose voices the sheep rightly refused—establishing his exclusive authority as the door.

context: I am the door. By me, if any man enter in, he shall be saved and shall go in and out and find pasture.
point_summary: Christ as the door guarantees salvation and abundant life for all who enter through him, grounding the overflowing cup of Psalm 23 in the shepherd's atoning death on behalf of his sheep.

context: The thief cometh not but for to steal and to kill and to destroy. I am come that they might have life and that they might have it more abundantly.
point_summary: Christ's self-identification as the door who gives abundant life stands in direct contrast to the thief who comes only to steal, kill, and destroy — a contrast that grounds the overflowing cup of Psalm 23:5 in the shepherd's atoning death.

context: I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep.
point_summary: Christ's identity as the Good Shepherd is the ground of the overflowing cup: because he gave his life for the sheep, the believer's cup runs over.

context: 'And the Father knoweth me, even so know I the Father, 'and I lay down my life for the sheep.'
point_summary: Christ's voluntary self-sacrifice—laying down his life with power to take it again—is presented as the ground for the believer's overflowing gratitude expressed in "my cup runneth over."

context: 'And other sheep I have, 'which are not of this fold, them also I must bring, 'and they shall hear my voice, 'and there shall be one fold and one shepherd.
point_summary: Christ's voluntary death for "other sheep" beyond the immediate fold demonstrates that the Good Shepherd willingly laid down his life for all who would be redeemed, not merely a select few.

context: 'Therefore doth my Father love me, why? 'Because I lay down my life, then I might take it again.'
point_summary: Christ's voluntary surrender of his life — not its seizure by men — is the ground of the Father's love for him and the proof that the cross was a willing act of redemption on behalf of his sheep.

context: Notice the powerful truth in verse 18. 'No man taketh it from me.' He said, but then the Romans arrest him and no man taketh it, he lays it down. He went willingly to that cross. He suffered all that he did willingly so that you and I could be redeemed. Notice this is in verse 18, 'But I lay it down on myself. 'I have power to lay it down 'and I have power to take it again. 'This commandment have I received of my Father.'
point_summary: Christ's death on the cross was entirely voluntary—no man took His life, but He laid it down of His own power, willingly dying in the place of guilty sinners so they could be redeemed.

context: But remember, Jesus said it is finished
point_summary: Christ's voluntary surrender of his life on the cross — "It is finished," then giving up the ghost — demonstrates that no man took his life; he laid it down willingly for our redemption.

context: I must needs go through Samaria
point_summary: Jesus' deliberate passage through Samaria in John 4 illustrates that the Son of Man actively seeks out specific lost sinners who need to hear the gospel.

context: I've come to give them a life that they might have it more abundantly
point_summary: Christ as sustainer is grounded in his own promise in John 10:10 to give life more abundantly, distinguishing him as not merely capable of sustaining but actively committed to it.

context: The Bible says, for God so loved the world. Aren't you thankful for the love of God? For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in Him should not perish, but have everlasting life.
point_summary: God's love in giving His Son (John 3:16–18) is set against humanity's prior condition of condemnation, establishing the need from which the Gospel rescues sinners.

context: Notice verse 17, for God sent not His Son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world through Him might be saved.
point_summary: Christ's mission was not condemnation but salvation, yet John 3:17–18 confirms that unbelievers are already condemned apart from Him—the very condition Paul addresses in Colossians 2.

context: The one that has no sin casts the first stone.
point_summary: The guilt recorded against sinners in the "handwriting of ordinances" is illustrated by Christ's writing in the sand, implying He exposed the accusers' own sins before declaring only the sinless may cast the first stone.

context: how that Jesus said it is finished. It is finished.
point_summary: Christ's "It is finished" (John 19:30) is interpreted through the image of a paid-in-full promissory note, signifying that the handwriting of ordinances against us has been completely and finally cancelled.

context: ...how can you know that John 316 can be trusted...
point_summary: The trustworthiness of John 3:16 stands or falls with the trustworthiness of Genesis 1–11, since undermining the foundation of Scripture is the strategy used to erode confidence in the gospel itself.

context: in the beginning was the Word. And the Word was with God and the Word was God.
point_summary: John 1:1 is cited to establish that Jesus is fully and eternally God—not "a God" as some translations render it—grounding the incarnation of Galatians 4:4 in his pre-existent, uncreated deity.

context: The same was in the beginning with God.
point_summary: John 1:2's affirmation that the Word "was in the beginning with God" grounds the claim that Christ's eternal deity preceded creation and that the incarnation was purposed before the foundation of the world.

context: all things were made by Him and without Him was not anything made that was made.
point_summary: John 1:3's declaration that all things were made by Christ establishes his eternal deity, grounding the incarnation in Galatians 4:4 as the entry of the eternal Creator into his own creation.

context: In Him was life and the life was the light of men.
point_summary: Christ's eternal nature as the life-giving Word, established before creation, is what made the Incarnation—God sending forth His Son in the fullness of time—possible and purposeful.

context: in the beginning was the Word and the Word was with God and the Word was God
point_summary: John 1:1 is cited to establish that Jesus is fully and unqualifiedly God—not "a God" as some modern translations render it—and eternally preexistent before His incarnation.

context: The Word was made flesh, notice, and dwelt among us. And we beheld His glory
point_summary: The eternal Word who was God from the beginning took on human flesh and dwelt among men — connecting John 1:14 directly to the Galatians 4:4 declaration that God sent forth His Son in the fullness of time.

context: John bear witness of Him
point_summary: John the Baptist's testimony in verse 15 confirms Christ's eternal preexistence: though Jesus came after John chronologically, John declares Him to have existed before him.

context: behold the Lamb of God which taketh away the sin of the world
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration "Behold the Lamb of God" is cited to establish that the eternal Son who came after John in time was before him in eternal existence.

context: John bear witness of Him and cried saying this was He of whom I spake, He that cometh after me is preferred before me
point_summary: John the Baptist's testimony that Christ "cometh after me" yet "was before me" is used to establish Christ's eternal pre-existence, making His incarnation a miracle of the eternal entering time.

context: Jesus is praying, this is right before He goes to the cross
point_summary: Christ's eternal pre-existence before creation is affirmed by His own prayer requesting restoration of the glory He shared with the Father before the world was.

context: and He says this in verse number 5, and now O Father, glorify Thou me with Thine own self, with the glory which I had with Thee before the world was.
point_summary: Jesus's prayer in John 17:5, claiming pre-creation glory shared with the Father, is cited as proof of His eternal deity—the very basis for the incarnation being miraculous.

context: for John 3 16 says for God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son that whosoever believeth in him should not perish but have everlasting life
point_summary: God's sending of his Son to redeem sinners, as declared in Galatians 4:4–5, is motivated by love — the very love John 3:16 identifies as the reason the Father gave his only begotten Son.

context: The Bible says it this way in John one in verse number 12, but as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name.
point_summary: Sonship with God is received, not earned — John 1:12 is cited to show that becoming a son of God comes only through receiving and believing on Christ, not through any human effort or labor.

context: Jesus said this, I am the way, the truth, and the life. It says this, no man cometh unto the father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus' own declaration — "I am the way, the truth, and the life; no man cometh unto the Father but by me" — is cited as authoritative proof that there is no alternative path to heaven.

context: But as many as received Him, John 1 and verse number 12, to them gave He power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on His name.
point_summary: Receiving Christ is not merely intellectual assent but involves repentance and turning to God, after which He grants the power to become sons of God and heirs through Christ.

context: I think about the situation in John 4 when Jesus said, I must go through.
point_summary: The Samaritan woman's testimony in John 4 is invoked as a parallel example of how a report about Jesus draws a crowd back to encounter Him.

context: And there's a great host of people that come back to where Jesus is in John 4. The Bible tells us in that passage of Scripture that there were some that believed because of the testimony of the woman. But the Bible tells us that there were a great number of people, many more that believed because of His own Word. He spent two days teaching those people that came back in John 4.
point_summary: The woman of Samaria's testimony drew a crowd to Jesus, illustrating how personal witness brings others to encounter Christ, whose own Word then produces deeper and more widespread belief.

context: These words spake Jesus and lifted up His eyes to heaven and said, Father, the hour has come. Glorify Thy Son that Thy Son also may glorify Thee.
point_summary: In John 17:1, Jesus' prayer "Father, the hour has come" is identified as the true Lord's Prayer—his high-priestly intercession offered on the eve of the cross, distinct from the model prayer of Matthew 6.

context: And now come I to Thee and these things I speak in the world that they might have my joy fulfilled in themselves.
point_summary: Christ's intercessory prayer in John 17 grounds the believer's mission in the world: left here rather than taken out, disciples are sent as Christ was sent, kept from evil and sanctified through truth.

context: I pray not that Thou shouldest take them out of the world, but that Thou shouldest keep them from the evil.
point_summary: Christ's prayer that believers not be removed from the world but kept from evil establishes that the church is intentionally left here as those sent, just as He was sent.

context: They are not of the world even as I am not of the world.
point_summary: Believers are left in the world—not taken out of it—because Christ has sent them into it, just as the Father sent Him.

context: Sanctify them through Thy truth. Thy Word is truth.
point_summary: Sanctification through God's Word is the ground on which Christ, in His high-priestly prayer, sends believers into the world rather than removing them from it.

context: As Thou hast sent me into the world, even so have I also sent them into the world.
point_summary: Just as the Father sent the Son into the world, Christ sends His disciples into the world—a parallel mission grounding the church's evangelistic purpose.

context: And for their sakes I sanctify myself and they also might be sanctified through the truth.
point_summary: Christ's sanctifying of Himself for His disciples' sake grounds the church's mission: as the Father sent the Son into the world, so the Son sends His people into the world.

context: Neither pray I for these alone, but for them also which shall believe on me through their word,
point_summary: Christ's intercessory prayer in John 17 extends beyond the disciples to all future believers, grounding the church's missionary sending in Jesus's own prayer before the cross.

context: that they all may be one as Thou, Father, art in me and I in Thee, that they also may be one in us, that the world may believe that Thou hast sent me.
point_summary: The unity of believers—mirroring the oneness of the Father and Son—is the purpose for which Christ prays, so that the world may believe He was sent by God.

context: in the glory which Thou gavest me, I have given them that they may be one even as we are one,
point_summary: The glory Christ received from the Father has been given to believers so that their unity might serve as the world's witness that the Father sent the Son.

context: I in them and Thou in me, that they may be made perfect in one, that the world may know that Thou hast sent me and hast loved them as Thou hast loved me.
point_summary: The unity Christ prays for in John 17:23—believers made perfect in one—is connected to the world knowing that the Father sent Him, framing the church's oneness as missional witness.

context: Father, I will that they also whom Thou hast given me be with me where I am,
point_summary: Christ's intercessory prayer expresses his will that believers be with him to behold his glory, grounding the church's mission in the assurance of that eternal destination.

context: that they may behold my glory which Thou hast given me for Thou lovest me before the foundation of the world.
point_summary: Christ's prayer that his own would be with him and behold his glory grounds the church's outward mission in the assurance that believers are given to him and sent by him into the world.

context: Oh, righteous Father, the world hath not known Thee, but I have known Thee and these have known that Thou hast sent me
point_summary: The world's ignorance of the Father stands in contrast to the disciples' knowledge that the Father sent the Son, grounding their commission to go and declare His name to the ends of the earth.

context: and I have declared unto them Thy name and will declare it that the love wherewith Thou hast loved me may be in them and I in them.
point_summary: Christ's declaration that he has declared the Father's name—and will continue to declare it—grounds the church's outward mission in the same sending pattern by which the Son was sent into the world.

context: Jesus said of John the Baptist, that he was a bright and shining light.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: last week Of course, we looked at that passage in John chapter number four when the Lord Jesus Christ went through Samaria
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: herein is my Father glorified that ye bear much fruit.
point_summary: Bearing much fruit—fulfilling the Great Commission—is the means by which the Father is glorified, making gospel obedience the driving motive for going forward.

context: Then there's the prayer of Jesus, the prayer of Christ in John 17.
point_summary: Prayer modeled by Christ himself—who as fully God and fully man regularly withdrew to commune with the Father—establishes prayer as a pattern his followers are meant to imitate.

context: for God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son that whosoever believeth in Him should not perish, but have everlasting life
point_summary: John 3:16 is cited as scriptural confirmation of God's general will that all mankind be saved, paralleling Paul's statement in 1 Timothy 2:4 that God desires all men to be saved and come to the knowledge of the truth.

context: But in John chapter number 16, in verse number seven,
point_summary: Christ's departure was the necessary condition for the Comforter's coming, establishing that the Holy Spirit's indwelling ministry to believers depended on the completion of Christ's redemptive work.

context: 'Nevertheless, I tell you the truth. 'It is expedient for you that I go away. 'For if I go not away, 'the Comforter will not come unto you, 'but if I depart, I will send Him unto you.'
point_summary: Christ's departure was the necessary condition for the Comforter's coming, and that indwelling Spirit guarantees guidance into all truth—the very enablement by which believers can try the spirits.

context: 'How be it when He, the Spirit of truth, is come. 'He will guide you into all truth.'
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's title "Spirit of truth" in John 16:13 guarantees—not merely offers—complete guidance into all truth, which is the believer's basis for discerning spirits.

context: 'For He shall not speak of Himself, 'but whatsoever He shall hear, that shall He speak, 'and He will show you things to come.'
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's title "Spirit of truth" in John 16:13 grounds the believer's confidence in spiritual discernment, since His guiding into *all* truth—not merely some—is the divine enablement for testing the spirits.

context: And as we run to the truth of John 16, we ought to rejoice that, listen, God, you told us to not believe every spirit.
point_summary: John 16:13's promise that the Spirit of truth "will guide you into all truth" is presented as the believer's God-given enablement to test and discern spirits, making Spirit-guided examination both possible and certain.

context: Jesus said to that religious leader, he said, ye must be born again.
point_summary: False teachers exploit familiar vocabulary—using terms like "born again" while meaning something entirely different, as Jesus's exchange with Nicodemus clarifies that the new birth is spiritual, not religious ritual.

context: And Nicodemus said, how can a man be born again when he's old? Can he enter into his mother's womb a second time?
point_summary: False teachers exploit familiar vocabulary—like "born again"—by redefining it, as Nicodemus's confusion in John 3 illustrates the distinction between physical birth and the spiritual rebirth that no religious exercise can substitute for.

context: Jesus makes it very plain. There is a physical birth, and there is a spiritual birth.
point_summary: False teachers exploit familiar vocabulary—like "born again"—by redefining it, so that religious acts such as baptism are substituted for the spiritual birth Jesus declared necessary to Nicodemus.

context: He that believeth not is condemned already.
point_summary: Condemnation already rests on the unbeliever, grounding the urgency of genuine new birth against mere religious exercise that leaves a person still under judgment.

reference_text: for God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son
context: that's as true as for God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son
point_summary: The promise of walking in the Spirit and not fulfilling the flesh's lusts is held to be as certain and foundational a truth as John 3:16 itself.

reference_text: The truth shall make you free
context: The truth shall make you free. Man, I'm so thankful for the truth of God's Word.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: I must needs go through Samaria
context: Remember what Jesus said in John chapter 4? I must needs go through Samaria.
point_summary: Jesus deliberately traveling through Samaria—despite Jewish hostility toward Samaritans—is presented as a deliberate lesson preparing the disciples to carry the gospel without ethnic or social partiality.

context: ...disciples would be mindful of the example of Christ back in John chapter 4, when he went through Samaria...
point_summary: Christ's deliberate journey through Samaria, despite Jewish hatred of Samaritans, modeled the impartiality that would later undergird the Great Commission's call to witness in Samaria and to all nations.

context: he says in John 17, as he's praying, before he would go to the cross, he says the hour has come
point_summary: Christ's voluntary departure from heavenly glory—referenced through his pre-crucifixion prayer in John 17—is presented as the supreme example of humility for believers to imitate.

context: But I wanna remind you of what Jesus said in John chapter number 13. In John chapter number 13, in verse number 34,
point_summary: Agape love, the same word used in Colossians 3:14, is defined by Christ's own standard: believers are commanded to love one another as He has loved them.

context: a new commandment I give unto you that ye love one another as I have loved you that ye also love one another
point_summary: Christ's command to love one another as He loved us establishes the standard and measure for the agape love believers are called to "put on" above all virtues in Colossians 3.

context: we're to love one another as He loved
point_summary: Love's supremacy over gifts and knowledge—echoed in Jesus's command that disciples be known by mutual love—is grounded here in 1 Corinthians 13's declaration that charity is the greatest of the three abiding virtues.

context: By this shall all men know that you're my disciples
point_summary: Love's visible expression among believers—being known as Christ's disciples by mutual love—is cited as the standard against which the qualities of charity in 1 Corinthians 13 are measured.

context: Note this in John 14, in verse 15, we're familiar with the verse of scripture, but it says, if you love me, keep my commandments.
point_summary: Obedience to Christ and love for Him — not the condition of lost people — must be the primary motivation for evangelism, grounded in the command of John 14:15.

context: Turn to the book of John, if you will. We'll look at two passages and then be done this evening. John chapter number 4. As soon as I mention John chapter 4, many of us are familiar with the text. We understand the woman at the well.
point_summary: The woman at the well abandoned her water pot and returned to the city empty-handed because her encounter with Christ was so transforming that her original errand became irrelevant.

context: The woman then left her water pot, and went her way into the city, and saith to the men,
point_summary: The woman at the well's abandonment of her water pot and immediate witness—"Come, see a man which told me all things that ever I did"—illustrates that personal testimony, however brief and untheological, powerfully draws others to Christ.

context: come and see a man, which told me all things that ever I did: is not this the Christ?
point_summary: The Samaritan woman's brief, theologically simple testimony—"Come, see a man which told me all things that ever I did"—resulted in many Samaritans believing, illustrating the power of personal witness over polished proclamation.

context: I that speak unto thee am he.
point_summary: Jesus' self-declaration as Messiah in John 4:26 gave the Samaritan woman the theological foundation to invite others to "come and see," resulting in many Samaritans believing.

context: Then they went out of the city, and came unto him.
point_summary: A simple personal testimony — "come, see a man who told me all things that ever I did" — moved an entire city to come to Christ, demonstrating the evangelistic power of one transformed life.

context: I sent you to reap that whereon ye bestowed no labour: other men laboured, and ye are entered into their labours.
point_summary: Believers enter into harvests prepared by others' prior labor, as the Samaritan woman's simple personal testimony brought many to faith before the disciples arrived.

context: And many of the Samaritans of that city believed on him for the saying of the woman, which testified, He told me all that ever I did.
point_summary: Many Samaritans believed solely because one woman shared her personal testimony, demonstrating that a simple, untheological witness can bring others to saving faith.

context: For the saying of the woman which testified, he told me all that ever I did. Many, I don't know how many, but many of the Samaritans of that city believed on him.
point_summary: The personal testimony of one woman—"he told me all that ever I did"—was the direct instrument through which many Samaritans came to believe on Christ.

context: Now, the Bible says in verse number 40, so when the Samaritans were come unto him, they besought him that he would tarry with them, and he abode there two days.
point_summary: Personal testimony opens the door, but extended time with Christ himself deepens and multiplies belief, as the Samaritans' faith grew beyond the woman's witness through two days abiding with Jesus.

context: And many more believed because of his own word.
point_summary: Many more believed because of Christ's own word directly, yet the woman's personal testimony remained an indispensable part of the chain that brought them to hear him.

context: And said unto the woman, now we believe not because of thy saying, for we have heard him ourselves and know that this indeed, or that this is indeed the Christ, the Savior of the world.
point_summary: Personal testimony, though ultimately surpassed by direct encounter with Christ, remains a vital instrument in bringing others to confess Him as Savior of the world.

context: And as Jesus passed by, he saw a man which was blind from his birth.
point_summary: The man born blind (John 9:1) is introduced as one whose personal testimony—"one thing I know, that whereas I was blind, now I see"—proved unstoppable despite repeated hostile questioning, illustrating that a firsthand witness to Christ's work carries its own compelling power.

context: Jesus tells him to go wash in the pool of Siloam and he came seeing.
point_summary: The man born blind, healed by Christ's command to wash in Siloam, becomes a model of personal testimony—his repeated, unshakeable witness under hostile questioning illustrating the power of a firsthand encounter with Jesus.

context: Then again called they the man that was blind and said unto him, give God the praise. We know that this man is a sinner.
point_summary: When pressed repeatedly by religious leaders who dismissed Christ as a sinner, the formerly blind man's persistent testimony illustrates how a personal encounter with Jesus compels witness even under hostile interrogation.

context: After being asked many times, in verse number 25, they called again.
point_summary: When pressed repeatedly by hostile questioners, the man born blind fell back on the one thing he could not be argued out of: the undeniable reality of his own personal experience with Christ.

context: He answered and said, Whether he be a sinner or no, I know not: one thing I know, that, whereas I was blind, now I see.
point_summary: When pressed repeatedly by hostile questioners, the formerly blind man's testimony reduced to its irreducible core: whatever he could not explain, he knew with certainty that he had been blind and now could see.

context: He says, look, I don't know how to answer all your questions but one thing I know, I was blind and now I see.
point_summary: When pressed with unanswerable questions, a believer's most powerful testimony is the simple, undeniable witness of personal transformation: "I was blind, now I see."

context: In chapter 11, the situation with Nicodemus, I know I said chapter 10, sorry, chapter 11. The end of the chapter, the Jews that are there, they say in verse 37, those that were mourning because of the death of Lazarus, could not this man which opened the eyes of the blind have caused that even this man should not have died.
point_summary: The healing of the blind man in John 9 was so impactful that it was still being discussed in John 11, where mourners cite it as evidence of Christ's power over death.

context: Jesus comes back to him and at verse number 38, he says, Lord, I believe and he worshiped him.
point_summary: When the formerly blind man encounters Jesus a second time, his response—"Lord, I believe, and he worshipped him"—is presented as the culmination of his growing understanding of who Jesus truly is.

context: herein is my Father glorified, that ye bear much fruit
point_summary: Bearing much fruit—engaging in the gospel work God has called his people to do in their community—is the means by which the Father receives glory.

context: he's promised that he's going to come back and to receive us unto himself, that where he is, we may be also
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:3 to return and receive believers unto himself is invoked as the future counterpart to the disciples' present joy at seeing the risen Jesus standing among them.

context: Jesus said this in John 14 and verse number 27, Peace I leave with you, my peace I give unto you, not as the world giveth, give I unto you. Let not your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid.
point_summary: Christ's greeting of "Peace be unto you" to the frightened disciples is grounded in His prior promise in John 14:27 of a peace distinct from and surpassing anything the world can offer.

context: Let not your heart be troubled.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in Me.
point_summary: Christ's assurance "Let not your heart be troubled" in John 14:1 is applied directly to present-day hearers as the risen Savior's answer to whatever troubles they carry, grounded in His living, sovereign control over all things.

context: In My Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.
point_summary: Jesus' promise in John 14:2—"In my Father's house are many mansions"—is cited as the risen Savior's own answer to troubled hearts, grounding present peace in his living authority and prepared place for believers.

context: And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto Myself that where I am, there ye may be also.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:3 to prepare a place and return grounds the call "Let not your heart be troubled," assuring believers that the risen, living Savior remains sovereignly in control amid present chaos.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Salvation comes not by works or by any self-chosen path, but exclusively through Jesus, who declared himself the only way, truth, and life to the Father.

context: Jesus said, why are you troubled? He said, before He would ever go to that cross, let not your heart be troubled.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus Christ said in John 14, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: The damsel's declaration of "the way of salvation" (not "a way") is confirmed by Christ's own words in John 14:6, establishing that salvation comes through one exclusive path.

context: Jesus said, as I quoted earlier, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man comes unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus is the only way to be saved, as affirmed by His own words in John 14:6 alongside Peter's declaration in Acts 4:12 that no other name saves.

context: Andrew that said 200 penny worth is not sufficient
point_summary: God's miraculous multiplication of a small lunch to feed five thousand — with twelve basketfuls remaining — is cited as a faith-building memorial the disciples should have remembered when facing their next trial.

context: John 14 begins by Jesus saying to these disciples, 'Let not your heart be troubled.'
point_summary: Troubled hearts find their remedy in redirected faith: Jesus commands His disciples not to despair at the news of Calvary because belief in God must extend equally to belief in Him.

context: a new commandment that they should love one another even as He loved them.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 'By this, by your love one for another, shall all men know that you are My disciples.'
point_summary: Mutual love among believers, identified in John 13:35 as the mark by which all men will know Christ's disciples, is cited as the new commandment Jesus gave before His crucifixion.

context: 'Let not your heart be troubled. Ye believe in God, believe also in Me.
point_summary: Jesus's command "Let not your heart be troubled" is grounded in His promise to return and personally receive believers unto Himself, making His second coming the specific remedy for troubled hearts.

context: In My Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 14:2 that He goes to prepare a place and will return to receive His own is presented as the specific, reasoned ground for the disciples' — and believers' — untroubled hearts.

context: 'And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto Myself, that where I am, there ye may be also.'
point_summary: Christ's promised return—"I will come again and receive you unto Myself"—is presented as the specific, personal ground on which troubled hearts find rest.

context: I just wonder as I study and I look at this passage of Scripture, I wonder if their minds race back to that day where we started in John 14, when they heard Jesus say
point_summary: Jesus's promise in John 14:1 to come again and receive His disciples unto Himself is presented as the anchor of encouragement that the disciples would later recall when the angels confirmed His return at the Ascension.

context: in the beginning was the word and the word was with God and the word was God
point_summary: John 1:1 is cited to establish that Christ is uncreated and fully God, ruling out any interpretation of "beginning of the creation of God" (Rev. 3:14) as implying he was the first created being.

context: the word in just a few verses, verse 14, was made flesh and dwelt among us
point_summary: John 1:14's identification of the Word made flesh as God incarnate is cited to refute any reading of "beginning of the creation of God" (Rev. 3:14) as implying Christ was a created being.

context: in verse two, the same was in the beginning with God
point_summary: John 1:2's affirmation that the Word was in the beginning with God establishes that Christ is not a created being but the eternal Creator through whom all things were made.

context: all things were made by him and without him was not anything made that was made
point_summary: Christ's role as Creator of all things—established in John 1:3 and echoed in Colossians 1—demonstrates that he is not a created being but God in flesh, refuting any misreading of "beginning of the creation of God."

context: What did Jesus teach in John 15? I am the vine, ye are the branches.
point_summary: The Laodiceans' claim to need nothing exposed their severance from Christ, the vine who is the sole source of spiritual life for the branches.

context: And Jesus taught in John 15, without me you can do nothing.
point_summary: The Laodicean church's self-sufficient declaration — "I have need of nothing" — is exposed as spiritually fatal by Christ's own teaching that apart from Him, as branches severed from the vine, believers can do nothing.

context: Jesus Christ said this in John 14 in verse number six, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Christ's declaration "I am the way, the truth, and the life" is cited to confirm that the Spirit's witness is reliable, since the Spirit is truth and Christ himself is truth.

context: We find that the Bible says in John 17, sanctify them by thy Word. Thy Word is truth.
point_summary: Scripture's own testimony that "thy Word is truth" grounds the Holy Spirit's witness as fully reliable, trustworthy, and dependable—not merely potentially so.

context: Most people would gladly receive the part of the record that says God so loved the world
point_summary: God's record demands an all-or-none response, and John 3:16 is cited as the part of Scripture most people willingly receive while still rejecting other portions of that same record.

context: as He says in John chapter number 10
point_summary: John 10 is cited as the source of Christ's promise of abundant life, which the sermon presents as a present-tense blessing accompanying the eternal life received through having the Son.

context: my sheep hear my voice and I know them.
point_summary: Christ's promise that His sheep shall never perish and no man can pluck them from His hand grounds the believer's assurance of salvation in God's keeping power, not human effort.

context: they follow me and I give unto them eternal life and they shall never perish. Neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand.
point_summary: Eternal security rests not in the believer's ability to maintain salvation but in being held by God's own hand, making assurance possible and humble.

context: John the Baptist said he must increase and I must decrease.
point_summary: Boasting in the Lord rather than in oneself is the posture John the Baptist modeled—he must increase, I must decrease—which the psalmist mirrors in making God the sole object of his boast.

context: Jesus said this in the book of John, in John chapter number 14 and what a blessing to think about this portion of scripture
point_summary: Christ's own command — "Let not your heart be troubled" — is presented as the ground of the peace of God that believers experience daily, distinct from peace *with* God received at salvation.

context: Let not your heart be troubled: ye believe in God, believe also in me.
point_summary: Jesus's words "let not your heart be troubled" in John 14 are presented as a direct response to the disciples' troubled hearts, grounding the peace of God in a personal promise rather than the absence of conflict.

context: In my Father's house are many mansions: if it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.
