context: But he spake of the temple of his body
point_summary: Christ's declaration "destroy this temple" (v. 19) referred to his own body, establishing from the very outset of his ministry that his death and bodily resurrection were central to his mission.

context: when therefore he was risen from the dead, his disciples remembered that he had said this unto them
point_summary: After the resurrection, the disciples' recollection and belief in Jesus's words about raising the temple of his body confirmed that his rising from the dead was the fulfillment of a promise he had announced from the very beginning of his earthly ministry.

context: how can we trust God in John 3?
point_summary: Undermining Genesis undermines the entire biblical framework: if God cannot be trusted in creation, his word concerning redemption in John 3 collapses with it.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me
point_summary: The exclusivity of salvation through Christ alone—"no other name," "I am the way"—is what gave Paul's grief its weight and compelled his tearful, ceaseless warning.

context: Jesus came to his own and his own received him not.
point_summary: Israel's rejection of Jesus—"he came to his own and his own received him not"—frames Paul's anguished burden for his Jewish kinsmen who, despite their heritage, still need the gospel.

context: If you love me, keep my commandments.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: John chapter number 11, Lazarus is sick, and Jesus delays. Right? Martha and Mary find that Jesus is coming, but Lazarus is already dead.
point_summary: John 11's account of Jesus deliberately delaying before raising Lazarus illustrates that God's apparent inaction is purposeful, working toward greater ends than immediate healing.

context: peace. I leave with you Then he says this My peace I give unto you not as the world giveth give I unto you Let not your heart be troubled neither
point_summary: Christ's peace is distinguished from any humanly manufactured peace — it is his own peace, personally given, not as the world gives, and meant to rule as an umpire over the believer's heart.

context: He must increase. I must decrease.
point_summary: Colossians 3:17's call to do all in Christ's name is grounded in John the Baptist's principle that Christ must increase and self must decrease, making exaltation of the Lord the governing purpose of church behavior.

context: remember he said in John chapter number 14 to the disciples, he said it a couple of times in the book of John, that it was expedient.
point_summary: Christ's physical absence after the ascension is framed as expedient for believers because it secured the permanent, indwelling presence of the Holy Spirit—the Comforter he promised to send.

context: he's walking upon the sea
point_summary: Christ's walking on the sea, noted briefly in John 6:19, is presented as a parallel account to Mark 6 confirming the same miraculous event, underscoring his sovereign power over creation.

context: so the men sat down and number about 5,000
point_summary: John 6:21's account of the disciples willingly receiving Jesus into the ship is set alongside Mark 6:48 to show that Christ's "passing by" was not abandonment but an invitation requiring a willing response.

context: they gathered up the fragments that remained
point_summary: John 6:21's account of the disciples willingly receiving Jesus into the ship is set alongside Mark 6:48's "would have passed by them" to show that God never forces himself upon those in need.

context: they filled the baskets
point_summary: John 6:13's account of the feeding of the five thousand establishes the shared event between Mark 6 and John 6, confirming the two passages describe the same occasion without contradiction.

context: the sea arose by reason of a great wind that blew
point_summary: John's parallel account of the storm confirms Mark's narrative without contradiction, showing that the disciples' willingness to receive Jesus resolved the crisis the great wind had created.

context: they see Jesus walking on the sea
point_summary: John's parallel account of Jesus walking on the sea culminates in the disciples willingly receiving him into the ship, revealing that God never forces himself upon us but awaits our willing reception.

context: He says unto them, it is I be not afraid
point_summary: Christ's self-identification — "It is I, be not afraid" — is set alongside the disciples' willing reception of him into the ship, establishing that God never forces himself upon those in need but awaits a willing response.

context: Then they willingly received him into the ship
point_summary: John 6:21's record that the disciples "willingly received" Jesus into the ship, set alongside Mark's "he would have passed by them," shows that Christ never forces himself upon those in need but waits to be received.

context: remember what John said in John chapter number 6, that they willingly received him into the ship
point_summary: John 6's parallel account is cited to confirm that the disciples *willingly* received Jesus into the ship, underscoring that the storm's end followed their receptive response to His coming to them.

context: You can read in John 6 and other portions, where He speaks about manna that came from heaven
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus said, I, singularly, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus' exclusive claim — "I am the way, the truth, and the life; no man cometh unto the Father but by me" — grounds the urgency to go and tell rather than hold one's peace.

context: Do you remember John 9 the healing of the man that was born blind? There's a great discourse that takes place there in John chapter 9. This man was born blind the disciples immediately say hey Who sinned his parents or him?
point_summary: John 9 is invoked as a narrative illustration of Israel's spiritual blindness, paralleling Paul's statement in Romans 11:25 that blindness in part has happened to Israel until the fullness of the Gentiles comes in.

context: but as many as received Him to them gave He power to become the sons of God
point_summary: Gentile believers, grafted in through Christ's work at Calvary, receive the privilege of becoming sons of God and personally claiming Him as their own God.

context: the account in which God gives us when the man that was born blind that He's healed
point_summary: Even miraculous evidence—like the healing of the man born blind—did not produce belief in those whose hearts were hardened, as the healed man's interrogators illustrate.

context: one thing I know that whereas I was blind, now I see
point_summary: The healed blind man's simple testimony — "whereas I was blind, now I see" — illustrates that personal experience of Christ's power can be more compelling than repeated religious questioning.

context: I have told you already, and you did not hear. Wherefore would you hear it again? Will you also be His disciples?
point_summary: Persistent unbelief in the face of overwhelming miraculous evidence—illustrated by the healed blind man's interrogators—shows that those who mocked Christ at Calvary had no genuine intention to believe regardless of the signs given.

context: The Bible says that Jesus proclaimed in John 14 in verse number 6, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by Me.
point_summary: Christ's self-declaration as "the truth" in John 14:6 stands in direct contrast to the religious leaders' charge that He was a deceiver, since God, being truth, cannot lie.

context: let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in Me.
point_summary: Christ's present work includes preparing a heavenly dwelling for believers, grounded in His own promise that His departure leads to reunion rather than abandonment.

context: My Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.
point_summary: Christ's present heavenly work includes preparing a specific place for believers, grounded in His own promise that His departure guarantees both the prepared place and His return to bring them there.

context: and if I go, He just said He was, and so since He's going and preparing a place for you, He says this, I will come again
point_summary: Christ's present work of preparing a place guarantees His promised return to personally receive believers unto Himself, so that they will dwell where He is.

reference_text: He must increase, but I must decrease.
context: when someone says why is Jesus all the world to you and if he is all the world to you we would repeat the words of John the Baptist when he declared he must increase I must decrease
point_summary: Declaring that Jesus is all the world to me demands the self-emptying posture of John the Baptist — he must increase, I must decrease — as the only fitting answer to why Christ is precious.

context: In John 4 and verse 24, the Bible says that God is a spirit and they that worship Him must worship Him in spirit and in truth.
point_summary: God defines the manner of true worship, requiring it to be offered in spirit and in truth, meaning not all that is called worship genuinely qualifies as such.

context: The Bible tells us that in John chapter number eight,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Sanctify them through thy truth. Thy Word is truth.
point_summary: Truth, identified in John 17:17 as God's Word, is the belt of the armor in Ephesians 6—the foundation on which the believer stands and from which he proclaims in spiritual warfare.

context: In John chapter number eight, our text for this morning, we'll look at a number of passages of scripture, but we find that Jesus is speaking here to some religious leaders, and often as the case was, there'd be debate and dialogue. And we find here in this text of scripture, we'll pick up right in the middle, in verse number 32, notice the proclamation of Jesus. He says, and ye shall know the truth,
point_summary: Freedom in Christ surpasses even American liberty because, unlike national citizenship, it is available to every human being regardless of where they live or what freedoms their government denies them.

context: and the truth shall make you free.
point_summary: Freedom promised in John 8:32 is defined contextually as liberation from sin's dominion, available to any person regardless of nationality or earthly citizenship status.

context: They answered him, these religious leaders, these men that he's speaking to, they say, we be Abraham's seed, and we're never in bondage to any man. How sayest thou, ye shall be made free?
point_summary: The religious leaders' claim in John 8:33 — that as Abraham's descendants they had never been in bondage — reveals their self-deception, since Jesus reframes the bondage in view as slavery to sin, not political servitude.

context: Jesus answered them, verily, verily, I say unto you, whosoever committeth sin is the servant of sin.
point_summary: Committing sin makes one a servant — that is, enslaved under sin's dominion — a bondage that exists regardless of one's religious heritage or perceived freedom.

context: And the servant abideth not in the house forever, but the son abideth ever.
point_summary: Slave status is temporary, but the Son's permanent place in the household grounds the eternal, unqualified freedom He alone can give to any person regardless of earthly citizenship.

context: If the son therefore shall make you free, ye shall be free indeed.
point_summary: Freedom through Christ transcends all earthly political boundaries, making genuine liberty available to every person on earth regardless of national citizenship or governmental restriction.

context: you shall know the truth and the truth shall make you free
point_summary: True freedom in Christ is not license to live however one pleases, but liberation from sin's dominion that enables genuine service to God.

context: we be Abraham's seed. And we're never in bondage to any man. How sayest thou, ye shall be made free?
point_summary: Claiming Abrahamic descent as proof of freedom, the religious leaders misunderstood Jesus entirely, revealing a spiritual blindness that deflected from the bondage to sin he was actually addressing.

context: Wonder why John 8 records where Jesus says that you're of your father the devil, that he's a murderer and he's a liar from the beginning.
point_summary: Satan's identity as liar and murderer from the beginning, cited in John 8:44, is grounded here in Genesis 3, where his assault on God's truth moves from planting doubt to outright contradiction.

context: If you love me, keep my commandments.
point_summary: Obedience to Christ's commands is framed as the evidence of genuine love for Him, directly countering false teachers who twist grace into license for unbridled lust.

context: Ye shall know the truth, and the truth shall make you free.
point_summary: Freedom from truth's promise in John 8:32 is set against false teachers who distort grace into license, contrasting genuine liberation through God's Word with a hollow earthly freedom that ignores eternity.

context: sanctify them through thy word. word is truth.
point_summary: Sanctifying truth is God's Word itself, cited here to ground the claim that Scripture is the providentially preserved source of the freedom Christ offers.

context: you shall know the truth and the truth shall make you free
point_summary: Knowing the truth of John 8:32 is tied here to God's providential preservation of Scripture and the gospel's power to free believers from sin's dominion.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me
point_summary: Jesus's self-declaration "I am the way, the truth, and the life" is cited to establish that He alone is the way to heaven, contrasting this exclusive claim with professing Christians who deny it.

context: Jesus said I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus's self-declaration as "the way, the truth, and the life" in John 14:6 is treated as the definitive refutation of any claim that he is merely one path to the Father among many.

context: we understand how that Jesus Christ is the bread of life. And we find that he gave that truth in the book of John
point_summary: The golden pot of manna in the Ark is read as a type of Christ, who declared himself the bread of life in John 6:35, illustrating how the tabernacle's contents pointed forward to his provision.

context: The Bible says in John chapter 4 that they that worship Him must worship Him in spirit and in truth.
point_summary: True worship must align with God's Word and God's prescribed way—not human invention—because Scripture demands worship in spirit and in truth, ruling out any self-determined approach to God.

context: The devil is a liar and the father of it. John 8, verse 44.
point_summary: Lying is identified as native to the old man's nature by grounding it in Jesus' declaration that the devil is a liar and the father of it.

context: In the beginning was the Word and the Word was with God and the Word was God
point_summary: John 1:1's declaration that "the Word was God" is cited to show the same apostolic author immediately establishing Christ's deity before developing the theme of God as light in both writings.

context: in him was life and the life was the light of men
point_summary: John 1:4 is cited to show that the Gospel of John, like 1 John, introduces Christ as the life who is the light of men, establishing the light-darkness contrast central to both writings.

context: the light shineth in darkness and the darkness comprehendeth it not
point_summary: John 1:5's depiction of light shining in darkness is cited as a parallel to 1 John 1:5, establishing that the light-versus-darkness contrast central to the epistle is rooted in the Gospel of John's own opening testimony about Christ.

context: There was a man sent from God whose name was John
point_summary: John the Baptist's role in John 1:6–7 is cited to show that the light-and-darkness contrast central to 1 John 1:5 runs throughout John's writings, not only in the epistle.

context: the same came for a witness to bear witness of the light
point_summary: John the Baptist's role in John 1:7 is cited to establish the light-and-darkness contrast that runs through both the Gospel of John and 1 John 1:5.

context: He, John the Baptist, was not that light but was sent to bear witness of that light
point_summary: John the Baptist's role as witness rather than the light itself underscores that true, uncreated light belongs to Christ alone, a contrast the sermon traces from John 1 into 1 John's opening declaration.

context: That was the true light which lighteth every man that cometh into the world
point_summary: John 1:9 is cited to show that the light-versus-darkness contrast central to 1 John 1:5 is not unique to the epistle but is already established in the Gospel of John's opening chapter.

context: He was in the world and the world was made by him and the world knew him not
point_summary: John 1:10's declaration that the world knew not the One by whom it was made is cited to reinforce the light-darkness contrast introduced in 1 John 1:5, showing that Christ's rejection by the world reflects the darkness's failure to comprehend the true Light.

context: Remember what Jesus said in John 14, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus Christ is the exclusive way to the Father, and access to God—enabling fellowship—comes only through Him and His blood.

context: Thy word is truth. Is that not what John 17 says?
point_summary: John 17:17 ("Thy word is truth") is cited to identify "the truth" in 1 John 1:6, establishing that absence of God's Word in a believer's life is directly linked to hindered fellowship with God.

context: And you shall never perish and no man shall ever pluck you out of the Father's hand.
point_summary: Salvation is a one-time, unrepeatable event, and the believer's eternal security—no one plucked from the Father's hand—means hindered fellowship requires restoration, not re-salvation.
John 13:3–10 is presented as a picture of the distinction between the once-for-all cleansing of salvation and the ongoing cleansing from sin that believers need, paralleling 1 John 1:9.

John 13:4–10's foot-washing scene is presented as a picture of the ongoing cleansing from 1 John 1:9—the already-bathed believer needing only partial washing illustrates continual forgiveness without full re-initiation into salvation.

John 13:5–10's foot-washing scene is presented as a picture of the ongoing cleansing from 1 John 1:9—the already-bathed believer needing only partial washing illustrates continual forgiveness without full re-initiation into salvation.
John 13:6–10's foot-washing scene is presented as a picture of the ongoing cleansing from 1 John 1:9—the already-bathed believer still needs continual washing from the defilement of daily sin.
Present obedience to Christ's cleansing sometimes precedes full understanding, as Jesus told Peter that what He was doing would only be known "hereafter."

John 13:8's "if I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me" is presented as a picture of the ongoing cleansing described in 1 John 1:9, illustrating that fellowship with Christ depends on receiving His cleansing.

John 13:9—Peter's sudden reversal from refusal to requesting full washing illustrates the believer's need for ongoing cleansing, distinguishing initial salvation from continual fellowship-restoring forgiveness in 1 John 1:9.

John 13:10 is read as a picture of the believer who, already cleansed at salvation, still needs ongoing confession to restore fellowship—illustrating the distinction between positional cleansing and continual cleansing in 1 John 1:9.

context: Peter saith unto Him after this situation, Lord, dost thou wash my feet?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus said, what I do thou knowest not in verse number 7 now, but thou shalt know hereafter.
point_summary: Jesus' statement that Peter would understand "hereafter" what He was doing frames the foot-washing as a picture of the ongoing cleansing described in 1 John 1:9, whose full significance believers may not immediately grasp.

context: Peter said, thou shalt never wash my feet. Jesus said, if I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me.
point_summary: John 13:8's "if I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me" frames Christ's cleansing of Peter as a picture of the necessity of the ongoing confession and forgiveness described in 1 John 1:9.

context: Simon Peter then says, I mean, he totally changed his tone, right? All right, Lord, not my feet only, but also my hands and my head.
point_summary: John 13:10's distinction between being "clean every whit" and needing only feet washed pictures the difference between the believer's complete positional cleansing and the ongoing confession needed for daily defilement.

context: Notice what Jesus says in verse number 10. He that is washed needeth not save to wash His feet, but is clean every wit, and ye are clean, but not all.
point_summary: John 13:10's distinction between a person already bathed needing only foot-washing and being "clean every whit" pictures the difference between positional cleansing and the ongoing confession and cleansing described in 1 John 1:9.

context: when Jesus says in verse number 10, he that is washed needeth not save to wash his feet
point_summary: Using the cultural practice of bathing and then washing only dirty feet upon returning home, John 13:10 illustrates that the saved person's sin condition is already dealt with, but ongoing cleansing is needed as we walk through a sin-cursed world.

context: Study John 13 more this afternoon, this evening.
point_summary: Jesus's washing of the disciples' feet illustrates that the saved person's sin-condition is already dealt with at salvation, but ongoing cleansing is needed as daily life in a sin-cursed world hinders fellowship with God.

context: For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life.
point_summary: God's love in giving His Son (John 3:16) stands alongside His wrath abiding on the unbeliever (v. 36), establishing that every person encountered daily is either saved or under divine judgment.

context: The Bible continues in verse number 17. It says God sent not his son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world through him might be saved.
point_summary: Christ's mission was not condemnation but salvation, cited here as the redemptive backdrop against which the lost remain under God's wrath until they believe.

context: But look at verse 36 in John chapter number three. We don't have time to read the entirety of it, but look at verse number 36 of John chapter number three, the last verse of this chapter. The Bible says this. He that believeth on the Son hath everlasting life. And he that believeth not the Son shall not see life, but the wrath of God abideth on him.
point_summary: John 3:36 is read to establish that every unsaved person encountered daily lives under the abiding wrath of God—a present sentence of eternal damnation that should compel believers to speak as God's spokesmen.

context: That's what Jesus said in John 17 when he prayed. He said, keep them from the evil
point_summary: Believers are called to remain in the world yet unspotted by it, as Christ's own prayer affirmed—kept from evil and sanctified through the word of truth.

context: sanctify them through thy word, thy word is truth
point_summary: Sanctification through God's Word—which is truth—is the means by which believers remain unspotted from the world while living within it.

context: John chapter number two, this is the first miracle, the beginning of miracles,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: his mother, Mary, saith unto the servants, whatsoever he saith unto you, do it.
point_summary: Mary's instruction to the servants — "whatsoever he saith unto you, do it" — is applied as a principle of total obedience to Christ's written Word, which the sermon connects to Gaius's exemplary obedience in 3 John.

context: Jesus tells us in John 14 and 15. He says, if you love me, keep my commandments.
point_summary: Genuine love for God, as illustrated by Gaius, necessarily produces obedience to His commandments — the direct connection Jesus draws in John 14:15.

context: when the mother of Jesus, Mary, she said, what sort of he saith unto you, do it
point_summary: Mary's instruction at Cana — "whatsoever he saith unto you, do it" — is used to call believers to active participation in God's work rather than passive spectatorship, as the servants who obeyed were the ones who witnessed the miracle firsthand.

context: the servants which drew the water knew is a statement in verse number nine
point_summary: The servants at Cana who drew the water "knew" what had taken place (v. 9) because they were active participants in the miracle, illustrating that involvement in God's work brings a firsthand knowledge spectators never gain.

context: And Jesus said without me you can do nothing.
point_summary: Trials drive believers toward dependence, as Jesus' own words — "without me you can do nothing" — are cited to show that God uses suffering to deepen our awareness of how much we need him.

context: I use that example because we understand that Jesus was confronted with that in John chapter number eight. You remember in John chapter number eight that they come to Jesus.
point_summary: John 8's account of the woman caught in adultery is cited as a New Testament illustration of the Mosaic law's capital penalty for adultery, which required death rather than any atoning sacrifice.

context: to write in the sand, and then all of a sudden all of the accusers are gone. Right? You remember John 8.
point_summary: John 8 is cited as a familiar illustration of Leviticus 20:10 in action—adultery was a capital offense under Mosaic law, with no atoning sacrifice prescribed for it.

context: Peter stands up in John 6 and verse number 68, and he says to whom? He says this, Lord, to whom shall we go? Thou hast the words of eternal life
point_summary: When all others desert Christ, Peter's confession in John 6:68 declares there is nowhere else to turn, because Jesus alone holds the words of eternal life.

context: Do you remember in John chapter number 13, Jesus is telling the disciples, we often reference how that Jesus tells them that He gives them a new commandment
point_summary: In John 13–14, Jesus' announcement of His departure flows directly into His promise to return, so the chapter break should not obscure the continuity of His encouragement to the disciples.

context: And Jesus says, let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in Me.
point_summary: Amid the disciples' troubled hearts at Christ's imminent departure, Jesus' command "let not your heart be troubled" grounds their peace in the promised return and prepared place.

context: In My Father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you.
point_summary: Christ's promised return to receive his own to himself is set forth as the antidote to troubled hearts overwhelmed by difficulty and prone to forgetting God's promises.

context: I go to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto Myself that where I am, there ye may be also.
point_summary: Christ's promised return to receive believers to himself is presented as the antidote to troubled hearts overwhelmed by difficulty and prone to forgetting God's promises.

context: It was in the third chapter, that great declaration when he says, I must decrease, but he must increase.
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration "He must increase, but I must decrease" is cited as the defining posture of a minister who understood his God-given assignment and fulfilled it completely.

context: behold, the Lamb of God which taketh away the sin of the world
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration "Behold, the Lamb of God which taketh away the sin of the world" is cited as evidence that he fulfilled the specific assignment God gave him as forerunner of Christ.

context: We find that in John chapter number 17, notice what the Bible tells us. We know that this actually is, in John 17, the prayer of Jesus.
point_summary: John 17 is identified as the true Lord's Prayer—Christ's own intercession—in which Jesus declares to the Father that He has glorified Him on earth, presenting His completed work as the supreme example of fulfilling one's God-given ministry unto death.

context: He says, I have glorified Thee on the earth.
point_summary: Jesus' declaration in John 17:4—"I have finished the work which Thou gavest Me to do"—is presented as a proleptic statement of absolute certainty about the cross, spoken before His death yet treated as already accomplished.

context: It begins in v. 1. Father, the hour has come. Glorify Thy Son that Thy Son also may glorify Thee.
point_summary: John 17:1 introduces the true Lord's Prayer, in which Jesus, facing the hour of His death, asks the Father to glorify the Son so that the Son might glorify the Father.

context: Jesus lifts up His head and as it says, it is finished. And He bowed His head and gave up the ghost.
point_summary: The cry "It is finished" in John 19:30 is read as the fulfillment of the work Jesus declared already complete in John 17, his substitutionary atonement accomplished with certainty from eternity.

context: He's the Good Shepherd and that the Good Shepherd lays down His life for the sheep.
point_summary: The Good Shepherd's voluntary self-sacrifice for the sheep, foretold in John 10, finds its fulfillment in Christ's deliberate, completed act of laying down His life on the cross.

context: Peter had said this, I go a-fishing. I go a-fishing in John 21 and verse number 3.
point_summary: Peter's declaration "I go a-fishing" illustrates the danger of abandoning one's God-given course—used here to set up Christ's pointed call to Peter to follow his own ministry, not another's.

context: Now Jesus had already told Peter in verse number 19, follow me.
point_summary: Knowing one's specific calling is essential to fulfilling it—Jesus' pointed command to Peter, "Follow thou me," redirects him from comparing his ministry to John's back to his own God-assigned course.

context: And I want you to notice what John 21 in verse number 21 says. Peter seeing him, this him is speaking of John according to verse number 20, saith to Jesus, Lord, and what shall this man do?
point_summary: Peter's question about John's calling—"what shall this man do?"—illustrates the danger of comparing ministries, met by Christ's pointed redirection: follow thou me, not another's course.

context: You know what Jesus' answer is in verse number 22? If I
point_summary: Each believer's calling is distinct and non-transferable; Jesus redirects Peter's comparison with John back to his own assigned course with the words "what is that to thee? Follow thou me."

context: John 15 speaks about the importance of abiding in him. You're the branches, Jesus said, but I am the vine.
point_summary: Fulfilling ministry requires total dependence on Christ as the vine, since apart from him the branch has no life and can accomplish nothing.

reference_text: without me, you can do nothing
context: And Jesus says, without me, you can do nothing.
point_summary: Fulfilling ministry requires total dependence on Christ as the vine, since apart from him—as Jesus declares—we can do nothing in our own strength.

context: Jesus said What I am the vine? Ye are the branches. That's what Jesus says.
point_summary: Spiritual independence from God is impossible, for as Jesus declares in John 15:5, the branches can do nothing apart from the vine who sustains all things.

context: Now remember the Lord Jesus Christ in John chapter 13 said a new commandment I give unto you.
point_summary: The love Christians show one another is the distinguishing mark by which all men recognize them as Christ's disciples, fulfilling the new commandment Jesus gave in John 13.

context: God so loved the world. We understand the truth of that declaration in John 3, 16.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: they that worship him, John 4, must worship him in spirit and in truth
point_summary: Genuine worship of God requires spirit and truth, and Scripture—as the inspired word—is the necessary foundation for any activity, including song leading, that aims to meet that standard.

context: as many as received Him, to them gave He power to become the sons of God
point_summary: Receiving Christ grants the believer a permanent Father-son relationship with God, distinct from fellowship, which sin can hinder.

context: The Bible says, if you love me, keep my commandments
point_summary: Obedience to Christ's commands must flow from love for Him, not mere duty, or it becomes the hollow, rote religion that left the Ephesian church active in service but empty of first love.

context: although Jesus said to the disciples that we're to love one another and that by this all men shall know that you're my disciples
point_summary: Loving one another as Christ commanded is not the deficiency cited at Ephesus; the abandoned love is love for Christ himself.

reference_text: if you love me, keep my commandments
context: Again, if you love me, keep my commandments.
point_summary: Obedience without love is hollow, as the Pharisees demonstrated—keeping commandments externally while lacking the heart relationship that genuine love for God produces.

context: when he was speaking to the Pharisees, some of the religious leaders of the day, and on two occasions in that particular passage of Scripture, in verse 33 and again in verse number 39, those religious leaders claimed to be Abraham's seed.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: when he was speaking to the Pharisees, some of the religious leaders of the day, and on two occasions in that particular passage of Scripture, in verse 33 and again in verse number 39, those religious leaders claimed to be Abraham's seed.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: you'll remember at the end of the passage, it was Jesus that said, before Abraham was, I am.
point_summary: Jesus's declaration "Before Abraham was, I am" (John 8:58) is cited as evidence of his eternality and deity, establishing that Christ is God.

context: Remember in John chapter number six when Jesus is speaking there and all of those disciples that had followed Him, they went away and Jesus says, are you going to go also?
point_summary: When social and political divisions tempt people to abandon Christ, Peter's answer in John 6:67—"to whom shall we go?"—illustrates that there is no alternative to Christ, who alone has the words of eternal life.

context: And Peter stands up and says this, to whom shall we go? He says that we know that thou art the Christ and that you have the words of eternal life.
point_summary: Peter's confession in John 6:68 — that Christ alone holds the words of eternal life — is invoked to warn believers against turning back, as the Hebrews recipients were tempted to do.

context: Remember what Jesus said in John 14, in verse number 6,
point_summary: Christ as forerunner and great high priest has made and is himself the only way to the Father, securing access that no earthly priest could provide.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father, but by me.
point_summary: Jesus, as the great high priest and forerunner, has personally made and is the way to the Father, fulfilling what no Old Testament priest could accomplish.

reference_text: if you love me, keep my commandments
context: Jesus said, if you love me, keep my commandments.
point_summary: Genuine love for God's Word is demonstrated not by verbal profession alone but by obedient behavior, as Jesus himself taught that love for him is proven through keeping his commandments.

context: We find that often we look at the letters or the number of letters in John 11, 35, Jesus wept.
point_summary: John 11:35 is cited only as a point of textual comparison—its letter count in English contrasted with the single Greek word of 1 Thessalonians 5:16—not for its theological content.

context: For God so loved the world.
point_summary: God's love in John 3:16 is both universal and individual — encompassing the whole world while including each person specifically, so that Christ's atoning death was personally intended for every individual soul.

context: Turn to John chapter 11. Let me show you a New Testament example of this truth this evening as we tie these things together. And we understand that God has all power. He has a purpose. He has a plan. We find that in John chapter 11, Jesus receives word that one that He loves is sick. His name is Lazarus.
point_summary: John 11 is cited as a New Testament parallel to Exodus 14, showing that God's deliberate delay in healing Lazarus—like Israel's seemingly wrong path—served a purposeful divine plan demonstrating His power.

context: after Jesus wept, in John 11 and verse 35, those Jewish people say they see through His actions, oh, how He loved him.
point_summary: Jesus' tears at Lazarus's tomb moved the mourning Jews to acknowledge His love, yet they questioned why His power had not prevented the death — a tension the sermon uses to frame God's purposeful delay.

context: Look in John 11 and verse number six, the Bible says this, when He, Jesus, heard therefore that He was sick, notice the next words, He abode two days.
point_summary: God's deliberate two-day delay in John 11:6 reveals that divine inaction is purposeful, designed to produce greater belief than an immediate healing would have accomplished.

context: I want you to notice in verse number 15, what the Bible says, Jesus says this to the disciples. He says, and I am glad for your sakes that I was not there to the intent, notice what He says, ye may believe, nevertheless, let us go unto Him.
point_summary: Christ's deliberate delay in going to Lazarus was not negligence but purposeful, so that the disciples would witness resurrection power and be brought to deeper belief.

context: If you study through John chapter 11, you're gonna find something very, I can't preach it all tonight because of time, but you're gonna find something amazing in John chapter 11.
point_summary: The divine delay in John 11—Jesus deliberately waiting two days—served a purpose beyond healing, bringing Lazarus back from four days of death so that all present, and all readers, might believe at a deeper level.

reference_text: He says, move that stone. Martha says, oh no, no, listen, He's been dead for four days, He stinks. Surely He stinketh.
context: He says, move that stone. Martha says, oh no, no, listen, He's been dead for four days, He stinks. Surely He stinketh.
point_summary: The divine delay in John 11—including Martha's protest at the stone in verse 39—was purposeful, designed to produce a greater faith than healing alone could have generated.

context: And He says, Lazarus, come forth. And what happens? Well, Lazarus comes walking out.
point_summary: The divine delay in John 11 — Jesus waiting, then calling Lazarus from the tomb — was designed to produce a deeper faith than healing alone could have accomplished.

context: and all of those Jews, the mourners that were there, and might I say us this evening, and every child of God that's read the Bible, would not have John 11 the way that we have it, if Jesus would have just gone and healed him of his sickness.
point_summary: The raising of Lazarus in John 11 is presented as a miracle that required Jesus to withhold a lesser healing so that all present—and all subsequent readers—might believe God can do anything.

context: Look at the verse. It's a familiar verse, John 3, in verse number 16. It's a verse that many could quote, no doubt. But in John 3, in verse number 16, the Bible says this, for God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son.
point_summary: The price God was willing to pay — His only begotten Son — establishes the supreme worth and value He places on every human life.

context: whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life
point_summary: God's willingness to give his only Son for "whosoever" demonstrates the immeasurable value he places on every human life he created.

context: For God sent not his Son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world through him might be saved
point_summary: God's purpose in sending Christ was not condemnation but salvation, cited here as evidence of the immeasurable value God places on every human life.

context: Jesus was the Lamb of God
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he that believeth on the Son hath everlasting life
point_summary: Belief in the Son brings everlasting life, while rejection leaves one under God's abiding wrath — the eternal stakes that underscore how greatly God valued humanity in designing redemption.

context: He said, I am the way, the truth and the life. No man cometh unto the father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus Christ's exclusive claim — "I am the way, the truth, and the life; no man cometh unto the Father but by me" — is cited as the basis for the urgent call to trust Christ alone for salvation.

context: the one who according to John 15 is abiding in Christ
point_summary: Abiding in Christ, as described in John 15, is presented as the defining mark of one whom God would call a man or woman of God.

context: what God says, the Lord Jesus Christ says to Thomas
point_summary: Faith that rests on sight rather than trust is rebuked when Christ confronts Thomas's demand for physical proof, illustrating the call to believe God's word without requiring visible evidence.

context: We have a God who is so gracious, who is so kind. We have a God who knows our frame. He wants us to be people of faith and here we find that Jesus, he goes to Thomas and notice what he says, reach hither thy finger, behold my hands and reach hither thy hand and thrust it into my side and be not faithless, but believing.
point_summary: Jesus's patient invitation to Thomas to touch His wounds — rather than rebuking his doubt — demonstrates both Christ's deity (accepted without correction) and His gracious work of building weak faith into believing faith.

context: Thomas answered and said unto him, my Lord and my God.
point_summary: Thomas's confession "My Lord and my God" went unrebuked because, as the sermon notes, Jesus is in fact God—making the declaration truth rather than presumption.

context: Jesus saith unto him, Thomas, because thou hast seen me, thou hast believed. What are they that have not seen and yet have believed?
point_summary: Faith that rests on God's word without physical sight is the blessed condition Jesus commends to Thomas—and the condition believers today are called to pursue.

context: And we understand that Jesus said this as the end of John, chapter number 13, he says, a new commandment I give you.
point_summary: The new commandment of John 13:34 raises the standard of love from the Old Testament's "love your neighbor as yourself" to the New Testament's "love as I have loved," with Christ's self-giving love as the measure.

context: John in chapter 14 deals with that as well. If you love me, keep my commandments
point_summary: Keeping Christ's commandments is presented as the demonstrable evidence—not merely the verbal claim—of genuine love for God.

reference_text: Lamb of God which taketh away the sin of the world
context: Praise the Lord for the precious blood of the Lamb of God which taketh away the sin of the world.
point_summary: Salvation's mercy is defined as the setting aside of the law's penalty, accomplished entirely through the blood of the Lamb who takes away the sin of the world.

reference_text: search the scriptures, for in them you think that you have eternal life, but they are they which testify of me
context: As Jesus is dealing with some people during his earthly ministry, he said, search the scriptures, for in them you think that you have eternal life, but they are they which testify of me, in John chapter number five.
point_summary: Scripture's testimony to Christ is the animating purpose behind diligent Bible study, as Jesus himself declared that the scriptures searched for eternal life ultimately point to him.

context: be of good cheer. And what a great thought that is from that portion of scripture as Jesus is going through and providing the disciples with so much information with regard to what they would face and days ahead and years ahead with regard to ministry. But he says, be of good cheer, why? I have overcome the world
point_summary: Christ's victory over the world grounds the believer's call to "be of good cheer," since the God they serve is undefeated, enthroned, and purposeful in his plan.

context: And what a joy that Jesus himself said, I will come again and receive you unto myself that where I am, there ye may be also.
point_summary: Christ's endurance of the cross was sustained by future joy—the same resurrection hope that anchors the soldier's call to endure hardness, culminating in his promised return for his own.

context: John the Baptist said, I must decrease, but he must increase.
point_summary: Self-decrease in service of Christ's supremacy—captured in John the Baptist's words "I must decrease, but he must increase"—is offered as the corrective to the self-centered mindset that abandons endurance.

context: the Bible tells us that He is the comforter

context: it's expedient for me that I go away. Why? Because if I don't go away then I can't send the comforter

context: He said I'm not going to leave you comfortless
point_summary: Christ's promise not to leave believers comfortless is fulfilled through the permanent indwelling of the Holy Spirit, a New Testament reality distinct from the Spirit's temporary, departing presence in the Old Testament.

context: The Bible tells us the Holy Spirit is not only the comforter but He's the teacher. He would guide us into all truth. He would teach us all things.
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's role as teacher—guiding believers into all truth and teaching them all things—is cited as the means by which He uses the Word of God to strengthen and equip soldiers of Christ.

context: But as many as received him to them gave he power to become the sons of God
point_summary: Sonship with God is not universal by creation but entered only through redemption, as John 1:12 grounds the claim that one becomes a child of God solely by receiving Christ.

context: Mary comes to those Servants and they say she says to them speaking of Jesus whatsoever. He saith unto you Do it
point_summary: Mary's words to the servants — "whatsoever he saith unto you, do it" — are held up as the simplest and most complete expression of the obedience every child of God is called to render to Christ's word.

context: in the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God

context: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us
point_summary: The integrity of Christ's life and lips as the incarnate Word—"the Word became flesh"—grounds the call for a father's message and manner to align.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by Me
point_summary: Jesus Christ, as the living Word made flesh, embodies the way, truth, and life He proclaimed, making His life and lips inseparable—a standard His followers are called to mirror.

context: as you read Matthew Mark Luke and John and you begin to read about the life and Ministry of the Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: search the scriptures why for in them you think They have eternal life, but then he says this they are they which testify of me
point_summary: Scripture itself testifies of Christ, so searching it diligently—as Jesus commands in John 5:39—is the means by which wisdom, which is the principal thing, is obtained.

context: he tells us that he came to give us life and that we might have it more abundant.
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 10:10 of abundant life means not only eternal life but a present quality of life enriched by hearing and obeying God's Word.

context: Whatsoever He saith unto you, do it.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Salvation comes exclusively through Jesus Christ—who died, was buried, and rose in victory—and no one reaches the Father except through Him.

context: Remember, Jesus said in John 15, without me, ye can do nothing
point_summary: Obedience to God's counter-intuitive commands—including overcoming evil with good—requires total dependence on Christ, since apart from him believers can do nothing in their own flesh.

context: He said, and we'll look at it in John 15, we'll look at the two verses at the end, but without me you can do nothing.

context: He said you're to worship me in spirit and in truth.
point_summary: Authentic worship is defined by God's own terms—spirit and truth—not by crowd size or human innovation, underscoring that he is a holy God whose presence demands reverence.

context: Jesus says, I am the true vine. My father's the husbandman.

context: I am the vine, ye are the branches... for without me ye can do nothing.

context: I want you to think about John chapter number four, a text of scripture that is always so impactful to us as we look at the Gospel of John
point_summary: Christ's purposeful movement in John 4—leaving Judea and departing toward Galilee with full knowledge of the Pharisees' awareness—illustrates that he walked with intentional, omniscient purpose rather than by circumstance.

context: when therefore the Lord knew how the Pharisees had heard that Jesus made and baptized more disciples than John

context: though Jesus himself baptized not, but his disciples

context: he left Judea and departed again into Galilee
point_summary: Christ's deliberate passage through Samaria—contrary to Jewish custom of avoidance—illustrates that he moved with intentional purpose to bring the Gospel to those others bypassed.

point_summary: Christ's deliberate route through Samaria—contrary to Jewish custom of avoidance—illustrates that he walked purposefully, crossing social and ethnic divides to transform a life.

context: Then cometh he to a city of Samaria, which is called Sychar, near to the parcel of ground that Jacob gave to his son Joseph.
point_summary: Jesus' deliberate route through Samaria—contrary to Jewish custom of avoiding it entirely—illustrates that he walked purposefully, crossing social and ethnic divides to transform a life.

point_summary: Jesus' weariness at Jacob's well illustrates that he walked purposefully into Samaria—crossing a deliberate cultural barrier Jews normally avoided by taking a longer route—to transform a woman's life.

context: Jesus therefore being wearied with his journey, sat thus on the well and it was about the sixth hour.

context: Then cometh a woman of Samaria to draw water and Jesus saith unto her, give me to drink.

context: No, Jesus said that he must needs go through Samaria.

context: Abide in me, and I in you, as the branch cannot bear fruit of itself, except it abide in the vine, no more can you except you abide in me.

context: if a man abide not in me
point_summary: Abiding in Christ, the true vine, is the textual foundation connecting John 15 to 1 John 2:6, where the claim to abide in Him must be evidenced by walking as He walked.

context: if ye abide in me
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I am the true vine, and my Father is the husbandman.
point_summary: Christ's declaration as "the true vine" in John 15:1 grounds the abiding language of 1 John 2:6, establishing that fruitbearing is only possible through genuine connection to him rather than any false substitute.

context: Every branch in me that beareth not fruit he taketh away: and every branch that beareth fruit, he purgeth it, that it may bring forth more fruit.
point_summary: Fruitfulness in the believer's life depends entirely on abiding in Christ, the true vine, since God is the one who produces the increase through those who remain connected to Him.

context: Now ye are clean through the word which I have spoken unto you.

context: except it abide in the vine. No more can ye except ye abide in me.
point_summary: Abiding in Christ as the vine is the necessary condition for bearing fruit, with progressive fruitfulness—from fruit to more fruit to much fruit—dependent entirely on that union.

context: I am the vine, ye are the branches. He that abideth in me, and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit. For without me he can do nothing.
point_summary: Abiding in Christ is the indispensable condition for fruitfulness, with the progression from fruit to more fruit to much fruit marking the deepening result of that union.

context: in verse number two, it says that if he beareth not fruit, and then it says in every branch that beareth fruit.
point_summary: Fruitfulness in the believer's life is progressive — from fruit to more fruit to much fruit — and this increase is conditioned entirely on abiding in Christ, without whom nothing can be produced.

context: John chapter number one, he came to his own and his own received him not.

reference_text: Verse 12, but as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name.
context: Verse 12, but as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name.
point_summary: Receiving Christ is presented as a universal choice available to every person, underscoring the urgency of soul-winning so that people even know they have a choice to make.

context: what does the truth say in John three in verse 36? Look, if you don't, you're condemned already, right?
point_summary: Those who have never heard the gospel are already condemned by default, making their ignorance of the choice to receive or reject Christ the urgent burden driving purposeful soul-winning.

context: Continue now in John 15 verse number six, if a man abide not in me, he has cast forth as a branch and is withered and men gather them and cast them into the fire. and they are burned.

context: If you abide in me and my words abide in you, you shall ask what you will and it shall be done unto you.

context: John 9, he saw a man that was born blind. It was an opportunity.

context: And in John 15, 10, if you keep my commandments, you shall abide in my love, even as I have kept my Father's commandments and abide in His love.
point_summary: Obedience to Christ's commandments is presented as the practical means of abiding in His love, with unrepentant sin identified as what causes that love to grow cold.

context: abide in me and I in you. As a branch cannot bear fruit of itself except it abide in the vine, no more can ye except ye abide in me.

context: In fact, in John 15, the Lord Jesus Christ instructs His followers to abide in Him.
point_summary: Abiding in Christ, as commanded in John 15, frames the Christian life as an ongoing journey of walking with the Lord rather than a static, once-for-all experience.

context: John 1 and verse number 12, but as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God.

context: ...God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son...

context: that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life.

context: But He declares in John 17, the hour has come.

context: John the apostle, he heard the Lord Jesus Christ speak in John 15 with regard to the truth of abide in me.

context: where Jesus says, for I do always those things that please him in John 8 and verse 29.

context: The Lord Jesus Christ, he said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.

context: In John 1, verses 1-3, the Bible gives us great truth with regard to the incarnation of Jesus Christ. ... the Bible says in the beginning was the Word. Is Word in your Bible capitalized? Amen. And the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
point_summary: John 1:1's capitalized "Word" is cited to confirm that the one who became flesh (v. 14) is fully God, grounding Colossians 2:9's claim that all the fullness of the Godhead dwells bodily in Christ.

context: The same was in the beginning with God.

context: ...for the law was given by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ.

context: No man hath seen God at any time. The only begotten Son, which is the bosom of the Father, He hath declared it.

context: Now we understand John 15, Jesus said abide in me and I in you, and He talks about the vine and the branches

context: after these things, Jesus walked in Galilee. He walked in Galilee. Notice this. He would not walk in Jewry. Why? Because the Jews sought to kill Him.

context: Turn over to John 11, if you will, just a few chapters over, John chapter number 11.

context: Jesus therefore walked no more openly among the Jews, but went fenced into a country near to the wilderness, into a city called Ephraim, and there continued with His disciples.
point_summary: Jesus withdrew from public ministry among the Jews not out of fear but out of wisdom, carefully ordering His movements until the appointed hour had come.

context: He did not walk openly in John chapter number 7 and verse number 1

context: it's first introduced to us in John chapter number 2
