context: in John chapter number 2, that mine hour is not yet come
point_summary: The phrase "mine hour is not yet come" introduced in John 2 anchors a thread running through the entire Gospel, showing that Christ's every movement was governed by divine timing rather than fear.

context: and it goes all the way to John 17 when He says the hour is come
point_summary: John 17 marks the culmination of a progression through the Gospel of John in which "the hour is come," signaling that Jesus walked wisely—not fearfully—through all preceding events until the appointed time was fully reached.

context: Jesus is washing the feet of the disciples.
point_summary: Jesus' act of washing his disciples' feet illustrates that true greatness, even for the Son of Man, is expressed through the lowest acts of humble service.

context: he riseth from supper and laid aside his garments and took a towel and girded himself
point_summary: Jesus's willingness to perform the most culturally degrading task—foot-washing, too lowly even to compel a servant to do—demonstrates the radical humility that defines how Christ walked.

context: After that, he poureth water into a base and began to wash the disciples' feet, and to wipe them with the towel wherewith he was girded
point_summary: Jesus's act of washing his disciples' feet—a task so menial that servants could not be compelled to perform it—is presented as the defining demonstration of his humble, servant-minded walk.

context: then cometh he to Simon Peter, and Peter saith unto him, Lord, dost thou wash my feet?
point_summary: Peter's stunned refusal in John 13:6 illustrates the radical, culture-defying humility of Christ, who willingly performed the lowest conceivable servant's task despite his lordship.

context: Jesus answered and said unto him, What I do thou knowest not now, but thou shalt know hereafter
point_summary: Christ's humble act of foot-washing surpassed even the lowest servant's duty, illustrating that true greatness is found in ministering to others rather than seeking preeminence.

context: Peter saith unto him, Thou shalt never wash my feet
point_summary: Foot-washing was considered so degrading that servants could not be compelled to perform it, making Jesus' act in John 13 the supreme demonstration of the humility Christians are called to imitate.

context: Thou shalt never wash my feet. Jesus said, If I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me.
point_summary: Peter's protest—"Thou shalt never wash my feet"—and his immediate reversal upon Christ's warning that refusal means having no part with Him illustrates how submission to Christ's servant act is inseparable from belonging to Him.

context: Then Peter changes. I mean, 180-degree turn here. He saith unto him, Lord, not my feet only, but also my hands and my head.
point_summary: Peter's confession of Christ as Lord made his initial refusal to let Jesus wash his feet understandable, yet Jesus's warning that Peter would have no part with him prompted an immediate, total reversal of that refusal.

context: Verse 10, Jesus saith unto him, He that is washed, he is not to say to wash his feet,
point_summary: Jesus' declaration that the already-bathed disciple needs only his feet washed illustrates positional cleansing already received, while Peter's dramatic reversal shows that belonging to Christ is the disciple's supreme concern.

context: but is clean every whip, and ye are clean, but not all.
point_summary: Jesus's declaration that the washed disciple needs only his feet cleansed grounds the foot-washing scene as a lesson in humility, leading directly to his command that his disciples likewise serve one another.

context: For he knew who should betray him, therefore said he, ye are not all clean.
point_summary: Jesus's foreknowledge of Judas's betrayal is cited to explain why he qualified "ye are clean" — he knew one among them was not.

context: So after he had washed their feet and had taken his garments and was set down again, he said unto them, Know ye what I have done to you?
point_summary: Christ's act of washing his disciples' feet, though he is acknowledged as Lord and Master, establishes humility as a defining mark of those who follow him.

context: Ye call me master and lord. Notice this, and ye say, Well, for so I am.
point_summary: Christ's own declaration that he is both Master and Lord grounds the foot-washing as a deliberate act of humility, making his service a binding pattern for his followers.

context: If I then, your lord and master, have washed your feet, ye also ought to wash one another's feet.
point_summary: Christ's act of washing his disciples' feet, though he is their Lord and Master, establishes humility as a defining mark of those who follow him, grounded in Philippians 2's call to share his self-emptying mind.

context: destroy this temple, and in three days, I will raise it up
point_summary: Jesus's claim in John 2:19 to raise the temple of his body in three days is presented as a deliberate pre-announcement of his own death and bodily resurrection.

context: you can read that in John chapter two, in verse number 19, the context makes it abundantly clear that Jesus is speaking about his body, his body is the temple, he's speaking about his death, and his glorious resurrection
point_summary: Jesus' declaration in John 2:19 is presented as a pre-announced prophecy of his own death and bodily resurrection, with the temple explicitly identified as his body.

context: Jesus said, in the way, the truth, and the life, no man cometh unto the Father but by me
point_summary: Jesus' exclusive claim in John 14:6 — "I am the way, the truth, and the life, no man cometh unto the Father but by me" — is cited to show that what one believes about Jesus makes an ultimate difference.

context: Jesus says, search the Scriptures, for they are they which testify of me.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: In John chapter number 20, again, the record of the resurrection is given to us in verses one through nine.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: But beginning in verse number 10,
point_summary: John 20:10 introduces the scene of Mary alone at the empty tomb—the disciples having departed—setting the stage for Christ's personal appearance to her as an individual.

context: And the Bible says in verse number 10, and the disciples went, then the disciples went away unto their own home.
point_summary: While the disciples returned home, Mary's lingering grief at the empty tomb became the occasion for Christ's first personal post-resurrection appearance, demonstrating how he ministers individually to his own.

context: But Mary stood without at the sepulcher weeping. And as she wept, she stooped down and looked into the sepulcher,
point_summary: Mary's grief at the empty tomb—weeping and mistaking the risen Jesus for a gardener—frames his personal appearance to her as the pattern by which Christ ministers to individuals in their sorrow.

context: and seeth two angels in white sitting, the one at the head and the other at the feet, where the body of Jesus had lain,
point_summary: Mary's encounter with two angels at the empty tomb (v. 12) sets the scene for Christ's personal appearance to her—demonstrating how the risen Jesus ministers individually to grieving, seeking hearts.

context: They said unto her, woman, why weepest thou? She saith unto them, because they have taken away my Lord, and I know not where they have laid him.
point_summary: Mary's grief over a missing Lord—"they have taken away my Lord"—is resolved not by argument but by Christ personally speaking her name, showing how the risen Jesus ministers to individuals in their sorrow.

context: When she had thus said, she turned herself back and saw Jesus standing. But notice what the Bible says, and knew not that it was Jesus.
point_summary: Mary's failure to recognize the risen Jesus until he spoke her name illustrates that Christ personally and intimately reveals himself to his grieving people.

context: Jesus saith unto her, woman, why weepest thou? Whom seekest thou? She, Mary, supposing him to be the gardener, saith unto him, sir, if thou have borne him, hence tell me where thou hast laid him, and I will take him away.
point_summary: Mary's failure to recognize the risen Jesus until he speaks her name illustrates how Christ personally and intimately ministers to grieving individuals in their moment of deepest loss.

context: And then Jesus says her name, Mary, Mary.
point_summary: Christ's personal address of Mary by name illustrates that the risen Jesus knows and calls his own individually, ministering to her grief with a single word.

context: And the Bible says that she turned herself and saith unto him, Rabboni, which is to say, master.
point_summary: The risen Christ's personal address of Mary by name reveals that he knows each believer individually, transforming her grief into recognition and worship.

context: Jesus saith unto her, touch me not, for I am not yet ascended to my father, but go to my brethren and say unto them, I ascend unto my father and your father, and to my God and your God.
point_summary: Christ's personal call of Mary by name in the garden illustrates that even in grief's blindness, the risen Lord knows and addresses his own individually.

context: Mary Magdalene came and told the disciples that she had seen the Lord and that he had spoken these things unto her.
point_summary: Mary Magdalene's report to the disciples—that she had seen the risen Lord and heard his words—is framed as the fruit of Jesus personally calling her by name amid her grief and confusion.

context: We dropped off with Mary in verse number 16 and following, we really read all the way down to verse 18.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We're going to pick up in verse 19 and look at the disciples that were gathered together.
point_summary: The risen Christ's appearance to the fearful, door-locked disciples on resurrection evening—passing through shut doors to stand among them and speak peace—demonstrates the reality and bodily power of His resurrection.

context: The Bible says then the same day at evening in verse 19 of John 20, being the first day of the week when the doors were shut, where the disciples were assembled for fear of the Jews, came Jesus and stood in the midst and saith unto them, peace be unto you.
point_summary: On the evening of the resurrection, the risen Christ appeared bodily to his fearful, grieving disciples behind shut doors, greeting them with peace, showing his wounds as proof of identity, and commissioning them as the Father had sent him.

context: And when he had so said, he showed unto them his hands and his side. Then were the disciples glad when they saw the Lord.
point_summary: The risen Christ's appearance to the assembled disciples — showing his hands and side — transformed their fear and grief into gladness, confirming the resurrection to those gathered together.

context: Then said Jesus to them again, peace be unto you. unto you, as my Father hath sent me, even so send I you.
point_summary: The risen Christ's commissioning of the disciples — "as my Father hath sent me, even so send I you" — is presented as a foundational message of instruction that anticipates the Great Commission given later in Galilee.

context: and when he had said thus, he breathed on them, and saith unto them, Receive the Holy Ghost,
point_summary: Christ's post-resurrection breathing on the disciples and command to receive the Holy Spirit is interpreted not as granting personal authority to forgive sins, but as commissioning them to preach the gospel, which alone holds the power of forgiveness.

context: and whosoever sins you remit, they are remitted unto them, and whosoever sins you retain, they are retained.
point_summary: The authority to remit or retain sins given in John 20:23 is interpreted as the disciples' commission to preach the gospel, which itself carries the power to forgive sin.

context: But Thomas, notice verse 24, one of the 12, called Didymus, was not with them when Jesus came.
point_summary: Thomas's absence from the gathered disciples when Jesus appeared serves as a pointed reminder that missing the assembly means missing what God does among his people.

context: The other disciples therefore said unto him, We have seen the Lord. But he said unto them, Except I shall see in his hands the print of the nails, and put my finger into the print of the nails, and thrust my hand into his side, I will not believe.
point_summary: Thomas's demand to physically verify the nail prints and wounded side before believing illustrates the skepticism the risen Christ would soon personally confront and overcome.

context: Thomas says, I will not believe.
point_summary: Thomas's demand for physical proof before believing mirrors a persistent human tendency to require direct divine encounter rather than trusting the written Word of God.

context: after eight days again, the disciples were within. And notice these words, and Thomas was with them. I guess so. After he missed it, he was with them. The Bible says, then came Jesus, the doors being shut, and stood in the midst and said, Peace be unto you.
point_summary: Christ's gracious return to Thomas eight days later—doors shut, appearing personally to address the doubter's exact conditions—demonstrates that God's faithfulness to the individual is not contingent on the individual's faith.

context: And notice this in verse 27. Just like in Mark's gospel, when it says, tell the disciples, and Peter, again, our God's an individual God.
point_summary: Christ's individual grace toward Thomas — meeting his specific doubts with specific, physical proof — demonstrates that God deals with each person personally, not merely collectively.

context: reach hither thy finger, and behold my hands. Reach hither thy hand, and thrust it into my side, and be not faithless, but believing.
point_summary: Christ's individual care is seen in his direct, personal address to Thomas—meeting his specific doubt with a specific invitation to touch the risen body.

context: Thomas answered and said unto Him, My Lord and my God.
point_summary: Thomas's personal encounter with the risen Christ — touching His wounds and confessing "My Lord and my God" — is presented as evidence that Jesus individually meets doubters where they are.

context: Jesus saith unto him, Thomas, because thou hast seen me, thou hast believed. Blessed are they that have not seen, and yet have believed.
point_summary: Faith in the risen Christ without physical sight is declared blessed, as Jesus' words to Thomas extend that blessing to all who believe through the written testimony of Scripture.

context: Many other signs truly to Jesus in the presence of His disciples, which are not written in this book.
point_summary: John's stated purpose — that these selected signs are written so readers might believe Jesus is the Christ and receive life through His name — frames the resurrection appearances as sufficient, purposeful testimony rather than exhaustive record.

context: these things are written that you might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that believing you might have life through His name.
point_summary: John's stated purpose — that these resurrection accounts are written so readers may believe Jesus is the Christ and receive life through His name — grounds the entire sermon's call to faith in the risen Lord.

context: he must increase, but I must decrease
point_summary: John 3:30 anchored the 2014 church theme—"He must increase, but I must decrease"—framing the entire year's study of John's Gospel around Christ's supremacy over self.

context: Mary says to those servants, whatsoever he saith unto you, do it.
point_summary: Obedience to Christ's word is simply and completely captured in Mary's instruction to the servants: "whatsoever he saith unto you, do it."

context: John 13 verse 1 speaks about, He loved them unto the end.
point_summary: Jesus's love for his disciples "unto the end" is held up as a perfect love that believers are called to emulate by continuing to love people, even enemies.

reference_text: For God so loved the world
context: For God so loved the world. I'm thankful that the truth of God's word is that God is not willing that any should perish
point_summary: God's love is described as universal because "God so loved the world," meaning every person encountered daily is loved by God regardless of their sinfulness.

context: Now before the feast of the Passover, when Jesus knew that his hour was come, that he should depart out of this world unto the Father, having loved his own which were in the world, notice this phrase, he loved them unto the end.
point_summary: The phrase "he loved them unto the end" carries a double meaning: love persisting to the very last moment and love extended to the uttermost, leaving nothing lacking.

context: and we find here that in John 13 and verse number 1, and before we get into the thoughts of the message this morning, I want you to understand as John is writing this, again under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit of God, and following the timeline of the life and ministry of the Lord Jesus Christ, that at this particular time, the Bible reveals to us, notice in verse number 1 again, when Jesus knew that his hour was come.
point_summary: John's phrase "he loved them unto the end" carries the double meaning of loving them to the very last and loving them to the uttermost—completely, with nothing lacking.

context: If you're not very familiar with the book of John, the gospel of John, it's important for us to understand that this phrase about God's hour, his hour, is repeated throughout the gospel of John. You find that it begins in John chapter number 2,
point_summary: John 2's account of the first miracle at Cana introduces the recurring "hour" motif in the Gospel of John, marking the beginning of a timeline that moves toward Christ's glorification and departure.

context: John 2 reveals to us on that very first time in the gospel of John with regard to this concept, this idea of the hour, and it says his hour was not yet come.
point_summary: John 2's account of the first miracle at Cana marks the earliest point in the Gospel where "his hour was not yet come," establishing a progressive timeline that culminates in John 13:1 when that hour finally arrives.

context: John chapter 12 and verse number 23, it says, and Jesus answered them saying, the hour is come that the Son of Man shall be glorified
point_summary: John 12:23 marks the pivotal turning point in the gospel's "hour" motif, where Jesus declares the hour has finally come—the same hour repeatedly deferred from John 2 onward—signaling his imminent departure through the cross.

context: John 13 and verse number 1, the Bible says when Jesus knew that his hour was come, notice what he knew, that he should depart out of this world unto the Father.
point_summary: The phrase "loved them unto the end" carries a double meaning—perseverance to the very last and love that is utterly complete—expressed at the precise moment Jesus knew his hour of departure through the cross had come.

context: He gives that promise in John chapter number 14 and verse number 1, down through verse number 3
point_summary: Christ's promise to return, referenced in John 14:1–3, is cited as the assurance given to disciples whom Jesus was preparing for his imminent departure to the cross and ascension.

context: He gives that promise in John chapter number 14 and verse number 1, down through verse number 3
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He gives that promise in John chapter number 14 and verse number 1, down through verse number 3
point_summary: Christ's promise to come again (John 14:1–3) is cited as the assurance given to disciples whom Jesus was preparing for his departure, not developed further in this segment.

context: As you study through this portion of the gospel of John, you eventually come to chapter number 17, where the Bible simply declares it this way, Father, the hour is come
point_summary: John 17:1's "Father, the hour is come" is presented as the culmination of a progressive theme tracking Christ's appointed hour from Cana to the cross.

context: the Holy Spirit led the writer, John, to write these words that he, Christ, loved them unto the end. Completely. To the uttermost. To the very last. I'm going to think about this subject of God's great love for us. in this truth here in John 13 in verse number one, that he loved them unto the end.
point_summary: Christ's love for his own, declared in John 13:1 as complete and unto the end, is not measured by possessions or circumstances but is settled in his own word and demonstrated at the cross.

context: Let me remind you of what John says. Turn back to John. You might still be there in chapter 13. Look over in verse number 23.
point_summary: John's self-description as "the disciple whom Jesus loved" is cited as a biblical example of reminding oneself of God's personal love, a practice the sermon urges every believer to imitate.

context: when Jesus had thus said, he was troubled in spirit and testified, and said, verily, verily, I say unto you that one of you shall betray me.
point_summary: Even amid the revelation of betrayal, John identifies himself not by name but as "the disciple whom Jesus loved," modeling the practice of grounding one's personal identity in the assurance of Christ's love.

context: here we find that Jesus is there on the cross of Calvary... When Jesus therefore saw his mother and the disciples standing by whom he loved,
point_summary: Even while dying on the cross, Jesus identified John as "the disciple whom he loved," demonstrating that his love for his own extended to the very end of his earthly life.

context: he said unto his mother, woman, behold thy son. Again, talking about John. He saw that disciple. He said, and the disciples standing by whom he loved.
point_summary: Even at the cross, amid his own suffering, Jesus' love for John was expressed in personal care—identifying him as "the disciple whom he loved" while entrusting his mother to that same beloved disciple.

context: In chapter 20, as it's the truth of the resurrection, Jesus has been crucified, he's been buried, and the first day of the week comes, and the Bible tells us in verse number one that Mary Magdalene went early down to the tomb, and it was dark, and unto the sepulcher, and seeth the stone taken away from the sepulcher.
point_summary: John 20:1 is cited as one of several passages where John identifies himself as "the disciple whom Jesus loved," illustrating his personal appropriation of Christ's love as a model for believers.

context: You can read it in chapter 21, in verse number seven, when they're out there on the ship. Remember, Peter said, I go a-fishing.
point_summary: John's repeated self-identification as "the disciple whom Jesus loved" across multiple Gospel scenes—including John 21:7—serves as a personal reminder that Jesus's love is individually directed, not merely general.

context: And Jesus appears on the ship, and he calls out in verse number five, children, have ye any meat?
point_summary: John's repeated self-identification as "the disciple whom Jesus loved" frames Jesus' question in John 21:5 within a relationship of personal, named love—illustrating that Christ's care is individual, not merely collective.

context: therefore, that disciple whom Jesus loved, saith unto Peter, it is the Lord.
point_summary: John's self-identification as "the disciple whom Jesus loved" at the moment of recognizing the risen Lord illustrates how awareness of Christ's personal love shapes the believer's recognition of and response to him.

context: You can look at verse 20 of John chapter 21, but we'll keep moving along.
point_summary: John identifies himself repeatedly as "the disciple whom Jesus loved," a self-designation the sermon highlights as a model of personally appropriating Christ's love, as Paul similarly did in Galatians 2.

context: Turn back to the book of John, if you will, in the 11th chapter. In John chapter number 11.
point_summary: John 11 is introduced as a narrative illustration of Jesus's love in action, with Lazarus's death and his sisters' grief framing the broader sermon theme that God loves his people.

context: now a certain man was sick named Lazarus of Bethany, the town of Mary and her sister Martha
point_summary: John 11:1 introduces Lazarus's illness as the occasion for Mary and Martha's appeal to Jesus, framed pointedly as "he whom thou lovest is sick"—demonstrating that they approached Christ on the basis of his known love, not merely Lazarus's need.

context: Was that Mary which anointed the Lord with ointment and wiped his feet with her hair, whose brother Lazarus was sick?
point_summary: John 11:2 identifies Mary by her act of anointing Jesus, establishing her as a known figure whose family Jesus personally loved before the crisis of Lazarus's death unfolds.

context: therefore his sister sent unto him, saying, Lord
point_summary: Mary and Martha's message—"he whom thou lovest is sick"—reveals that their appeal to Jesus was grounded not merely in Lazarus's need but in Jesus' own love for him.

reference_text: this sickness is not unto death, but for the glory of God, that the Son of God might be glorified thereby
context: this sickness is not unto death, but for the glory of God, that the Son of God might be glorified thereby.
point_summary: Lazarus's sickness, declared by Christ in John 11:4 to be for God's glory rather than unto death, illustrates that dire circumstances do not negate God's love—a love evident to family, disciples, and onlooking Jews alike.

reference_text: now Jesus loved Martha and her sister and Lazarus
context: now Jesus loved Martha and her sister and Lazarus.
point_summary: John 11:5's explicit statement that Jesus loved Martha, her sister, and Lazarus grounds the assurance that God's love remains real and personal even when difficult circumstances tempt believers to doubt it.

context: The Bible says that the Jews that were gathered there in verse number 36 said, behold, how he loved him.
point_summary: Even onlookers at Lazarus's tomb recognized and declared Christ's love, showing that his love was evident not only to those closest to him but to all who witnessed it.

context: for God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son
point_summary: God's gift of his Son in John 3:16 is cited as the defining proof of divine love underlying Romans 8:32's declaration that he "delivered him up for us all."

context: John 8 and verse number 28, the same chapter that we're looking in, the verses that precede what we just looked at
point_summary: God's unchanging goodness, grounded in Romans 8:28, assures believers that even life's messiest circumstances are being worked together for good by a God whose love remains constant.

context: Jesus boldly declared, I am the way. The one and only way.
point_summary: Jesus's use of the definite article — "the way," not "a way" — is pressed to exclude all alternative paths to God and establish Christ as the sole means of redemption for sinners separated from Him.

context: We're familiar with the verse in John 3, 16, for God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son that whosoever believeth in Him should not perish, but have everlasting life.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus says to Nicodemus, ye must be born again.
point_summary: New birth is defined here as spiritual regeneration distinct from physical birth, with Jesus' words to Nicodemus used to refute any equation of baptism with being born again.

context: Nicodemus in a time of confusion and uncertainty as to what does this mean to be born again? He says this, how can a man be born again when he's old right?
point_summary: Nicodemus's confused, literal question about re-entering the womb illustrates that "born again" means a distinct spiritual birth, not a physical or ceremonial act like baptism.

context: Jesus says except a man be born of water, physical birth, and of the Spirit, second birth, being born again.
point_summary: Being born again means spiritual rebirth by the Spirit—not baptism—as Jesus clarifies to Nicodemus by distinguishing physical birth (water) from the second, Spirit-wrought birth.

context: to Nicodemus, ye must be born again. And Nicodemus inquires and says, how can a man be born when he's old?
point_summary: Being born again means spiritual rebirth by the Spirit—not baptism—as Jesus clarified to Nicodemus, whose confusion prompted the distinction between physical and second birth.

context: And Nicodemus inquires and says, how can a man be born when he's old?
point_summary: Nicodemus's literal, earthly misreading of "born again" is used to refute the claim that baptism constitutes the new birth Jesus required.

context: And Jesus says you need to be baptized. No, that's not what Jesus said.
point_summary: Being born again means spiritual rebirth by the Spirit—not water baptism—as Jesus clarified to Nicodemus, whose confusion prompted the distinction between physical birth and the second birth.

context: I will come again
point_summary: Christ's promise "I will come again" is cited as the paradigmatic unconditional promise—one requiring no human condition to be met, fulfilled solely at God's appointed time.

context: Jesus was breaking down those barriers because in John chapter number 4, he said, I must needs go through Samaria.
point_summary: Jesus's deliberate passage through Samaria illustrates his intentional dismantling of Jewish-Gentile hostility, providing the redemptive backdrop for understanding why tongues served as a sign to a sign-seeking Jewish people.

context: Why? Because for God so loved the world.
point_summary: The universal scope of God's love in John 3:16 is invoked to explain why Jesus deliberately crossed Jewish-Gentile barriers, breaking down the disdain that made tongues as a sign to Israel necessary.

context: Jesus said, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: John 14:6 is cited to confirm that salvation comes exclusively through Christ's name, reinforcing Acts 10:43's declaration that "whosoever believeth in him shall receive remission of sins."

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth and the life. No man cometh unto the father, but by me.
point_summary: Jesus Christ as the exclusive way of salvation is affirmed by citing John 14:6, underscoring that Cornelius's conversion confirms one way—not many—to the Father.

context: John says that if all the things were written, the world itself couldn't contain the books.
point_summary: John's acknowledgment that the world could not contain all the books recording Christ's deeds underscores that the gospel accounts preserve only a portion of His earthly ministry, yet sufficient to trace the pattern of His walk.

context: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God
point_summary: John 1:1 is cited as the direct answer to the religious leaders' question about Jesus' authority—he acts by divine right because he is the Creator God, the Word who was with God and was God.

context: We find in John chapter seven, turn over if you will, and I want you to see in John chapter number seven, we looked at this passage a couple of weeks ago, and as we talked about the wisdom of the Lord,
point_summary: Jesus's declaration that "my time has not yet come" in John 7 illustrates his deliberate, sovereign control over his own movements and timing, contrasted with his brothers whose time was always ready.

context: then Jesus said to them, my time has not yet come, but your time is always ready
point_summary: Christ's declaration that "my time has not yet come" illustrates the purposeful, wise, and courageous dimensions of his walk as interwoven qualities, not independent virtues.

context: go ye up unto the feast. I go not up yet unto this feast, for my time is not yet full come
point_summary: Jesus' deliberate delay in going to the feast—going later and secretly rather than openly—illustrates that His courage was inseparable from wisdom and purpose, with each quality woven together in how He walked.

context: when he had said these words in verse number nine unto them, he abode still in Galilee
point_summary: Christ's deliberate delay in Galilee (v. 9) illustrates how courage, wisdom, and purposefulness are inseparably woven together in his walk—not isolated virtues but a unified pattern.

context: Jesus walked in Galilee, for he would not walk in Jewry because the Jews sought to kill him.
point_summary: Jesus deliberately avoided Judea because the Jews sought to kill him, yet still attended the feast as required by law, demonstrating courage by teaching openly in the temple despite the mortal threat.

context: about the midst of the feast, he went up into the temple and he taught.
point_summary: Despite knowing the Jews sought to kill him, Jesus publicly entered the temple at the feast and taught, demonstrating courage in fulfilling his duty regardless of personal danger.

context: the Jews marveled, saying, how knoweth this man letters, having never learned?
point_summary: Jesus's formal education was unverifiable to the Jews, yet His authoritative temple teaching prompted their astonishment and His own declaration that His doctrine originated not with Himself but with the Father who sent Him.

context: Jesus answered them and said, my doctrine is not mine, but his that sent me.
point_summary: Christ's bold public teaching in the temple, despite threats on his life, is presented as the courage that flows from speaking not his own doctrine but the Father's.

context: If any man will do his will, he shall know of the doctrine, whether it be of God or whether I speak of myself.
point_summary: Willingness to do God's will is the condition Jesus sets for recognizing whether His doctrine is from God or from Himself.

context: He that speaketh of himself seeketh his own glory, but he that seeketh his glory that sent him, the same is true and no unrighteousness is in him.
point_summary: Seeking God's glory rather than one's own is the mark of truthfulness, as Jesus demonstrates by teaching openly in the temple despite mortal danger, subordinating self-preservation to the Father's mission.

context: did not Moses give you the law
point_summary: Jesus confronts his accusers directly with their own lawbreaking, exposing the contradiction of those who claim Moses' law yet plot murder in violation of it.

context: receives circumcision, that the law of Moses should not be broken. Are you angry at me because I have made a man every whit whole on the Sabbath day?
point_summary: Jesus' healing of a man on the Sabbath is defended by his own logic: if circumcision lawfully makes one member whole on the Sabbath, how much more should making a man entirely whole be permitted.

context: Judge not according to the appearance, but judge righteous judgment.
point_summary: Facing those who sought to kill him, Jesus nonetheless taught publicly and confronted his accusers directly, calling them to judge by righteous judgment rather than outward appearance.

context: Now Jesus knew all things.
point_summary: Knowing full well that arrest and death awaited Him, Jesus stepped forward to meet those who came for Him, demonstrating deliberate, courageous submission to the Father's plan.

context: Jesus in John 17 said the hour has come.
point_summary: Jesus' declaration in John 17:1 that "the hour has come" demonstrates that he entered his arrest and passion with full foreknowledge, having deliberately prepared his disciples for what he knew was coming.

context: We find that the Bible says in verse one, when Jesus had spoken these words, that's chapter 17, the hour's come, so on and so forth, he went forth with his disciples over the Beuchatron, where was a garden into the which he entered and his disciples.
point_summary: Jesus' deliberate return to a place Judas knew demonstrates that he went to the cross with full foreknowledge and sovereign control, not as a victim overtaken by circumstances.

context: In John chapter 18 and verse number five, they say that they're seeking Jesus of Nazareth and Jesus said unto them, I am.
point_summary: Jesus's self-identification as "I am" — with *he* italicized and absent from the Greek — links his arrest-scene declaration directly to the divine name revealed in Exodus and his earlier claim in John 8:58.

context: What did Jesus say in John chapter number eight, when he says, before Abraham was, I am.
point_summary: Jesus' self-identification as "I am" in John 8:58 echoes the divine name of Exodus, establishing his deity and grounding the courage with which he faced his betrayers in Gethsemane.

context: he goes to these group of people and he says, I am. And Judas also, which betrayed him, stood with them.
point_summary: When Jesus answered "I am" (omitting the italicized "he" of the KJV) in John 18:5, his divine self-identification—echoing Exodus and John 8—caused his arresters to fall backward, demonstrating the courage and sovereign authority with which Christ walked toward the cross.

context: as soon then, in verse number six, as he said unto them, I am, they went backward and fell to the ground.

context: Jesus says in verse number 8, he says, if I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me.

context: I go to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto myself.

context: He said that where I am, there ye may be also.

context: The Bible says that Jesus, the word, was made flesh and dwelt among us

context: The Bible says in John 16 that he convicts and he convinces, what, of sin.

context: Paul didn't hear the voice when Jesus was speaking to the disciples in John 14, but he understood the truth.
point_summary: Paul's confidence in Christ's return rested on the promise Jesus made to His disciples in John 14, a promise Paul received not firsthand but through direct revelation from Christ.

context: John uses this little phrase at the end of John chapter number two that we would be ashamed at Him at His appearing.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.

context: God so loved the world, and we'll look at that in just a moment

context: we come to where we are with John and first John

context: turned to the Gospel of John
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus answered and said unto him, verily, verily, I say unto thee

reference_text: except a man be born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God
context: except a man be born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God
point_summary: Nicodemus's religious credentials could not save him, illustrating that new birth—not religion—is the universal requirement Jesus declares for entering the kingdom of God.

context: He tells him in verse number seven, marvel not that I said unto thee, ye must be born again

reference_text: for God so loved the world

context: he gave his only begotten son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life

context: for God sent not his son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world through him might be saved

context: for God so loved the world

context: greater love hath no man than this that a man lay down his life for his friends

context: John chapter number 13 begins in verse number 1 by talking about he loved them unto the end

context: John 13 and verse number 23. The Bible says, now there was leaning on Jesus's bosom one of his disciples, then notice this, whom Jesus loved

point_summary: John's repeated self-identification as "the disciple whom Jesus loved" illustrates the sermon's emphasis that God's love is personal and individual, not merely collective.

context: then Peter turning about, seeth the disciple whom Jesus loved following, which also leaned on his breast at supper and said, Lord, which is he, which is he that betrayeth thee?
point_summary: John's repeated self-identification as "the disciple whom Jesus loved" illustrates that God's love is personal and individual, not merely collective.

context: you must be born again

context: He came unto His own, and His own received Him not

context: as many as received Him, to them gave He power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on His name

context: He came to His own, but His own received Him not

context: But as many as received Him

context: as many as received Him, to them gave He power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on His name
point_summary: Receiving Christ—choosing to do so on this side of eternity—is what moves a person from verse 11's rejection into verse 12's gift of becoming a son of God.

context: I know that God so loved the world

context: He said, you must be born again

context: let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in me

context: In my father's house are many mansions. If it were not so, I would have told you

context: I go to prepare a place for you, and if I go, I will come again and receive you unto myself, that where I am, there ye may be also
point_summary: Christ's promise to return and receive believers unto himself grounds the "confident expectation" that every born-again believer will see Jesus and be with him forever.

context: John 14, verses one through three

context: I'm going to come back

context: except a man be born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God

context: marvel not that I say unto thee, ye must be born again

context: behold the Lamb of God which taketh away the sin of the world

context: For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life

context: no man taketh it from me, but I lay it down

point_summary: John 8:33's record of some hearers claiming freedom from bondage while others believed illustrates that every person who encounters Christ's teaching faces a genuine, immediate choice to receive or reject him.

context: he came to his own and his own received him not

context: as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name
point_summary: Receiving Christ is a genuine individual choice—some believe and are given power to become sons of God, while others reject or defer, and both responses carry eternal consequence.

context: in John 8, was not to be delivered from the bondage of the Romans, it was the bondage of sin

context: that I say unto thee, ye must be born again

point_summary: Rejecting the Son carries an eternal consequence: the wrath of God does not merely threaten but already abides on the unbeliever, meaning condemnation is a present reality, not only a future one.

context: He that believeth not the Son shall not see life, but the wrath of God abideth on him

reference_text: he that believeth on the Son hath everlasting life

context: in verse number 27, my sheep hear my voice and I know them and they follow me

context: and I give unto them eternal life, and they shall never perish

context: if you continue my word, then are you my disciples indeed and you shall know the truth and the truth shall make you free

context: whosoever committeth sin is the servant of sin

context: If the son therefore shall make you free, you shall be free indeed
point_summary: The freedom Christ gives—unlike civil liberty—is inviolable, incapable of being taken away by any human power, making it more precious than any national freedom.

context: he came to his own and his own received him

context: I am a branch. But the branch needs to be attached to the vine.

context: in John chapter number four, and we'll finish there this morning, but look in John chapter number four

context: There are many references between John 2 and John 17 of the hour
point_summary: John 2:1 introduces the miracle at Cana as a backdrop for Mary's command in verse 5—"whatsoever he saith unto you, do it"—illustrating that knowing God's word and obeying it are entirely different things.

context: There are many references between John 2 and John 17 of the hour

context: whatsoever he saith unto you, do it

context: Turn to John one
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the next day, John seeth Jesus coming unto him. Notice what he says, behold, the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration — "Behold, the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world" — is presented as evidence that God's eternal plan was always for Christ to die and pay for sin with his blood.

context: in verse 36, again, as John the Baptist sees
point_summary: John the Baptist's repeated declaration — "Behold, the Lamb of God" — is cited as evidence that Christ's atoning death was always the predetermined plan of God.

context: the next day in verse 35 and verse 36
point_summary: John the Baptist's repeated declaration "Behold, the Lamb of God" on successive days underscores that Christ's atoning death was always the predetermined plan of God.

context: looking upon Jesus as he walked, he saith, behold, the Lamb of God
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration identifies Jesus as the sacrificial Lamb whose death would be the means of taking away the world's sin.

context: they asked for a sign in verse 18
point_summary: When the Jews demanded a sign in John 2:18, Jesus answered by pointing to his own death and resurrection—the destruction and raising of the temple of his body—as the ultimate sign.

context: Jesus in verse 19 answered and said unto them, destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up
point_summary: Christ's prediction "destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up" is shown to refer to his body—his crucifixion and resurrection—as confirmed by verse 21.

context: Then said the Jews, 40 and six years was this temple in building and wilt thou rear it up in three days
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: but notice verse 21, the Bible says, but he spake of the temple of his body
point_summary: Jesus' reference to "the temple of his body" is read as a veiled prediction of his crucifixion and bodily resurrection three days later, confirming that the cross was always God's plan.

context: we find this plan of the Lord, the plan of God in John chapter number 10
point_summary: Christ's self-identification as the Good Shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep (v. 14–15) is cited as part of the unfolding Gospel of John testimony that the cross was always God's plan.

context: I am the good shepherd and know my sheep and am known of mine
point_summary: Christ's death was entirely voluntary—no man took His life, but He laid it down of Himself, with power to take it again, in order to redeem mankind and provide remission of sins.

context: As the father knoweth me, even so know I the father and I lay down my life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's death on the cross was entirely voluntary—he laid down his life of his own power and authority, for the redemption of mankind.

context: And other sheep I have which are not of this fold, them also I must bring. They shall hear my voice and there shall be one fold and one shepherd
point_summary: Christ's voluntary death as the Good Shepherd—laying down his life willingly for sheep beyond one fold—underscores that the cross was a deliberate, self-chosen act of redemption, not a death imposed upon him.

context: Therefore doth my father love me, why? Because I lay down my life that I might take it again
point_summary: Christ's death on the cross was entirely voluntary—he laid down his life of his own power, with authority also to take it again, in order to redeem mankind.

context: No man taketh it from me, but I lay it down of myself. I have power to lay it down and I have power to take it again. This commandment have I received of my father
point_summary: Christ's death on the cross was entirely voluntary—no man took his life from him; he laid it down of himself, with power both to die and to rise again.

context: We find that in John chapter number 18
point_summary: Christ's death on the cross was entirely voluntary, as John 10:18 makes plain—a truth John 18:11 is introduced to further confirm.

context: then said Jesus unto Peter, put up thy sword into thy sheath. The cup which my father hath given me, shall I not drink it?
point_summary: Christ's willing acceptance of "the cup" in John 18:11 demonstrates that his death was entirely voluntary — the predetermined plan of God, not a fate imposed upon him.

context: when Jesus said, it is finished. It is finished, hallelujah.
point_summary: Christ's cry "It is finished" at Calvary declared the sin debt of humanity paid in full, the culmination of his voluntary, purposeful death on the cross.

context: While Jesus has already said it is finished, it's paid in full, he's already paid for it with his blood.
point_summary: Christ's declaration "It is finished" (John 19:30) is presented as the definitive payment for sin in full, rendering all human attempts at self-atonement unnecessary and unaccepted.

context: I am the way, the truth and the life. No man cometh unto the father, but by me.
point_summary: God determined before the foundation of the world that there is only one way of salvation — through Christ alone — as Jesus himself declared in John 14:6.

context: he came to his own, and his own received him not, but as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name
point_summary: Christ's atoning payment must be personally received, as John 1:11–12 shows: he came to his own who rejected him, yet those who individually receive him are granted sonship.

context: For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son
point_summary: God's love, demonstrated in giving his Son, is offered freely to all—not earned by human effort or moral achievement.

context: that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life
point_summary: God's love expressed in giving his Son is cited as the basis for the gospel offer — that salvation is a free gift received by faith, not earned by anything a person can do.

context: In John chapter number 17, the Bible tells us of the glory that he had in heaven
point_summary: The pre-incarnate glory Christ possessed in heaven, referenced in John 17, frames why his willingness to leave that throne room makes the purpose of his birth so weighty.

context: John is speaking about the Lamb of God, he talks about the one that is after him is preferred above him
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son. We read that in John chapter 3, in verse number 16, that whosoever believeth in Him should not perish, but have everlasting life
point_summary: God's gift of His only begotten Son, cited from John 3:16, is presented as the expression of divine love that makes possible the redemption—the buying back—of sinners held under the law.

context: the one that said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me
point_summary: The law's role as schoolmaster, pointing sinners to their need for salvation, culminates in Christ alone as the way, the truth, and the life—the only one who can give life and bring men to the Father.

context: in John chapter number 1, the Bible says in verse number 11, that he came unto his own, and his own received him not. But as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name
point_summary: John 1:11–12 grounds the sermon's point that adoption into God's family comes not by law-keeping but by personally receiving Christ, who gives power to become sons of God.

context: in the world you shall have tribulation, but in me you have peace
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the tribulation that Jesus speaks of in John 16 and verse number 33
point_summary: The tribulation described in Revelation is distinguished from the ordinary persecution Jesus promised believers in John 16:33, arguing the sealed judgments represent something categorically different than the church's historic suffering.

context: let me take you over, hold your place here, but look in John chapter 19, if you will
point_summary: Pilate's declaration that he held power over Christ, met by Jesus's correction that such authority could only come from above, grounds the sermon's claim that God ultimately authorizes the red horse rider's power.

context: then came Jesus forth wearing the crown of thorns and the purple robe. And Pilate saith unto them, behold the man.
point_summary: Pilate's claim to hold power over Christ sets up the sermon's point that all governing authority — even over the Son of God — is ultimately derived from and permitted by God.

context: When the chief priests, therefore, and officers saw him, they cried out saying, crucify him, crucify him.
point_summary: Pilate's claim to hold power over Christ is used to illustrate that all governing authority—including that of the second seal's rider—is ultimately derived from and permitted by God.

context: The Jews answered him, we have a law. And by our law, he ought to die because he made himself the son of God.
point_summary: Pilate's claim to hold power over Christ is shown to be derivative, illustrating that all governing authority—including that of the second seal's rider—ultimately originates with God.

context: He came to his own and his own received him not.
point_summary: The Jews' rejection of Jesus as Messiah — "he came to his own and his own received him not" — is cited as background to explain why they pressed Pilate to crucify him.

context: Speakest thou not unto me? Knowest thou not that I have power to crucify thee and have power to release thee?
point_summary: Pilate's boast of sovereign power over Christ is corrected by Jesus himself, who declares that all such authority exists only because God granted it from above.

context: thou couldest have no power at all against me except it were given thee from above
point_summary: All governing authority—including the power to condemn or release Christ—originates from God above, not from any human office or official.

context: Jesus mentions that in chapter 4 and verse number 1
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: behold, the Lamb of God which taketh away the sin of the world
point_summary: Christ's authority to open the seals is grounded in his identity as the Lamb who came first to die, as John the Baptist declared in John 1:29, before returning as the Lion.

context: he came to his own and his own received him not
point_summary: Israel's national rejection of Jesus as Messiah—"he came to his own and his own received him not"—sets the stage for why the Jewish people remain open to receiving a false messiah, the Antichrist.

context: John chapter number one, it doesn't mean that no Jewish people got saved
point_summary: John 1's testimony that Christ "came to his own and his own received him not" grounds the sermon's argument that Israel's national rejection of Messiah leaves them vulnerable to accepting the Antichrist as a counterfeit in his place.

context: my sheep hear my voice, and they follow me and I give unto them eternal life and they shall never perish
point_summary: The personal assurance of John 10:27—that Christ's sheep hear his voice and receive eternal life—grounds the call to confirm that the Good Shepherd is *my* shepherd before entering the sermon's main theme.

context: Notice, if you will, in verse number 16 of John 14
point_summary: God's promise of another Comforter in John 14:16 is set against the disciples' unique, unrepeatable experience of walking personally with Christ during his earthly ministry.

reference_text: let not your heart be troubled
context: Jesus said this, let not your heart be troubled
point_summary: Jesus's command "let not your heart be troubled" (John 14:1) is presented as comfort spoken directly into the disciples' distress over his impending crucifixion.

context: verse number 16, and I will pray the Father and he shall give you another comforter that he may abide with you. What does it say? Forever.
point_summary: Christ's promise to send "another Comforter" who would abide forever is set against the disciples' unique but temporary experience of Christ's physical presence, showing that the Spirit's indwelling provides an enduring comfort no earthly proximity could.

context: Verse 17, as he continues, even the spirit of truth whom the world cannot receive because it seeth him not, neither knoweth him, but ye know him for he dwelleth with you and he shall be in you.
point_summary: The Holy Spirit, identified as the Spirit of truth who dwells within believers, is presented as the permanent divine Comforter sent to replace Christ's physical presence with his disciples.

context: In verse 18, the Bible says, I will not leave you comfortless, I will come to you.
point_summary: Christ's promise "I will not leave you comfortless" is linked directly to his sending of the Spirit, showing that the Comforter's coming is Christ himself coming to his people.

context: he's gonna send the comforter, I'll give you another comforter in verse 16
point_summary: Christ's promise of "another Comforter" who would abide with believers forever means that though only one generation walked with Jesus physically, every believer since receives the permanent indwelling presence of the Holy Spirit.

context: verse 18, he says, I will come to you
point_summary: Christ's promise "I will not leave you comfortless" is read alongside verse 16's promise of the Comforter as Trinitarian evidence that the Spirit's indwelling presence is Christ himself coming to his disciples.

context: Look, if you will, in chapter 16 and look at verse number seven. Again, as he continues to have this discourse with his disciples leading to his crucifixion, we find in verse number seven of chapter 16, it says, nevertheless, I tell you the truth. It is expedient for you that I go away.
point_summary: Christ's departure was not abandonment but necessity, because only through His going away would the Comforter be sent to permanently indwell every believer.

context: Jesus says it is expedient for you that I go away. Notice the rest of the verse, for if I go not away, the comforter will not come unto you, but if I depart, I will send him unto you.
point_summary: Christ's departure was necessary so that the Holy Spirit—the Comforter—could come to permanently indwell believers, a greater provision than his physical presence with the disciples.

context: Help us not to be troubled and to have anxiety and worry
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus Christ said that in John chapter 16 in verse number 33
point_summary: Christ's command to "be of good cheer" in the face of tribulation (John 16:33) is shown to be the same encouragement Paul received directly from the Lord when men vowed to kill him in Jerusalem.

context: there's going to be tribulations, there's going to be trials
point_summary: Christ's command to "be of good cheer" in the face of tribulation (John 16:33) is shown to be the same encouragement Paul receives directly from the Lord when men vow to kill him in Jerusalem.

context: these things I have spoken unto you, that in me you might have peace, in the world you shall have tribulation, but be of good cheer. I have overcome the world
point_summary: Christ's own command "be of good cheer" in John 16:33, given amid promised tribulation, is shown to be the identical greeting God spoke to Paul when his life was threatened in Jerusalem.

context: His sheep know my voice, and He knows them, and He says, I give unto them eternal life
point_summary: Eternal security grounds endurance in trials: believers are kept by God's power, held in Christ's hand and the Father's hand, so trials cannot destroy them.

context: he came to his own, but his own received him not
point_summary: Christ's universal offer of salvation is met by human choice, as John 1:11–12 illustrates that some reject him while others receive him and become sons of God.

context: But as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name
point_summary: Salvation is received or rejected by individual choice, as John 1:12 distinguishes those who receive Christ and are given power to become sons of God from those who do not.

context: John seven, between him and his brethren
point_summary: John 7's record of Jesus interacting with his brothers is cited as evidence that James likely heard Christ's earthly teaching firsthand, informing the epistle's themes.

context: Jesus Christ said it is finished
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I'm the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus alone gives life—not law-keeping—establishing that behavioral alignment with Scripture flows from salvation already secured, not from any effort to earn or maintain it.

context: The Lord Jesus Christ in his first miracle in John chapter number 2
point_summary: Mary's instruction to the servants at Cana — "whatsoever he saith unto you, do it" — is cited as a simple but demanding model of applying God's word in obedience, with the miracle itself being the fruit of their compliance.

context: whatsoever he saith unto you, do it
point_summary: Mary's instruction to the servants at Cana — "whatsoever he saith unto you, do it" — is cited as a simple yet demanding model of applying God's word in obedience, with the miracle itself being the fruit of their compliance.

context: we're in the world, but we're not of the world
point_summary: Being in the world but not of it—the believer's call to remain unspotted from the world—is grounded in Jesus's own prayer that his followers be kept from evil while remaining in the world.

context: keep them from the evil
point_summary: Being in the world but not of it — kept from evil by God's power — grounds the call to remain unspotted from the world as pure, observable religion.

context: Jesus in John chapter 13, what does he do? Well, he washes the feet of the disciples
point_summary: Keeping oneself unspotted from the world requires ongoing cleansing from daily defilement, illustrated by Jesus's distinction in John 13 between the once-for-all bath of salvation and the continual washing of the feet.

context: Jesus says he's that's washed, need not to be washed, but his feet only
point_summary: Salvation is a once-for-all cleansing, but daily contact with the world dirties the believer's "feet," requiring ongoing spiritual cleansing without repeating the new birth.

context: Jesus comes to Peter and Peter's like, no, no, no, no. And Jesus has an interaction with Peter and Peter says, not my feet only, but everywhere
point_summary: Salvation is a once-for-all cleansing, but daily contact with the world dirties the believer's "feet," requiring ongoing spiritual cleansing without repeating the new birth.

context: When I go into the world, I'm in the world, but I'm not of the world
point_summary: Being in the world without being of the world (John 17:16) means believers will encounter moral pollution that requires ongoing cleansing through God's Word, illustrated by Jesus washing the disciples' feet.

context: at the end of the 13th chapter of the book of John, that he gave them a new commandment
point_summary: John 13's "new commandment" to love one another as Christ loved is cited as the standard by which Paul's demonstrated love for the Corinthians is to be understood.

context: he gave them a new commandment. And that new commandment was that they love one another
point_summary: Christ's command that believers love one another as he loved them grounds Paul's demonstrated love for the Corinthians, establishing the standard by which genuine apostolic care is measured.

context: by this, by your love one for another, shall all men know that you're my disciples
point_summary: Loving one another as Christ loved is the visible mark by which all people recognize true disciples, a standard Paul applies to his own costly, unrequited love for the Corinthians.

context: Jesus said to him, ye must be born again
point_summary: Regeneration is the only remedy for the lost — not religion, rules, or good works — as Jesus' words to Nicodemus declare that new birth is an absolute necessity for salvation.

context: memorizing John 3.16 is wonderful
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the Bible says in John that without me you can do nothing
point_summary: Victory in the Christian life is not self-generated — citing John 15:5's "without me ye can do nothing," the sermon grounds all triumph over sin in God's working rather than human effort.

context: It is that God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life
point_summary: John 3:16 is cited as the gospel in summary form, equating its declaration of God's love and the offer of everlasting life through belief with the death, burial, and resurrection Paul outlines in 1 Corinthians 15:3–4.

context: In John chapter number two in verses 19 through 21 Jesus here is beginning his earthly ministry
point_summary: Christ's own words — "destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up" — are presented as a pre-ministry promise of resurrection certainty, spoken directly to his opponents and later understood as a prophecy of his bodily rising.

context: chapter one reveals twice where John the Baptist says behold the Lamb of God which taketh away the sin of the world
point_summary: John the Baptist's double declaration in John 1 — "Behold the Lamb of God" — is cited as narrative context establishing Jesus's identity before his own resurrection promise in John 2:19–21.

context: Jesus answered and said unto them destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up
point_summary: Jesus' own prediction — "destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up" — is presented as his deliberate, foreknown promise of death and bodily resurrection made before it occurred.

context: the Jews they said this forty and six years was this temple in building and wilt thou rear it up in three days
point_summary: Jesus' own prediction — "destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up" — is shown to be a deliberate, foreknown promise of his death and bodily resurrection, spoken before either event occurred.

context: John chapter number 10 the passage that deals with Jesus being our good shepherd
point_summary: Christ's self-identification as the Good Shepherd in John 10 is connected to the personal claim of Psalm 23:1, showing that eternal dwelling with God depends on whether the Lord is one's own shepherd.

context: the thief cometh not but for to steal and to kill and to destroy I am come they might have life and they might have it more abundantly
point_summary: Christ's self-identification as the Good Shepherd who gives abundant life is inseparable from his willingness to lay down his life for the sheep, linking the resurrection's gift of life to his voluntary death.

context: I am the good shepherd and the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's identity as the Good Shepherd is defined by his voluntary self-sacrifice, giving his life for the sheep, which the sermon connects to his purposeful death as the one born to die.

context: but he that is an hireling and not the shepherd whose own the sheep are not see it the wolf coming and leaveth the
point_summary: The hireling's abandonment of the sheep contrasts with Christ the Good Shepherd, who willingly lays down his life for them rather than fleeing.

context: sheep and fleeth and the wolf catcheth them and scattereth the sheep the hireling fleeth because he is an hireling and careth not for the sheep
point_summary: John 10:12 is cited in contrast to Christ as the good shepherd, who unlike the hireling willingly lays down his life for the sheep and takes it again, establishing his resurrection as an act of sovereign self-giving power.

context: I am the good shepherd and know my sheep and am known of mine the father knoweth me even so know I the father and I lay down my life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's self-identification as the good shepherd in John 10:14 is tied directly to his voluntary death and resurrection—he laid down his life for the sheep and had power to take it again.

context: the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's self-declared authority as the Good Shepherd—laying down his life voluntarily and taking it up again—grounds the resurrection not as something done to him but as his own sovereign act.

context: I laid down my life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's voluntary self-sacrifice—laying down his life for the sheep—is inseparable from his power to take it again, grounding the resurrection not as accident but as sovereign, self-willed act.

context: other sheep I have which are not of this fold them also I must bring they shall hear my voice and there shall be one fold and one shepherd
point_summary: Christ's sovereign authority over his own death is underscored in John 10:16–18, where he declares he lays down his life and takes it again of himself, establishing the resurrection as a deliberate act, not a defeat overcome.

context: therefore doth my father love me because I lay down my life
point_summary: Christ's own declaration that he had power both to lay down his life and to take it again grounds the resurrection not as something done to him but as his sovereign, self-initiated act.

context: His disciples in John chapter number 20. You'd look at the disciples as they're gathered here in John chapter number 20
point_summary: On resurrection evening, the disciples gathered behind shut doors — establishing the timeline and setting for Christ's post-resurrection appearance as proof of the risen Lord.

context: John chapter number 20, in verse number 19, the Bible says then the same day at evening
point_summary: On resurrection evening, the risen Christ appeared bodily to the assembled, fearful disciples, showing them his hands and side as tangible proof that he had truly conquered death.

context: being the first day of the week when the doors were shut, where the disciples were assembled for fear of the Jews
point_summary: On resurrection evening, the risen Christ appeared bodily to the assembled disciples behind shut doors, showing them his hands and side, transforming their fear into gladness.

context: came Jesus and stood in the midst and saith unto them, peace be unto you
point_summary: On resurrection evening, the risen Christ appeared bodily to the assembled disciples behind shut doors, showing them His hands and side, transforming their fear into gladness.

context: he showed unto them his hands and his side
point_summary: Christ's physical wounds—his hands and his side—shown to the gathered disciples on resurrection evening became the direct cause of their gladness, confirming the bodily reality of the resurrection.

context: Then were the disciples glad when they saw the Lord
point_summary: Christ's physical resurrection is confirmed as Jesus shows His disciples His wounded hands and side, turning their fear into gladness on that same resurrection evening.

context: As my father hath sent me, even so send I you
point_summary: Christ's resurrection commission — "as my Father hath sent me, even so send I you" — is presented as the risen Lord's direct charge to the gathered disciples on that same resurrection evening.

context: the Bible says in verse 24, but Thomas, one of the 12 called Didymus, was not with them when Jesus came
point_summary: Thomas's absence from the gathered disciples on resurrection evening is presented as an unfortunate failure to be where he should have been, setting up his subsequent unbelief.

context: the Bible says in verse 25, the other disciples therefore said unto him, we have seen the Lord, but he said unto them, except I shall see in his hands the print of the nails and put my finger into the print of the nails and thrust my hand into his side, I will not believe
point_summary: Thomas's absence from the gathered disciples and his demand for physical proof of the nail prints illustrate that unbelief persists even when eyewitness testimony of the risen Christ is given.

context: and after eight days again, his disciples were within, and notice what it says, and Thomas with them
point_summary: Thomas's absence at the first resurrection appearance and deliberate presence eight days later illustrates both the danger of unbelief and God's patient, pursuing grace toward the doubter.

context: then saith he to Thomas. He said, reach hither thy finger and behold my hands. And reach hither thy hand and thrust it into my side. And be not faithless, but believing
point_summary: Christ's personal, gracious address to doubting Thomas—inviting him to physically examine the wounds—demonstrates that God meets persistent unbelief with patient, tangible evidence rather than condemnation.

context: Thomas answered and said unto him, my Lord and my God
point_summary: Thomas's confession "My Lord and my God" is treated as direct evidence of Christ's deity, with Jesus's acceptance of that worship confirming he knew himself to be God.

context: Thomas, because thou hast seen me, thou hast believed. Blessed are they that have not seen and yet have believed.
point_summary: Faith in the risen Christ without physical sight is declared blessed, applied here to believers today who trust the scriptural record rather than firsthand witness.

context: he came to his own and his own received him not
point_summary: Israel's rejection of Jesus as Messiah is grounded in John 1:11, cited here as scriptural testimony that he came to his own people and was not received by them.

context: We read that in John chapter number one
point_summary: John 1's record that Christ "came to his own and his own received him not" is cited as scriptural confirmation that Israel rejected Jesus as her Messiah.

context: Pilate saith unto them, shall I crucify your king? And they said, we have no king but Caesar. And that's in John chapter 19 in verse number 15
point_summary: Israel's rejection of Jesus as Messiah is anchored in their own declaration before Pilate — "we have no king but Caesar" — confirming the nation's refusal to receive Christ in fulfillment of the prophecy that Messiah would be cut off.

context: You remember in John 9 when Jesus healed the man that was born blind?
point_summary: The healing of the man born blind in John 9 is invoked to illustrate that Israel's current condition is one of spiritual blindness—far graver than physical blindness—resulting from their rejection of Messiah.

context: Mary said this in John chapter number two to those servants in the marriage of Cana. She said, whatsoever he saith unto you, speaking about Jesus, do it.
point_summary: Mary's instruction to the servants at Cana — "whatsoever he saith unto you, do it" — is cited as a biblical pattern of unconditional obedience to Christ, applied to faithfulness in trials.

reference_text: I am the way, the truth, and the life
context: Jesus is truth. I am the way, the truth, and the life.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Sanctify them by thy word. Thy word is truth in John 17.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: when he stood at the tomb of Lazarus and said, Lazarus, come forth
point_summary: Lazarus's resurrection is cited as a later, known example of God's power over death—contrasting Abraham's faith, which trusted that same power with no prior precedent to draw from.

context: he came to his own, his own received him not
point_summary: Israel's rejection of Christ ("he came to his own, his own received him not") is cited to establish that the church and Israel are distinct entities, guarding against misreading 1 Peter 2's royal priesthood language as applying to Israel.

context: You ever read John 15? He expects for us to be fruitful
point_summary: God's expectation of fruitfulness in John 15 is invoked to reinforce that believers are to maintain good works not to earn salvation, but as the natural yield of being saved.

context: John 15 speaks much about that truth of abiding in him
point_summary: John 15's teaching on the vine and branches is invoked to illustrate the abiding fellowship with Christ that Paul's call to unity and peace in 2 Corinthians 13:11 is meant to protect and sustain.

context: go over to the book of John, if you will, John chapter number one
point_summary: The incarnation of Christ—the eternal Word becoming flesh—is the theological ground for understanding why Paul's benediction speaks of "the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ."

context: beginning was the word and the word was with God. And the word was God.
point_summary: John 1:1 and 1:14 are read together to ground "the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ" in the incarnation — the eternal Word who was God becoming flesh, full of grace and truth.

context: The same was in the beginning with God.
point_summary: John 1:2's affirmation that the Word "was in the beginning with God" grounds the incarnation's significance—getting Christ's identity wrong carries eternal consequences, making right belief about Jesus non-negotiable.

context: All things were made by him. And without him was not anything made that was made
point_summary: John 1:3's affirmation that all things were made by the Word establishes the pre-incarnate deity of Christ, grounding the "grace of the Lord Jesus Christ" in the miracle of the incarnation.

context: in him was life. And the life was the light of men.
point_summary: Life residing in the incarnate Word connects John 1:4 to the broader passage, establishing that the grace Paul invokes in his benediction is grounded in the person of Christ who became flesh.

context: verse number 14. The Bible says, and the word was made flesh and dwelt among us. And we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the father. Notice this full of grace and truth.
point_summary: John 1:14's description of the incarnate Word as "full of grace and truth" grounds Paul's benedictory phrase "the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ" in the theological reality of the incarnation itself.

context: And it's full of grace and truth. We read at the end of verse number 14.
point_summary: The incarnation of the Word made flesh is identified as the source of grace and truth, grounding the benedictory phrase "the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ" in Christ's coming in human form.

context: for the law was given by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ
point_summary: Grace and truth are identified as gifts that came specifically through Jesus Christ's incarnation, distinguishing his person and work from the law given through Moses.
