point_summary: God's love, cited from John 3:16, is identified with redemption—Calvary being named as love's greatest expression, demonstrated by the Father giving His Son to save rather than condemn the world.

context: God, he sent not a son of the world to condemn the world, but that the world through him might be saved

context: He says at the end of the book in verse number 36
point_summary: John 3:36 is cited to show that God's love carries an unavoidable binary: belief in the Son brings everlasting life, while unbelief leaves the wrath of God abiding on a person.

context: I quoted John 3, in verse 16
point_summary: God's love, illustrated by John 3:16, is identified here specifically with redemption—the Father's giving of His Son as the supreme demonstration of love, culminating at Calvary.

context: he that believeth on the Son hath everlasting life, and he that believeth not the Son shall not see life, but the wrath of God abideth on him
point_summary: God's love, expressed in redemption, carries both promise and warning: belief in the Son grants everlasting life, while unbelief leaves one under the abiding wrath of God.

context: without me you can do nothing
point_summary: Because no man can tame the tongue alone (James 3:8), Jesus's words in John 15:5—"without me you can do nothing"—establish that guarding the tongue requires dependence on God, not human effort.

context: He is the light of the world
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he came to his own and his own received him not

context: as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God
point_summary: Receiving Christ is presented as the dividing line between those who reject him and those who are granted the right to become sons of God, illustrating why gospel proclamation must reach every person.

context: He came to his own and his own received him not
point_summary: John 1:11 underlies the observation that Israel rejected Christ ("he came to his own and his own received him not"), yet many Jewish people were nonetheless saved by grace.

context: what he says in verse number 20
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I am gonna back up to verse number 14, and we'll read down through verse number 20
point_summary: Believers, born again through the word of truth, are not of the world—a reality Jesus himself declared in prayer and that James's call to be "firstfruits" presupposes.

context: I have given them thy word. The world hath hated them because they are not of the world even as I am not of the world
point_summary: Believers are not of the world even as Christ is not, yet are sent into it, sanctified through God's word of truth to preach the gospel — a reality Jesus prays over them in John 17.

context: I pray not that thou shouldest take them out of the world, but that thou shouldest keep them from the evil
point_summary: Christ's prayer that believers be kept from evil rather than removed from the world establishes the tension between their heavenly identity and their earthly mission to preach the gospel.

context: Sanctify them through thy truth. Thy word is truth
point_summary: God's word is the instrument of sanctification, identified here as truth, connecting Jesus's high-priestly prayer directly to James's warning against erring from that truth.

context: As thou hast sent me into the world, even so have I also sent them into the world
point_summary: Believers are sent into the world as Christ was sent—commissioned to preach the gospel—a mission belonging to every born-again believer, grounded in Christ's own high-priestly prayer.

context: and for their sakes I sanctify myself that they also might be sanctified through the truth

context: Neither pray I for these alone, but for them also what shall believe on me through their word
point_summary: Christ's prayer in John 17:20 extends beyond the first disciples to all future believers, including present-day Christians, who came to faith through the unbroken chain of faithful gospel preaching across two millennia.

context: Jesus is speaking of as he's praying in John 17
point_summary: Christ's prayer in John 17 encompasses all future believers, including those today, because faithful gospel preaching across 2,000 years has continuously brought souls to salvation through the word.

context: it's very like what Jesus prays in John 17

context: being born again
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The Bible says you must be born again

context: John 3 and verse number 16. For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son that whosoever believeth in him should not perish but have everlasting life
point_summary: Everlasting life in John 3:16 is marshaled as a plain-sense witness to eternal security, contrasting "everlasting" with what God did not promise—temporary or probationary life.

context: John chapter number 10, it's one of my favorite passages of Scripture
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I give unto them eternal life. And then notice what he says, and they shall never perish. Neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand. My Father, which gave them me, is greater than all, and no man is able to pluck them out of my Father's hand
point_summary: Christ's double guarantee in John 10:28—eternal life given and the promise "they shall never perish"—is cited as clear biblical evidence against the possibility of losing one's salvation.

reference_text: I am the bread of life

point_summary: Jesus's self-declaration as the bread of life in John 6 is used to parallel the "heavenly gift" language of Hebrews 6, illustrating how some who encountered him turned away without true saving faith.

context: at the end of verse number 60 this is a hard saying who can hear it

context: from that time many of his disciples went back and walked no more with him

context: Lord to whom shall we go thou hast the words of eternal life and we believe and are sure that thou art that Christ the son of the living God
point_summary: Peter's confession that Christ alone holds the words of eternal life is set against Judas's presence among the Twelve, illustrating that proximity to Christ and orthodox profession do not guarantee genuine saving faith.

context: Jesus answered them have not I chosen you twelve and one of you is a devil
point_summary: Judas, though chosen by Christ as one of the Twelve, is called "a devil," illustrating that proximity to Jesus and outward discipleship do not guarantee genuine salvation.

context: he spake of Judas Iscariot the son of Simon for he it was that should betray him being one of the twelve

context: John chapter number 13

context: when Jesus had thus said he was troubled in spirit and testified and said verily verily I say unto you that one of you shall betray me

context: there was leaning on Jesus bosom one of the disciples whom Jesus loved

context: Simon Peter therefore beckoned to him that he should ask who it should be of whom he spake

context: Jesus said one of you's gonna betray me

context: what John 1 12 says it doesn't say just believe on Jesus has received to as many

context: Look in John chapter 15 if you will. In John chapter 15 I'm going to read a little lengthy passage of scripture here

context: He says in verse 18 of John 15, if the world hates you, you know that it hated me before it hated you.

context: If ye were of the world, the world would love his own. But because ye are not of the world, but I have chosen you out of the world, therefore the world hateth you.
point_summary: Because believers are chosen out of the world, the world's hatred and persecution of them—intensifying to martyrdom during the tribulation—is the inevitable consequence Christ himself foretold.

context: Remember the word that I said unto you, the servant is not greater than his Lord. If they have persecuted me, they will also persecute you.
point_summary: Persecution of Christ's followers is not incidental but inevitable, because the world's hatred of believers flows directly from its prior hatred of Christ himself.

context: But all these things will they do unto you for my namesake, because they know not him that sent me.
point_summary: Persecution of believers flows directly from the world's rejection of Christ himself, as Jesus warned his disciples that all such things would be done to them "for my name's sake."

context: If I had not come and spoken unto them, they had not had sin, but now they have no cloak for their sin.

context: He that hateth me, hateth my Father also.

context: If I had not done among them the works which none other man did, they had not had sin, but now have they both seen and hated both me and my Father.

context: we're in the world, but we're not of the world

context: that wonderful verse of Scripture in John 3 in verse number 16

context: in verse number one of that chapter, the Bible says that Jesus loved them unto the end

context: a new commandment I give unto you that you love one another

context: This new commandment that I give you, that you love as I have loved you

context: in John 13, turn over to John 15, if you will, and in John chapter 13, the Bible says that he loved them unto the end

context: turn over to John 15, if you will

context: this is my commandment, that you love one another as I have loved you

context: a new commandment, give I unto thee

context: greater love hath no man than this, that a man lay down his life for his friends

context: John 3, 16

context: I have declared unto them thy name, and will declare it, that the love wherewith thou hast loved me may be in them, and I in them

context: the love wherewith thou hast loved me may be in them, and I in them

context: By this, Jesus said in John 13 and verse number 35

context: by this, by your love one for another, shall all men know that you're my disciples

context: He came to his own and his own received him not.

context: But as many as received him, to them gave he the power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me

reference_text: God so loved the world, that He gave His only begotten Son, that whosoever believes in Him should not perish, but have everlasting life

context: God so loved the world, and He died so that mankind would not have to go and spend eternity in the lake of fire

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me

context: We've passed from death unto life

context: his mother, so it's Mary, saith unto the servants, notice this statement, whatsoever he saith unto you, what are the next two words? Do it.

context: It was the beginning of miracles according to verse number 11

context: Sanctify them through thy word, thy word is truth, John 17 and 17

point_summary: Hatred from the world is expected for believers because Jesus himself experienced it first, making trials no surprise to the faithful.

context: he must increase, I must decrease

context: God is a spirit and they that worship Him must worship Him in spirit and in truth
point_summary: Genuine worship of God, who is spirit, stands in contrast to the church functioning as a mere corporation, requiring both spirit and truth rather than institutional methods alone.

context: he came to his own, his own received him not, but as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name
point_summary: Eternal life cannot be earned or purchased but only received as a gift, illustrated by the contrast between those who rejected Christ and those who accepted him and received the right to become God's children.

context: if you look in John 10 and 1 Peter, that we know his voice and we have eternal life and we shall never perish
point_summary: Eternal security belongs to those who have received Christ as Savior—they know his voice, have eternal life, and shall never perish.

context: no man spake like this man, for he speaketh with authority
point_summary: Authentic teaching of God's word carries inherent authority—as witnessed of Jesus himself—distinguishing it from the self-promoting eloquence of false teachers.

context: he must increase, but I must decrease
point_summary: Self-diminishment before Christ—"he must increase, but I must decrease"—is presented as the liberating posture that frees a believer from fear of others' opinions and enables more effective gospel witness.

context: I pray, God, that you would convict them of their sin and of Christ's righteousness and of judgment to come
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: For God so loved the world that he gave, right? Is that what John 3, 16 says?
point_summary: God's identity as a giving God — established by John 3:16's declaration that He "gave" His Son — grounds the proper perspective that Christian generosity is a response to divine generosity, not a transaction.

context: he came to his own and his own received him not. But as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God
point_summary: Eternal life cannot be earned or purchased but only received, as John 1:12 frames salvation as the act of receiving Christ, which grants the power to become sons of God.

context: you must be born again is what Jesus said to the religious leader Nicodemus
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I am the way, the truth and the life. No man cometh unto the father but by me
point_summary: Jesus' self-identification as "the way, the truth, and the life" is applied to the eunuch's sincere searching, with the point that genuine desire for truth will be met by the one who is Truth himself.

context: Sanctify them through thy word. Thy word is truth
point_summary: Truth is not relative but absolute — grounded in God's Word — as affirmed by Christ's own prayer that the Father sanctify believers through the Word, which is truth.

context: For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in Him should not perish but have everlasting life
point_summary: God's love expressed in giving His Son cuts through Satan's religious confusion with the clear gospel offer that believing in Christ means everlasting life rather than perishing.

context: He's condemned already according to John 3 and verse 36
point_summary: Those without the Son stand already condemned, making the gospel's offer of life urgent for anyone uncertain of heaven.

context: behold, the Lamb of God which taketh away the sin of the world.
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration that Jesus is the Lamb who "taketh away the sin of the world" is used to show that Christ's atoning work removes sin's separating barrier regardless of its magnitude.

context: God so loved the world
point_summary: God's love for the world drove Him to deal with sin through Christ's cross, but the gift must be personally received—rejecting it leaves one's sin and separation from God unchanged.

context: He came to His own and His own received Him not
point_summary: John 1:11 illustrates that the gift of salvation can be offered and yet rejected, leaving one's sin—and its eternal separation from God—fully intact.

context: as many as received Him, to them gave He power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on His name
point_summary: Receiving Christ, not merely hearing about Him, is what removes sin's separation and grants the power to become a child of God reconciled to the Father.

context: he that cometh to me, I will in no wise cast out
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 6:37 that he will turn away no one who comes to him is cited as assurance that he is a willing, loving Savior ready to receive any sinner who responds.

context: they're going to hate you, but they hated me before they hate you
point_summary: Anticipated opposition from the world, echoed in Jesus's warning that the disciples would be hated as He was hated, is cited as reason to resolve never to quit the race.

context: Jesus says in John 13 that he loved them to the end
point_summary: Jesus's love for his disciples "to the end" (John 13) is cited as the model undergirding Paul's selfless, spent-for-others love toward the Corinthians.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life, no man cometh unto the Father but by me
point_summary: Jesus is the exclusive Savior—the only name under heaven by which men must be saved—invoked here to expose false apostles whose message contradicts that singular gospel.

context: the term born again is a Bible term. I said, if I could take the Bible and show you where Jesus used that term born again
point_summary: Regeneration is distinct from baptism — being "born again" is a Bible term Jesus used that false teachers co-opt with redefined meaning, equating it with water baptism rather than salvation.

context: He came to his own. His own received him not according to John 1 verse 11.
point_summary: John 1:11–12 is used to frame the gospel's simplicity: Christ came to his own who rejected him, yet receiving him as a free gift—not earned or purchased—grants one the power to become a son of God.

context: But as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God.
point_summary: Receiving Christ as a free gift—not purchased or earned—is what grants the power to become a son of God, contrasted with those who rejected him in the preceding verse.

context: Thy word is truth
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: it is finished
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: if we receive not, if we believe not, that we're condemned already in John chapter number 3
point_summary: Condemnation apart from belief in Christ is the personal backdrop against which the preacher frames his own conversion, citing John 3 to establish the guilty, condemned state from which he was saved.

context: For God so loved the world
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: God so loved the world
point_summary: God's universal love in John 3:16 became personally applied to the preacher as a 17-year-old, as the Holy Spirit convicted him of guilt and condemnation before his conversion.

context: The Bible says in John chapter number 16 that he reproves the world of sin to convince
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's convicting work described in John 16—reproving the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment—is cited as the divine agency that brought personal awareness of guilt and condemnation before conversion.

context: my sheep, my sheep, hear my voice, and I know them, and I give unto them eternal life
point_summary: Christ's promise that His sheep hear His voice and receive eternal life grounds the assurance that believers can know—not merely hope—their salvation is secure.

context: he must increase and I must decrease
point_summary: Conversion replaces self-centered living with a new purpose: decreasing oneself so that Christ increases, as John the Baptist declared.

context: he says he Jesus must increase and I must decrease
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration that "he must increase and I must decrease" is applied as the defining new purpose of the regenerate life—self displaced, Christ exalted.

context: we're in the world, we're just not of the world
point_summary: Salvation brings an eternal perspective that transforms how believers view time, possessions, and life in the world, even while they remain in it.

context: He loves the whole world
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: you're of your father, the devil, and the lust of your father you will do. Notice this, he was a murderer from the beginning and abode not in the truth because there is no truth in him. When he, Lucifer, Satan, when he speaketh a lie, he speaketh of his own, for he is a liar and the father of it
point_summary: Satan's defining character as murderer and liar from the beginning establishes the nature of the spiritual warfare that has raged since his fall and that Stephen's persecutors embodied.

context: the thief cometh not but for to steal and to kill and
point_summary: John 10:10 presents the sharpest possible contrast between the thief—identified as Lucifer—whose sole purpose is to steal, kill, and destroy, and Christ, who comes to give abundant life.

context: I am come that they might have life and they might have it more abundantly
point_summary: John 10:10 presents an absolute contrast: the thief—identified as Satan—comes only to steal, kill, and destroy, while Christ comes to give abundant life.

context: if the world hates you, ye know that it hated me before it hated you
point_summary: The world's hatred of believers is traced to its prior hatred of Christ, establishing that persecution follows from belonging to him rather than to the world.

context: ye were of the world, the world would love his own. But because you're not of the world, but I have chosen you out of the world, therefore the world hateth you
point_summary: Believers are hated by the world precisely because Christ has chosen them out of it, making that hatred a mark of belonging to him rather than to the world.

context: you're in the world, but you're not of
point_summary: Believers are in the world but not of it, making them strangers and pilgrims whose true home lies elsewhere, a reality that explains the world's hatred toward them.

context: these things have I spoken unto you that ye should not be offended
point_summary: Jesus forewarned his disciples that religious persecutors would kill them believing they served God, so that when such opposition came, his followers would not be caught off guard or fall away.

context: They shall put you out of the synagogues, yea, the time cometh that whosoever killeth you will think that he doeth God service
point_summary: Persecution of Christ's followers by those who believe they serve God is not incidental but was forewarned by Jesus himself as a mark of spiritual warfare against his disciples.

context: these things will they do unto you because they have not known the Father nor me
point_summary: Religious persecution of believers stems from persecutors' fundamental ignorance of both the Father and the Son, a reality Jesus forewarned so his disciples would not be caught off guard.

context: But these things have I told you that when the time shall come, you may remember, ye may remember that I told you of them. And these things I said not unto you at the beginning because I was with you
point_summary: Forewarning of persecution serves a preserving function: Christ disclosed these things in advance so that when opposition comes, his followers will remember he predicted it and not be caught off guard.

context: the truth that we see in John 8 verse 44
point_summary: John 8:44, read alongside John 10:10 and 1 Peter 5:8, identifies the devil as the believer's active adversary, establishing that Christians are engaged in ongoing spiritual warfare against a real enemy.

context: again in John 10 in verse number 10
point_summary: John 10:10's depiction of the thief who comes to steal, kill, and destroy is cited alongside John 8:44 as scriptural grounding for the reality that believers face a real adversary engaged in active spiritual warfare against them.

context: the enemy, the threefold purpose, is to steal, kill, and destroy
point_summary: Satan's threefold purpose to steal, kill, and destroy (John 10:10) is cited as the driving force behind spiritual warfare, explaining why the enemy consistently targets God's people throughout biblical history.

context: if they knew me, they would not do this
point_summary: Those who oppose the gospel do so not out of mere hostility but because, not knowing the Father, they are unsaved — making them the mission field rather than the enemy.

context: before Abraham was I am
point_summary: Stephen's sweeping survey from Abraham to Jesus mirrors Christ's own claim in John 8:58 — "Before Abraham was, I am" — establishing Jesus as the eternal fulfillment of all Israel's history and prophecy.

context: the thief cometh not
point_summary: Satan's destructive agenda—stealing, killing, and destroying—is cited as the driving force behind millennia of Christian martyrdom, explaining why the church has faced persecution throughout history.

context: the threefold purpose of stealing, killing, and destroying
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Notice with me in John chapter number six, and we'll look at just a couple of verses this evening and note the example, but in John chapter number six and verse number 59
point_summary: Jesus teaching in the synagogue at Capernaum illustrates that authoritative instruction flows from the word itself, not the teacher's personal standing, a pattern believers carry when they bring Scripture to the next generation.

context: John six and verse number 59, these things said he in the synagogue as he taught in Capernaum
point_summary: John 6:59 is cited as evidence that Jesus modeled a pattern of regular, authoritative teaching, grounding the call to instruct future generations in his own example.

context: In the beginning was the word, and the word was with God, and the word was God
point_summary: Christ's eternal preexistence and divine richness—established in John 1:1—grounds Paul's appeal in 2 Corinthians 8:9 that the incarnation was a voluntary condescension from infinite wealth to poverty for believers' sake.

context: all things were made by him. Without him was not anything made that was made
point_summary: John 1:3's declaration that all things were made by Christ establishes his eternal, divine richness—the theological ground for understanding what he voluntarily relinquished in the incarnation.

context: John chapter number one
point_summary: Christ's eternal preexistence and divine richness—established in John 1:1—grounds Paul's appeal in 2 Corinthians 8:9 that the incarnation was a voluntary condescension from infinite wealth to poverty.

context: there's one that is preferred before me. Why? For he was before me
point_summary: John 1:30's testimony that Christ existed before John the Baptist—despite John's physical seniority—is cited as evidence of Christ's eternality, grounding the claim that "he was rich" in 2 Corinthians 8:9.

context: and this is life eternal, that they might know thee, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom thou hast sent
point_summary: Christ's pre-incarnate glory—the riches he possessed with the Father before the world was—grounds the claim that he was rich before becoming poor for our sakes.

context: I have glorified thee on the earth. I have finished the work which thou gavest me to do
point_summary: Christ's pre-incarnate glory—the riches he possessed with the Father before the world was—grounds the claim that he was rich before he became poor for our sakes.

context: And now, O Father, glorify thou me with thine own self, with the glory which I had with thee before the world was
point_summary: Christ's pre-incarnate glory with the Father, referenced in his prayer that it be restored, grounds the claim that he was genuinely rich before becoming poor for our sakes.

context: the precious spirit of God who resides in our heart, who has come to teach us, to bring all things to remembrance
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's indwelling role as teacher and reminder (John 14:26) is invoked to ground the call for surrender, framing submission to the Spirit as the believer's proper response to divine conviction and instruction.

context: God so loved the world
point_summary: God's love for "the whole world" is held alongside his love for each individual person, grounding the call for personal, unashamed praise of a God worthy of it.

context: John chapter number five and the Bible says that there's a great multitude of impotent folk that were lying there at Bethesda
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: a man that had an infirmity for 38 years
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he came to his own and his own received him not but as many as received him to them gave he power to become the sons of God even to them that believe on his name
point_summary: John 1:11's familiar language about receiving Christ is invoked to contrast the usual human-as-receiver framing with Revelation 4:11's striking reversal: God himself is the one receiving glory, honor, and power.

context: according to John chapter number 10
point_summary: Christ's identity as the good shepherd who gave his life for the sheep (John 10) grounds the assurance that salvation is his work alone, not a combination of his effort and ours.

context: for God so love the world kind of God
point_summary: God's universal love ("For God so loved the world") grounds the personal truth that he works individually in each heart, not only in humanity broadly.

context: that time when the friend of Jesus, Lazarus, died. Both Martha and Mary, before he died, when he was grievously ill, they sent to the Lord and they said, your friend Lazarus is ill
point_summary: God's perceived inactivity—illustrated by Jesus deliberately delaying His journey to Bethany despite knowing Lazarus was ill—does not mean God is absent or unresponsive, but that He is working beyond what we can yet see.

context: if you'd have come, if you'd have done it my way, my brother wouldn't be dead yet. My brother would still be living
point_summary: God's perceived inactivity—illustrated by Jesus deliberately delaying His arrival at Bethany—does not mean He is absent or unresponsive, but that He is working beyond what we can yet see.

context: You didn't come. You didn't do it how I thought you should do it
point_summary: Even mature, spiritually devoted believers can fall into bitter complaint that God has not acted on their timetable, as both Martha and Mary did when Jesus delayed coming to Lazarus.

context: Jesus said, I will come again
point_summary: Christ's promised return in John 14:3 grounds absolute confidence that every unfulfilled prophecy will be fulfilled, shaping how believers live as they await that day.

context: I am the way of the truth and the life. No man, no woman, no boy, no child, no teenager, no person cometh unto the Father but by Me
point_summary: Jesus Christ's own declaration that He alone is the way, the truth, and the life dismantles the illusion that personal goodness can secure access to the Father.

context: He that cometh to me I will in no wise cast out
point_summary: Christ's promise in John 6:37 — "Him that cometh to me I will in no wise cast out" — is cited as assurance that Jesus will receive any sinner who comes to Him in faith.

reference_text: destroy this temple and in three days I'll raise it up
context: He said, destroy this temple and in three days I'll raise it up
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus said let not your heart be troubled
point_summary: Christ's knowledge of his disciples' troubled hearts is the point — he knows not only their location but their inner condition, their sadness, as John 14:27's promise of peace meets real-world tribulation.

context: these things have I spoken unto you, that in me ye might have peace. In the world ye shall have tribulation, but be of good cheer. Why? I have overcome the world.
point_summary: Even amid the tribulation Christ promised his followers would face, he knows not only their location but their inward condition—their sadness—and draws near to them there.

context: we have tribulation in the world
point_summary: Even amid the tribulation Christ promised his followers would face, he draws near with full knowledge of their condition—their location and their sorrow—because he has overcome the world.

context: we've never been in bondage to anyone
point_summary: The disciples on the road to Emmaus, like the religious leaders in John 8:33 who denied ever being in bondage, sought only temporal deliverance from Rome, blind to the greater redemption Christ came to accomplish.

context: God so loved the world
point_summary: God's love for "the world" in John 3:16 is personally applied to each individual believer, as the sermon moves from the universal statement to the intimate reality that God loves *me*.

context: Thy Word is truth
point_summary: Sanctification and spiritual growth depend on regular, faithful engagement with Scripture, which alone contains the truth God has given for his people — neglecting it leaves believers vulnerable to error.

context: without me, ye can do nothing
point_summary: Doubt arises when God's people forget that their ability is irrelevant—as Jesus declared, apart from Him they can do nothing, making every task entirely dependent on God.

context: I'm the vine, you're the branches
point_summary: Doubt arises when God's people shift focus from divine ability to their own, forgetting that apart from Christ they can do nothing—as Israel forgot God's promise and declared themselves unable.

context: without him, we can do nothing
point_summary: Doubt takes root when believers forget that fruitfulness was never about their own ability, since Jesus declared that apart from him they can do nothing.

context: Jesus said in John 10 in verse number 10 I am come to give them life and if they might have it more abundantly
point_summary: Christ as the last Adam is contrasted with the first Adam to show that where Adam brought death and curse, Christ came to bring life and abundance.

context: Thy word is truth
point_summary: Scripture alone is the reliable source for knowing God's true character, making the word of God—which is truth—the believer's defense against the enemy's lies about who God is.

context: my sheep hear my voice, and I know them, I give them an eternal life, and they shall never perish
point_summary: Eternal security is affirmed through John 10:27, but only as a backdrop to urging believers to confess sin and maintain unbroken fellowship with God.

context: for God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son
point_summary: God's love for the world, voiced through John 3:16, is held in tension with His holiness: He never sacrifices righteousness on the altar of love.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. He says, no man cometh unto the Father but by Me.
point_summary: The single door of Noah's ark serves as a typological picture of Christ's exclusive claim in John 14:6—that He is *the* way, not *a* way, to the Father.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth, and the life
point_summary: The definite article in "I am the way" — not "a way" — establishes Christ's exclusivity as the singular path to God, just as the ark had one door, not many.

context: Jesus did say, I am the way, the truth, and the life.
point_summary: Christ's exclusive claim — "I am the way, the truth, and the life" — is presented as confirmed rather than contradicted by the ark's single door, refuting the notion that Jesus never declared himself the only way to heaven.

context: as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God
point_summary: Receiving Christ as Savior places the believer "in Christ," a security the sermon parallels with Noah's command to come into the ark.

context: the devil, he's the father of lies, and he's a liar from the beginning, John 8
point_summary: Satan's character as the father of lies and a liar from the beginning is invoked as the contrast to God, who never lies, urging hearers to believe God's word rather than the devil's deception.

context: go to John chapter 1
point_summary: Creation's completion is not a truth confined to Genesis; John 1 is cited to demonstrate that the Creator-God of Genesis is attested throughout all of Scripture.

context: John 1 in the beginning was the Word and the Word was with God and the Word was God
point_summary: Creation's completion in Genesis is confirmed across all Scripture, as John 1:1–3 identifies the Word—Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God—as the one through whom all things were made.

context: verse number 14 says and the Word was made flesh and dwelt among us and we beheld his glory the glory is of the only begotten of the Father full of grace and truth
point_summary: John 1:14 is cited to identify the incarnate Word as the same eternal Creator through whom all things were made, reinforcing that the doctrine of creation is not confined to Genesis.

context: beginning was the Word the Word was with God and the Word was God the same was in the beginning with God
point_summary: John 1:1 is cited to demonstrate that Jesus Christ, as the eternal Word co-equal with the Father, is the Creator of all things — confirming that the doctrine of creation is not confined to Genesis but runs throughout Scripture.

context: all things how many things all things were made by him and without him was not anything made that was made
point_summary: Creation's scriptural witness is not confined to Genesis; John 1:3 is cited to show that Jesus Christ, the eternal Word, is the agent through whom all things without exception were made.

context: the Word was made flesh and dwelt among us
point_summary: The incarnation of the eternal Word (John 1:14) is cited to establish that Jesus Christ, co-equal and co-eternal with the Father, is the same Creator through whom all things were made.

context: We see it in the book of John, chapter number 20, 21, you see reference to that.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: turn to the book of John, if you will, in the seventh chapter
point_summary: The Feast of Tabernacles serves as the historical and liturgical backdrop for John 7, grounding Jesus's actions and words within a living first-century observance that included the water-pouring ceremony at the Temple.

context: after these things, Jesus walked in Galilee, for he would not walk in Jewry, because the Jews sought to kill him
point_summary: Jesus' avoidance of Judea despite the Feast of Tabernacles obligation illustrates the tension between his duty as a Jewish male to appear in Jerusalem and the mortal threat the Jewish leaders posed to him.

context: now the Jews' feast of tabernacles was at hand
point_summary: The Feast of Tabernacles placed Jesus under obligation as a Jewish male to travel to Jerusalem, creating the tension between his legal duty and the mortal danger awaiting him there.

context: his brethren therefore said unto him, Go hence and go into Judea, that thy disciples also may see the works that thou doest. For there is no man that doeth anything in secret, and he himself seeketh to be known openly
point_summary: Jesus' unbelieving brothers press Him to go publicly to Jerusalem for the Feast of Tabernacles, setting up the tension between their demand for open display and His declaration that His time has not yet come.

context: If thou do these things, show thyself to the world, for neither did his brethren believe in him
point_summary: Jesus's attendance at the Feast of Tabernacles despite the Jews seeking to kill him illustrates the tension between his obligation to the law and the timing of his appointed hour.

context: My time is not, notice this, yet come, but your time is already ready
point_summary: Jesus's declaration that "my time is not yet come" at the Feast of Tabernacles is read as evidence that his attendance and its timing were governed by a divine timetable, not merely family or social pressure.

context: The world cannot hate you, but me it hateth, because I testify of it, that the works that are of are evil
point_summary: The world's hatred of Christ is grounded in his testimony against its evil works, distinguishing his situation from that of his unbelieving brothers who faced no such opposition.

context: Go ye up unto this feast. And then he says this, I go not up yet unto this feast, for my time is not yet full come
point_summary: Christ's declaration that "my time is not yet full come" frames his deliberate, law-observant attendance at the Feast of Tabernacles as governed by divine timing rather than family pressure or personal safety.

context: When he had said these words unto them, he abode still in Galilee
point_summary: Jesus' attendance at the Feast of Tabernacles, though veiled in secrecy due to murderous opposition, demonstrates that he was bound by the law's requirement yet sovereignly governed by his own appointed time.

context: Then went he also up unto the feast, not openly, but as it were in secret
point_summary: Jesus' secret attendance at the Feast of Tabernacles in John 7:10 is presented as evidence that the feast carried legal obligation for Jewish males, which Jesus himself was bound to fulfill.

context: Then the Jews sought him at the feast and said, Where is he?
point_summary: Jesus' obligation as a Jewish male to attend the Feast of Tabernacles in Jerusalem frames the tension of John 7, where the Jews' murderous intent forced him to go secretly rather than openly.

context: in the last day, that great day of the feast, Jesus stood and cried
point_summary: On the final day of the Feast of Tabernacles, as priests poured water from the pool of Siloam upon the altar, Jesus declared himself the true fulfillment of that ritual by offering living water to all who thirst.

context: if any man thirst, let him come unto me and drink
point_summary: On the final day of the Feast of Tabernacles, as priests poured water from the pool of Siloam upon the altar, Jesus declared himself the true source of living water for all who thirst.

context: he that believeth on me as the scripture hath said, out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water
point_summary: Against the backdrop of the priest pouring water from the pool of Siloam upon the altar on the feast's final day, Jesus declares that the believer becomes a source of living water—identified as the Holy Spirit not yet given.

context: But this spake he of the spirit, which they that believe on him should receive for the holy ghost was not yet given because that Jesus was not yet glorified.
point_summary: John 7:39 identifies the "living water" Jesus promised at the Feast of Tabernacles as the Holy Spirit, not yet given because Christ had not yet been glorified—connecting the water-pouring ceremony to its fulfillment in him.

context: They say at the end of the text in verse 46, never man spake like this man.
point_summary: The crowd's verdict — "Never man spake like this man" — confirms that Jesus' declaration of Himself as the source of living water, made on the climactic last day of the feast, was recognized as utterly unprecedented.

context: if any man thirst, let him come unto me and drink
point_summary: Jesus' declaration "if any man thirst, let him come unto me and drink," spoken on the final day of the Feast of Tabernacles as priests poured water from Siloam, reveals him as the fulfillment of the law's shadow.

context: Now you recall that back in John chapter number four, Jesus had that interaction with the woman at the well
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus observed it in John 7
point_summary: The Feast of Tabernacles, observed by Israel and kept by Jesus in John 7, is shown to foreshadow the millennial reign of Christ, when all nations will be required to keep it literally.

context: Jesus said this, John 14 in verse number six
point_summary: God's pre-foundation determination that redemption is possible only through Christ is confirmed by Christ's own exclusive claim in John 14:6 that no one comes to the Father except through Him.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me
point_summary: Redemption is exclusively through Christ because God determined before the foundation of the world that there is no other way, as Christ himself declared in John 14:6.

context: Peter who heard the Lord Jesus Christ make that statement in John 14
point_summary: God determined before the foundation of the world that redemption is possible only through Christ, as Jesus himself declared in John 14:6 that no one comes to the Father except through him.

context: John in chapter number one and verse number 12 says, but as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name
point_summary: Receiving Christ and believing on His name results in adoption into God's family—the redeemed become children of God, not by merit, but through Jesus Christ alone.

context: he's the good shepherd and that he lays down his life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's voluntary self-sacrifice is underscored by John 10's declaration that no man takes his life from him—he lays it down willingly for the sheep.

context: he said this, it is finished
point_summary: Christ's cry "It is finished" from John 19:30 is expounded as the declaration that the entire plan of redemption was completed at the cross, leaving no human work to add to it.

context: we should abide in Christ
point_summary: Redemption, once granted, is permanent and kept by God's power—not maintained by the believer's effort—securing an incorruptible, heavenly inheritance that can never be lost.

context: my sheep hear my voice and I know them and I give unto them eternal life and they shall never perish
point_summary: Redemption through Christ is permanent, not probationary—Jesus' promise in John 10:27 that His sheep receive eternal life and shall never perish grounds the assurance that believers are kept by God's power, not their own effort.

context: And Jesus said this in John 15
point_summary: Redemption through Christ's blood is the ultimate expression of love, as Jesus himself defined it: laying down one's life for his friends.

reference_text: greater love hath no man than this, that a man lay down his life for his friends
context: Jesus said this, greater love hath no man than this, that a man lay down his life for his friends
point_summary: Christ's laying down His life on the cross for His friends is presented as the supreme act of love that accomplished redemption for a humanity in need of it.

context: Without him we can do nothing
point_summary: Worship demands unhurried priority because, as John 15:5 underscores, without God we can do nothing — every breath is His gift, making Him too great to be merely fit into a schedule.

context: Jesus said there in John chapter number 4 when he was speaking to the woman at
point_summary: God sets the terms of acceptable worship, and Jesus' declaration that true worshippers must worship in spirit and in truth confirms that worship must conform to what God himself has prescribed.

context: God is a spirit and they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth
point_summary: God's own words in John 4:24 establish that worship must be conducted in spirit and in truth, on His terms rather than man's.

context: they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth
point_summary: True worship requires spirit and truth, not mere outward form—a standard invoked to condemn Israel's corrupt offerings in Malachi as hollow ritual rather than genuine worship.

context: he gives to us the bread of life
point_summary: Christ's self-identification as the bread of life in John 6:35 grounds the sermon's claim that God's provision is ultimately spiritual sustenance — refreshing and restoring the soul — not merely physical supply.

context: Thy word is truth
point_summary: God's word, cited as "thy word is truth" from John 17:17, is identified as the means by which God leads his people in paths of righteousness, providing directional guidance for the sheep under his care.

context: In the beginning was the word, and the word was with God, and the word was God
point_summary: John 1:1 is cited to establish that Jesus, as the eternal Word who existed before creation, possesses the everlasting nature required to offer humanity everlasting life.

context: in the beginning was the word, and the word was with God, and the word was God
point_summary: John 1:1's identification of the Word as God, present "in the beginning," establishes Christ's eternal pre-existence—the necessary ground for His ability to offer everlasting life to humanity.

context: The same was in the beginning with God
point_summary: John 1:2's affirmation that the Word was "in the beginning with God" establishes Christ's pre-existence and eternal nature as the foundation for His unique ability to offer everlasting life.

context: All things were made by him, and without him was not anything made that was made
point_summary: Christ's eternal pre-existence, demonstrated by John the Baptist's declaration that Jesus "was before me" despite being born after him, establishes that Jesus is the everlasting God who alone can offer everlasting life.

context: The word was made flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth
point_summary: The incarnation of the eternal Word in John 1:14 establishes that Jesus, God in flesh, possesses the everlasting nature required to offer humanity everlasting life.

context: John bear witness of him and cried, saying, this was he of whom I spake. He that cometh after me is preferred before me, for he was before me
point_summary: John the Baptist's declaration that Jesus—though born after him—existed before him establishes Christ's eternal pre-existence as evidence that he is the everlasting God capable of offering everlasting life.

context: in the opening statements of the gospel of John, we see very abundantly clear that Jesus is God, and we see the eternality, the fact that he is the everlasting God
point_summary: John the Baptist's birth preceding Jesus' earthly birth yet his own declaration that Christ "was before me" establishes Jesus' eternal pre-existence, confirming the opening claim of John 1:1 that the Word already existed before creation.

context: John 8 in verse number 58
point_summary: Jesus's claim "before Abraham was, I am" is presented as an unambiguous assertion of eternal deity, confirmed by the Jews' immediate attempt to stone him for blasphemy.

context: verse number 57, the Jew said unto him, thou are not yet 50 years old, and hast thou seen Abraham?
point_summary: John 8:57 frames the Jews' challenge about Jesus's age as the setup for His "I AM" declaration in verse 58, which the sermon presents as an unmistakable claim to eternal deity—confirmed by the Jews' immediate attempt to stone Him.

context: before Abraham was, I am
point_summary: Jesus's claim "before Abraham was, I am" is identified as an unambiguous assertion of deity and eternality, confirmed by the Jews' immediate attempt to stone him for blasphemy.

context: and now, O Father, glorify thou me with thine own self, with the glory which I had with thee before the world was
point_summary: Christ's pre-incarnate glory shared with the Father "before the world was" is cited as direct evidence of His eternality and deity.

context: For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have what? Everlasting life.
point_summary: Because God is eternal, the everlasting life he offers in John 3:16 flows directly from his own everlasting nature as El Olam.

context: We find that this promise in John 3, in verse 16, in John 10, in verse number 28, the Lord Jesus Christ is speaking about the fact that he's the good shepherd

context: My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me
point_summary: Because God is eternal, he alone can guarantee eternal life — the sheep he knows will never perish and cannot be taken from his hand.

context: And I give unto them eternal life, and they shall never perish, neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand
point_summary: Because God is eternal, his promise of everlasting life in John 10:28 carries absolute certainty — only an everlasting God can guarantee that his sheep shall never perish.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me

context: and ye now therefore have sorrow, but I will see you again, and your heart shall rejoice, and your joy no man taketh from you

context: you shall know the truth and the truth is gonna what? It's gonna make you free

context: What did Jesus say in John chapter number eight?
point_summary: Knowing the truth, as Jesus promised in John 8:32, is what sets believers free from false teaching—contrasted here with those who depart from the faith by following doctrines of devils.

context: Are we blind also?

context: you shall know the truth and the truth shall make you free. John 8 verse 32
point_summary: Knowing God's Word is the remedy against false teaching, as truth encountered through Scripture is what sets believers free from deception.

context: search the scriptures
point_summary: Scripture itself commands believers to search the Scriptures, making diligent study of God's Word—particularly Genesis—essential for establishing the foundation of Christian faith.

context: when Jesus then lifted up His eyes and saw a great company come unto Him, He saith unto Philip, When shall we buy bread that these may eat?
point_summary: God's question to Philip in John 6:5 was not born of ignorance but of divine purpose, for verse 6 reveals He already knew what He would do—asking only to prove Philip.

context: Philip answered Him, 200 penny worth of bread is not sufficient for them that every one of them may take a little
point_summary: When human resources are exhausted and man confesses his own insufficiency, God already knows what He will do, and the glory belongs to Him alone.

context: In verse number 6, the Bible says this, and this He said to prove Him. For He Himself knew what He would do.
point_summary: God's testing of Philip — asking a question to which He already knew the answer — illustrates that divine trials are not inquiries born of ignorance but deliberate proofs of whether His people truly trust His power and provision.

context: the lad is mentioned in verse 9, there's a lad here which hath five barley loaves and two small fishes
point_summary: The lad's meager lunch—five loaves and two fish—illustrates that human resources appear hopelessly insufficient against impossible need, yet God works beyond what any natural calculation can account for.

context: they had as much as they would at the end of verse number 11

context: he said unto his disciples, gather up the fragments that remain, that nothing be lost. There's a whole principle that we can gain from verse 12

context: it says in verse 13, therefore they gathered them together and filled twelve baskets with the fragments of the five barley loaves which remained over and above unto them that had eaten
point_summary: God's provision at the feeding of the five thousand exceeded what was available at the start—twelve baskets of fragments remaining after all were filled—demonstrating that divine supply surpasses human calculation.

context: to Philip, when shall we buy bread that these may eat?
point_summary: God's question to Philip in John 6:5 was a test of faith, designed to prove whether Philip truly believed God was able to meet an impossible need.

context: They that worship him, God is a spirit. They that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth.
point_summary: God's terms alone govern acceptable worship, as the sermon draws on John 4:24 to establish that man cannot approach God any way he chooses but must come in spirit and in truth.

context: Jesus who stood and boldly made the declaration in John 14 and verse 6 when He said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by Me
point_summary: Christ's exclusive claim in John 14:6 is cited as God's prescribed way of acceptance, contrasting with any human offering or works-based approach that, however sincere, falls short of God's standard.

context: I am the way, the truth, and the life
point_summary: Christ's exclusive claim in John 14:6 establishes that God alone prescribes the acceptable way of approach, ruling out any human offering—however sincere—as sufficient for righteousness.

context: John chapter number 11, the scriptures give us the account of the death of Lazarus

context: I'm gonna jump in at verse number 20. The Bible says, then Martha, as soon as she heard that Jesus was coming, went and met him, but Mary sat still in the house.

context: In verse 21, I wanna draw your attention here. The Bible says, then said Martha unto Jesus, Lord, if thou had been here, my brother had not died.
point_summary: Martha's words "Lord, if thou hadst been here" are read as a confession of genuine faith—she believed Jesus had power over disease when physically present, though her faith had not yet stretched to resurrection.

context: You find in verse 23, actually, let's skip down.

context: Jesus saith unto her, I am the resurrection and the life

context: He that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live. And whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die

context: and then said the Jews, behold how he loved him

context: Man that was born blind, Jesus heals him
point_summary: John 9's healing of the man born blind is cited by the mourners as evidence of Jesus's power, underscoring that faith should extend beyond what he has already done to what he can still do.

context: Jesus therefore again, groaning in himself, cometh to the grave

context: Satan is a liar. He is the father of it. He is a deceiver

context: Without me, Jesus said, you can do nothing

context: it's only the truth that God said would make men free

context: the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world

point_summary: Genesis 22:8's "God will provide himself a lamb" is read as dual fulfillment: a substitute for Isaac, and ultimately Christ as the Lamb of God bearing away the world's sin in substitutionary atonement.

context: The Bible tells us in John 16 and verse 13 that the spirit of truth was going to come and the Holy Ghost says he shall guide you

context: he will guide you into all truth
point_summary: The Holy Spirit, identified as the divine teacher, guides believers into all truth—meeting the human need for divine instruction that no self-sufficient person can supply on their own.

context: peace I leave with you, my peace I give unto you. Not as the world giveth, give I unto you. Let not your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid.
point_summary: Peace is not found in the absence of trouble but in the presence of God, as Christ's promise in John 14:27 demonstrates that his peace transcends worldly circumstances and cannot be taken away.

context: I have spoken unto you that in me ye might have peace. In the world ye shall have tribulation, but be of good cheer, I have overcome the world.
point_summary: Peace is not found in the absence of trouble but in the presence of God, as Christ's own words in John 16:33 promise peace "in me" even amid guaranteed tribulation.

context: Remember, we looked at John 11 last week, and in John 11, we dealt with the fact that God wants us to believe.

context: in John 15, herein we bring God glory that we bear fruit

context: He said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Salvation comes exclusively through Christ — "I am the way, the truth, and the life" — ruling out baptism, tongues, religious rites, or family heritage as any basis for reaching heaven.

context: God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life

context: they that believe not on him, they're condemned already
point_summary: Those who do not believe in Christ stand condemned already, a divine verdict that makes gospel proclamation an urgent necessity for a perishing world.

context: In John chapter number 15, we're reminded that Jesus is the true vine

point_summary: Fruitfulness in the believer's life requires abiding in Christ the true vine, who as husbandman both removes unfruitful branches and purges fruitful ones so they bear more fruit.

context: now you're clean through the word, which I have spoken unto you

context: abide in me and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit of itself, except it abide in the vine, no more can ye except ye abide in me.
point_summary: Fruitfulness in the believer's life depends entirely on abiding in Christ, the true vine, apart from whom no branch can bear fruit of itself.

context: I am the vine, you're the branches. He that abideth in me and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit, for without me he can do nothing.
point_summary: Fruitfulness in the believer's life depends entirely on abiding in Christ, since apart from him—as the true vine—no branch can bear fruit of any kind.

context: If a man abide not in me, he is cast forth as a branch and is withered. Men gather them and cast them into the fire and they are burned.
point_summary: Fruitfulness in the believer's life requires abiding in Christ as the vine, since apart from him no branch can bear fruit — a truth illustrated by Joseph's fruitful life in Genesis.

context: If you abide in me and my words abide in you, you shall ask what you will and it shall be done unto you
point_summary: Abiding in Christ as the vine is the condition for bearing much fruit, which the Father is glorified by — connecting directly to Joseph's fruitfulness as a type of Spirit-dependent productivity.

context: Herein, notice this verse in John 15 and verse eight, herein is my Father glorified that ye bear much fruit
point_summary: Bearing much fruit glorifies the Father, and abiding in Christ is the indispensable condition for that fruitfulness, as illustrated in Joseph's life described as "a fruitful bough."

context: We read in John chapter number 10, when Jesus stands and says, I am the good shepherd. We find that it's the good shepherd that he giveth his life for the sheep.

context: My peace give I unto thee. Not as the world giveth, give I unto thee.

context: We must abide in Christ. We must understand that it brings glory to God when we're fruitful, when we bear fruit. We're to bear much fruit.

context: it brings glory to God when we're fruitful, when we bear fruit

context: they hated me, they're going to hate you. They're going to put you out. They're going to kill you. They're going to think that they're doing some great thing.

context: if you love me, keep my commandments

reference_text: worship him in spirit and in truth

context: Without me, Jesus said, you can do nothing. Without me, you can do nothing.

context: It's like John the Baptist. He must increase, I must decrease

context: if you've seen me you've seen the Father
point_summary: Colossians 1:15's description of Christ as "the image of the invisible God" is read through the lens of John 14:9—Christ's incarnation manifests and reveals the Father, not as a created being but as the eternal Creator himself.

context: John one in verse number five
point_summary: The light of Genesis 1:3 is identified with God himself, supported by John 1:5's declaration that God is light shining in darkness, distinct from the sun created on day four.

context: In the beginning was the word
point_summary: The light of Genesis 1:3 is identified with the eternal Word of John 1:1–5, who is himself life and light, distinct from the sun created on day four.

context: and the word was with God, and the word was God
point_summary: The light of Genesis 1:3 is identified not as the sun—created on day four—but as God himself, with John 1:1–9 invoked to show that Christ, the eternal Word, is the true light.

context: The same was in the beginning with God
point_summary: The light God spoke into existence on the first day is identified with God himself, since Christ—the Word present in the beginning—is the true light that shines in darkness.

context: All things were made by him, and without him was not anything made that was made
point_summary: The light of Genesis 1:3 is identified not as the sun—created on day four—but as God himself, the uncreated light whose nature John 1:3–9 confirms by attributing all creation and life-giving light to the pre-incarnate Word.

context: In him was life, and the life was the light of men
point_summary: Life in Christ is identified as the light of men, establishing that the primordial light of Genesis 1:3 points to God himself rather than any created celestial body.

context: And the light shineth in darkness, and the darkness comprehendeth it not
point_summary: John 1:5's identification of Christ as the light shining in darkness supports the claim that the light of Genesis 1:3 refers not to the sun but to God himself, since God is light.

context: there was a man sent from God whose name was John
point_summary: John the Baptist's explicit denial that he was the light, and his role as witness to it, is used to confirm that Jesus Christ — not John, not the sun — is the true light referenced from the beginning.

context: The same came for a witness to bear witness of the light, that all men through him might believe

context: He was not that light, but was sent to bear witness of that light

context: Jesus is the word

context: He told them to search the scriptures for the scriptures testified of him in John chapter number 5

context: Jesus Christ said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
