context: He says in verse 38, what seek ye?
point_summary: Christ's question "What seek ye?" frames the sermon's comfort that God is not only the God who sees us, but who actively engages those who draw near, inviting them to "come and see" where He dwells.

context: verse 39 says they came and saw where He dwelt and abode with Him that day for it was about the tenth hour
point_summary: Christ's invitation "Come and see" in John 1:39 frames the sermon's central comfort: God is not a distant, hidden deity but one who draws near and welcomes personal encounter.

context: Peter going and finding his brother and saying in verse 41
point_summary: The joyful proclamation "we have found the Messiah" in John 1:41 illustrates how God is actively working, calling, and drawing people to Christ through the witness of one believer to another.

context: Philip findeth Nathanael, and saith unto him, we have found him, of whom Moses and the law and the prophets did write, Jesus of Nazareth, the son of Joseph
point_summary: Philip's declaration that Jesus is the one "of whom Moses and the law and the prophets did write" identifies Jesus as the fulfillment of all Old Testament Scripture, a truth the risen Christ himself confirmed on the road to Emmaus.

context: Nathanael said unto Him, can there any good thing come out of Nazareth?
point_summary: Nathanael's dismissive question about Nazareth is answered not by argument but by the same invitation extended earlier—"come and see"—showing that encounter with Jesus himself is the sufficient response to skepticism.

context: Philip then saith unto Him, come and see, Jesus saw Nathanael coming to Him, and notice what Jesus says, it says, and saith unto Him, behold, in Israel I indeed, in whom is Nogaiel
point_summary: Nathanael's skeptical question — "Can any good thing come out of Nazareth?" — gives way to full confession of Christ as Son of God once Jesus reveals He had already seen Nathanael, demonstrating the transforming power of being known by God.

context: Then Nathanael says in verse 48, he saith unto Him, whence knowest thou me? Jesus answered and said unto him, just notice this phrase, before that Philip called thee, when thou wast under the fig tree, notice these words, I saw thee
point_summary: Jesus's declaration "I saw thee" before Philip called Nathanael connects to the sermon's El Roi theme—God is the one who sees, whose omniscient gaze precedes and transforms human doubt into confession.

context: Now notice in verse 49, Nathanael answered and saith unto Him, Rabbi, thou art the Son of God, thou art the King of Israel
point_summary: Nathanael's confession of Jesus as Son of God and King of Israel in verse 49 stands in direct contrast to his earlier skepticism in verse 46, the turning point being Christ's declaration "I saw thee."

context: Nathanael speaking to Philip in verse number 46
point_summary: Nathanael's dramatic reversal—from skeptical dismissal in verse 46 to full confession of Christ's identity in verse 49—is traced entirely to Jesus' declaration "I saw thee," revealing that being known by God transforms doubt into worship.

context: what then Nathanael says to Jesus in verse 49
point_summary: Nathanael's sudden confession of Jesus as Son of God and King of Israel is traced directly to Christ's declaration "I saw thee," revealing that being truly seen by God produces transforming faith.

context: Jesus said, before that Philip called thee, when thou wast under the fig tree, I saw thee
point_summary: Jesus's words "I saw thee" — spoken before any introduction — become the sermon's lens for El Roi, the God who sees, demonstrating that divine sight precedes human awareness and calls the doubting to rest in God's knowledge of them.

context: Let not your heart be troubled
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the Bible would tell us in John chapter number 2 that those that were gathered together, they knew not how these things had come about, but the servants, the ones that were with Jesus, they knew
point_summary: Those who are with Jesus, like the servants at Cana, receive knowledge of what others cannot explain—proximity to God yields truth unavailable to the wider crowd.

context: the Lord Jesus Christ in John four said, I must needs go through Samaria, the woman at the well in John chapter number four
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: if you can't send anything more than John 3.16, please send John 3.16
point_summary: John 3:16 is cited as the irreducible minimum of saving truth a people group desperately sought, illustrating how Scripture alone reveals that God loves humanity and that Jesus is the only way of salvation.

context: Look at John chapter 10, if you will
point_summary: Because God identifies himself as our shepherd, believers have biblical warrant to expect his protection—not presumptuously, but as a natural implication of the shepherd-sheep relationship he has established.

context: the thief cometh not but for to steal and to kill and to destroy
point_summary: Against the thief who comes only to steal, kill, and destroy, Jesus the Good Shepherd stands in contrast as the one who gives abundant life and, unlike the hireling, never abandons his sheep to the wolf.

context: I am come that they might have life, that they might have it more abundantly
point_summary: Believers are warranted to expect protection from Christ because, unlike the thief who destroys and the hireling who flees, the Good Shepherd remains with and lays down his life for his sheep.

context: I'm the good shepherd, the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's identity as the Good Shepherd is distinguished from the hireling by his refusal to flee when danger comes, establishing that believers can expect genuine protection from one who owns and cares for the sheep.

context: he that is an hireling and not the shepherd, whose own the sheep are not, seeth the wolf cometh
point_summary: Unlike the hireling who flees at danger and leaves the sheep vulnerable, the Good Shepherd's genuine ownership and care guarantee that he will not abandon his flock to the wolf.

context: Do you see that in the middle of verse 12? It's what the hireling does, that's what the thief does. It says that he sees the wolf coming and he fleeth, and the wolf catcheth them and scattereth the sheep.
point_summary: Unlike the hireling who flees at the wolf's approach, the Good Shepherd stays—implying that Christ, as our shepherd, does not abandon his sheep when danger comes.

reference_text: I am the good shepherd and know my sheep and am known of mine
context: I am the good shepherd and know my sheep and am known of mine.
point_summary: Christ's identity as the Good Shepherd is contrasted with the hireling's flight from danger, establishing that unlike one who abandons the sheep, Jesus stays and lays down his life for them.

reference_text: As the Father knoweth me, even so know I the Father, and I lay down my life for the sheep
context: As the Father knoweth me, even so know I the Father, and I lay down my life for the sheep.
point_summary: Christ's willingness to lay down his life for the sheep, contrasted with the hireling who flees at danger, establishes that the Good Shepherd does not abandon his flock when threat comes.

context: and I give unto them eternal life and they shall never perish, neither shall any man
point_summary: The eternal security of believers is grounded in Christ's own declaration that He gives His sheep eternal life and that no man can pluck them out of His hand.

context: Neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand
point_summary: Christ's promise that no one can pluck His sheep from His hand, reinforced by the Father's equal grip in verse 29, establishes a "double security" grounded in the Good Shepherd's unfailing protection of His own.

context: My Father which gave them Me is greater than all, and no man is able to pluck them out of My Father's hand
point_summary: The Father's greater power, combined with the Son's hold, creates what the sermon calls "double security" — the believer held simultaneously in Christ's hand and the Father's hand.

context: My sheep hear My voice, and I know them, and I give unto them eternal life
point_summary: Mutual recognition between Christ and His sheep — "I know them and they follow me" — grounds the double security of believers held in both the Son's and the Father's hand, never to be plucked out.

context: We see in John 11 a divine delay
point_summary: God's protective work sometimes operates through divine delay, as seen in John 11, where he allows difficulty in one area to shield his sheep from a greater unseen harm.

context: Turn over to John 19, if you will
point_summary: John's account of Gethsemane is read to show that Christ's arrest in the garden—where He knowingly surrendered to those who came for Him—was the event that made restoration to the tree of life possible.

context: look in chapter number 18
point_summary: The Garden of Gethsemane, where Jesus was arrested, is presented as the pivotal counterpart to Eden—the place where Christ's willing surrender made restoration of the tree of life possible.

context: the Bible tells us in verse number one of John 18, when Jesus had spoken these words, he went forth with his disciples over the brook Kedron, where was a garden into the which
point_summary: John 18:1 is read to establish that Gethsemane was a garden where Jesus, knowing all things that would come upon him, was arrested—the pivotal contrast to Eden that made the tree of life in Revelation 22 accessible.

context: he entered and his disciples. And Judas also, which betrayed him, notice what it says, knew the place, for Jesus oft times resorted thither with his disciples
point_summary: Judas knew Gethsemane as Jesus' habitual place of prayer, making that garden the appointed site where Christ, fully knowing what lay ahead, was arrested and delivered to the cross.

context: Judas then, having received a band of men and officers from the chief priests and Pharisees, cometh thither with lanterns and torches and weapons
point_summary: In the Garden of Gethsemane, Jesus—fully knowing all that was coming upon him—stepped forward to meet the armed band, demonstrating his willing, sovereign submission to the cross.

context: Jesus therefore knowing all things that should come upon him
point_summary: Jesus's voluntary self-disclosure — "Whom seek ye?" — demonstrates that he went to the cross with full foreknowledge of his suffering, not as a victim but in willing obedience to the Father's redemptive purpose.

context: if you read chapter number 17, he says, Father, the hour is come
point_summary: Jesus's prayer "Father, the hour is come" in John 17:1 is read as his declaration that he was going to the cross to finish the work the Father gave him to do.

context: verse number four of chapter 18, Whom seek ye?
point_summary: Jesus's voluntary self-disclosure — "Whom seek ye?" — demonstrates that he went to the cross with full foreknowledge, not as a victim but in willing obedience to the Father's redemptive purpose.

context: They answered him, Jesus of Nazareth. And Jesus said, I am he
point_summary: Christ's sovereign declaration "I am he" in Gethsemane — spoken with full foreknowledge of the cross — caused his armed arresters to fall backward, demonstrating his willing submission rather than helpless capture.

context: as soon then as he said unto them, I am he, they went backward and fell to the ground
point_summary: When Jesus declared "I am he" and the arresting party fell backward to the ground, his sovereign foreknowledge of the cross is shown to be not a defeat but a willing, purposeful surrender to accomplish the Father's redemptive work.

context: chapter number 19 of the scourging and the crucifixion
point_summary: The scourging and crucifixion recorded in John 19 are presented as the culminating work of Christ that restores access to the tree of life, lost in Eden and recovered in the eternal state.

context: chapter number 20 and you read of the resurrection
point_summary: John 20's resurrection account is cited as the redemptive turning point that restores access to the tree of life, lost in Genesis 3 and recovered through Christ's completed work from Gethsemane to the cross.

context: that started in the Garden of Gethsemane when He was arrested there in John chapter 18
point_summary: The arrest of Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane (John 18) is presented as the beginning of the redemptive work on the cross that ultimately restores humanity's access to the tree of life lost in Eden.

context: this is the condemnation, that light is coming to the world
point_summary: Light's arrival in the world through Christ exposes the reason the wicked attack: men love darkness rather than light because their deeds are evil.

context: in the beginning was the word of the word was with God and the word was God
point_summary: John 1:1 is cited briefly to establish that the "light" coming into the world in John 3:19 is Jesus, the incarnate Word, before the sermon moves on without further exposition of the verse itself.

context: in him was life in verse number four, and the life was the light of men
point_summary: Life in Christ is identified as the light of men, establishing why the world's hatred of believers is ultimately a hatred of the light that exposes evil deeds.

context: I am the light of the world
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: this is the condemnation that light is coming to the world and men love darkness rather than light. Why? Because their deeds were evil
point_summary: Men love darkness rather than light because the light of Christ exposes and convicts their evil deeds, explaining why the wicked instinctively oppose the righteous.

context: Go forward with me to John chapter 15. Notice what Jesus says... I want you to see what Jesus says in John chapter 15 in verse number 18. Notice the Lord Jesus Christ says this. If the world hates you, ye know that it hated me before it hated you.
point_summary: The world's hatred of believers is grounded in its prior hatred of Christ, and if the world loves a Christian, something is spiritually wrong.

context: If you were of the world, the world would love his own, but because you're not of the world, but I have chosen you out of the world, therefore the world hateth you.
point_summary: The world's hatred of believers is not incidental but inevitable, because Christ has chosen them out of the world, making them as foreign to it as he himself was.

context: If you were of the world, the world would love his own
point_summary: Because believers are chosen out of the world by Christ, the world's hatred of them is not incidental but inevitable—and if the world loves us, something is spiritually wrong.

context: they're gonna desire to kill you
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I have given them thy word and the world hath hated them because they are not of the world, even as I am not of the world
point_summary: The world's hatred of believers is grounded in their separation from it through the Word, not in any hostility the righteous themselves initiate toward the ungodly.

context: the world's going to hate you because you're not of the world
point_summary: Believers face the world's hatred as an expected reality because, as Jesus declared, they are not of the world—a hostility that should be met with trust in God rather than retreat.

context: men love darkness rather than light
point_summary: Men love darkness rather than light, confirming that the world's hostility toward believers is not surprising but expected, and that this hatred is rooted in rejection of the gospel light.

context: turn to John 10, if you will, and we see the good shepherd
point_summary: John 10 is cited to establish that Jesus, as the Good Shepherd, is distinguished from the hireling by his willingness to give his life for the sheep, contrasted with the thief who comes only to steal, kill, and destroy.

context: the thief cometh not, but for to steal and to kill and to destroy. Notice the contrast. Jesus says, I am come that they might have life and that they might have it more abundantly
point_summary: John 10:10's contrast between the thief who comes to steal, kill, and destroy and Christ who comes to give abundant life frames Jesus as the Good Shepherd who willingly laid down his life for the sheep.

context: I am the good shepherd. And he says this, the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's identity as the Good Shepherd is defined by his willing self-sacrifice—giving his life for the sheep—contrasted with the hireling who abandons them in danger.

context: he that is an hireling and not the shepherd whose own the sheep are not, seeeth the wolf coming and leaveth the sheep and fleeth, and the wolf catcheth them and scattereth the sheep
point_summary: In contrast to the hireling who abandons the flock when danger comes, Jesus as the Good Shepherd willingly laid down his life for the sheep, bearing sin in their place.

context: The hireling fleeth because he's an hireling and careth not for the sheep
point_summary: Unlike the hireling who abandons the flock out of self-interest, Jesus as the Good Shepherd willingly laid down his life for the sheep, bearing sin in their place.

context: I am the good shepherd and know my sheep and am known of mine. As the father knoweth me, even so know I the father
point_summary: As the Good Shepherd, Jesus is distinguished from the hireling by his willingness to lay down his life for the sheep, demonstrating sacrificial ownership and care.

context: I lay down my life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's voluntary self-sacrifice for the sheep, stated in John 10:15, is presented as the defining act of the Good Shepherd, distinguishing him from the hireling who abandons the flock.

context: and other sheep I have, which are not of this fold, them also I must bring, and they shall hear my voice, and there shall be one fold and one shepherd
point_summary: Christ's voluntary self-sacrifice for "other sheep" beyond the immediate fold points forward to his resurrection, as he lays down his life with the declared intention of taking it again.

context: Therefore doth my father love me because I lay down my life, that I might take it again
point_summary: Christ's self-giving death was entirely voluntary, not a fate imposed by Rome, as the verse's declaration that no man takes His life anchors the resurrection promise "that I might take it again."

context: no man taketh it from me
point_summary: Christ's death was entirely voluntary, not a murder forced upon him by Rome — he laid down his life of himself, with full authority to do so.

context: no man taketh it from me. He willingly went to the cross of Calvary. He could have called scores, legions of angels, but he chose to go to that cross. He says, I lay it down of myself. I have power to lay it down and I have power to take it again. This commandment have I received of my father.
point_summary: Christ's voluntary surrender of his life — not Roman execution — is the point of John 10:18, as illustrated by a personal account of correcting a classmate who claimed Jesus was simply murdered against his will.

context: he gave up the ghost
point_summary: Christ's voluntary surrender of his life on the cross — not execution by Rome — is the point, grounded in Jesus' own declaration that no man takes his life from him but that he lays it down of himself.

context: he willingly laid down his life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's self-substitutionary death was entirely voluntary — no man took his life from him — as he willingly bore the sin of the world in the place of his sheep.

context: Jesus, our shepherd, he has revealed to us as being the good shepherd and the good shepherd gave his life for his sheep
point_summary: Christ's death was not a murder forced upon him but a willing self-sacrifice, as he himself declared he had power to lay down his life for the sheep.

context: our shepherd, the good shepherd, the Lord Jesus Christ, would give his life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's self-giving death for his sheep in John 10:11 is shown to fulfill the prophetic suffering depicted in Psalm 22, establishing Jesus as the good shepherd who literally laid down his life.

context: in John chapter number 10 that Jesus, the Good Shepherd, that He gives His life for the sheep
point_summary: John 10 identifies Jesus as the Good Shepherd who gives His life for the sheep, the first of three New Testament shepherd titles traced through Psalm 22, 23, and 24.

context: Jesus said this in John 16, He said, in me you have peace. In the world you shall have tribulation, but in me you have peace
point_summary: Christ's peace, not the world's, is available to believers precisely because he has overcome the world—cited here to contrast tribulation with the surpassing peace Jesus alone provides.

context: my peace give I unto thee. Not as the world giveth
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus Christ is the Good Shepherd, He giveth His life for the sheep
point_summary: Christ's title as "Good Shepherd" in John 10:11 is defined by His self-giving death for the sheep, which is then contrasted with His title as "Great Shepherd" in Hebrews 13, where the resurrection is the defining emphasis.

context: Maybe that would be John 3 16 or some other verse
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he heard Jesus speak in John 14
point_summary: Peter's firsthand presence at the foot-washing in John 13 sets the stage for his hearing Jesus speak the words of John 14, grounding his confident hope in the Chief Shepherd's return in direct personal experience.

context: he was the one there in John 13 when Jesus is washing the feet of the disciples
point_summary: Peter's firsthand witness of Jesus—including the foot-washing episode in John 13—grounds his confident apostolic authority to write about the Chief Shepherd's return.

context: when Jesus is washing the feet of the disciples in John 13
point_summary: Peter's bold yet erratic responses during the foot-washing—refusing, then overcorrecting—illustrate Jesus's lesson on humility and daily cleansing for service.

context: let not your heart be troubled, to begin chapter 14 of the book of John
point_summary: Peter's firsthand witness of Jesus speaking the words of John 14:1–3 grounds his confident hope in a coming again and a prepared place, because Jesus, who cannot lie, spoke these promises directly to him.

context: let not your heart be troubled, you believe in God, believe also in me
point_summary: Peter's firsthand witness of Jesus speaking the words of John 14:1–3 grounds his confident hope in a coming again and a prepared place, because he heard those promises directly from One who cannot lie.

context: In my father's house are many mansions
point_summary: Peter's firsthand witness of Jesus's promise in John 14:2—that he goes to prepare a place and will return—grounds the apostle's confident hope in the certainty that Jesus, who cannot lie, will fulfill his word.

context: if it were not so, I would have told you, I go to prepare a place for you and if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and receive you unto myself that where I am there ye may be also
point_summary: Jesus's promise in John 14:1—"let not your heart be troubled"—anchors Peter's later confidence in Christ's return, connecting the Upper Room assurance directly to Peter's eyewitness testimony and apostolic writing.

context: from John 14, one through three
point_summary: Jesus's promise in John 14:1—"let not your heart be troubled"—is presented as the foundational word Peter personally heard, anchoring his later confidence in Christ's promised return.

context: he heard Jesus speak this in John 14, he hears the instruction of Jesus
point_summary: Jesus's promise in John 14:1—"let not your heart be troubled"—is presented as the foundational word Peter personally heard, anchoring his later confidence in Christ's promised return.

context: destroy this temple. In three days I'll raise it up again
point_summary: Christ's resurrection—foretold in his own words about raising the temple of his body in three days—establishes him as the great Shepherd who conquered death.

context: He's not a hireling. When the enemy comes, he doesn't flee
point_summary: Unlike the hireling who flees when danger comes, Christ the Good Shepherd loves his own with an everlasting love and will not abandon them.

context: My sheep, hear my voice and follow me
point_summary: Sheep hearing and following the Shepherd's voice (John 10:27) illustrates the relational dynamic of Jehovah Raah—God as shepherd who guides his people as a flock.

context: he is a good shepherd
point_summary: Christ as the chief shepherd will never fail or disappoint his people, contrasting the fallibility of under-shepherds, because he is inherently and perfectly good.

context: He is a good shepherd
point_summary: Christ's identity as the "good shepherd" in John 10:11 grounds the sermon's opening exploration of what shepherding means, establishing that knowing the shepherd's role is prerequisite to understanding what it means to have the Lord as one's shepherd.

context: my sheep hear my voice and they follow me
point_summary: Sheep's instinctive recognition of their own shepherd's voice illustrates why believers must learn to hear Christ's voice—which comes through Scripture—so he can guide them.

context: let not your hearts be troubled you believe in God believe also in me in my father's house or many mansions if it were not so I would have told you I go to prepare a place for you and if I go I will come again and receive you unto myself that where I am there ye may be also
point_summary: Jesus's promise to return (John 14:1–3), heard firsthand by Peter, is the very pledge that end-times scoffers mock when they demand, "Where is the promise of his coming?"

context: marvel not if they hate you for they hated me before they're gonna hate you
point_summary: Hatred directed at Paul by his accusers is traced back to their hatred of God, echoing Jesus's warning in John 15:18 that the world's hostility toward believers originates in its prior rejection of Christ.

context: I believe that Jesus gives us insight to this in John chapter 13
point_summary: Washing before service in the tabernacle foreshadows Christ's washing of the disciples in John 13, where Peter's resistance reveals that the deeper meaning of such cleansing cannot yet be understood.

context: In John 13, notice in verse number six, then cometh he, Jesus, to Simon Peter and Peter saith unto him, Lord, dost thou wash my feet?
point_summary: Peter's resistance to Christ washing his feet illustrates the tension between recognizing Christ's lordship and submitting to his servant act of consecration for service.

context: Jesus answered and said unto him, What I do, thou knowest not now, but thou shalt know hereafter
point_summary: Present incomprehension of Jesus's actions is not grounds for refusal, because understanding will come later—a principle Jesus applies directly to Peter's objection to the foot-washing.

context: if I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me
point_summary: Submission to Jesus's cleansing work is not optional for those who would share in him — Peter's refusal would have cost him all part with Christ.

context: Simon Peter saith unto him, Lord, not my feet only, but also my hands and my head
point_summary: Peter's impulsive request for total washing — hands, head, and feet — sets up Jesus' distinction in verse 10 between complete cleansing already received and the ongoing washing still needed.

context: he that is washed needeth not save to wash his feet
point_summary: Partial bathing suffices for one already clean — Jesus' distinction between full washing and foot-washing illuminates the Old Testament consecration rites, where initial cleansing and ongoing maintenance were separate acts.

context: if we can take the truth of John chapter 13 and apply it back to the Old Testament
point_summary: Jesus washing the disciples' feet illustrates the distinction between the once-for-all cleansing of salvation and the ongoing removal of daily defilement, paralleling the priestly washings of Exodus 29–30.

context: verse number 11 of John 13. It says, for he knew who should betray him, that being Judas, therefore said he, ye are not all clean
point_summary: Judas's uncleanness amid the washed disciples illustrates that the initial washing of salvation must precede any consecration for priestly service.

context: here in John chapter 13, when we understand the truth of Exodus 29 and 30
point_summary: Jesus washing his disciples' feet in John 13 illustrates the humility of one who came to serve rather than be served, and typologically parallels the priestly washing required before consecrated service in Exodus 29–30.

context: Jesus in John 13 gave that instruction that you're to love one another
point_summary: Jesus' new commandment in John 13 — to love one another as He loved us, not merely as oneself — establishes the standard against which Cain's fratricide stands as the ultimate violation.

context: if the world hates you
point_summary: The world's hatred of believers is traced to their separation from it by Christ's choosing, mirroring the pattern of Cain's hatred of Abel whose righteous works exposed his own evil.

context: you know that it hated me before it hated you
point_summary: Hatred from the world is not incidental to faithful discipleship but is the expected and forewarned consequence of being chosen out of the world by Christ.

context: If ye were of the world, the world would love his own. But because you're not of the world, but I have chosen you out of the world, therefore the world hateth you
point_summary: Chosen out of the world and therefore hated by it, believers should expect persecution — a reality Jesus forewarned His disciples precisely to prepare them for what they would endure.

context: remember the word that I said unto you, the servant is not greater than his Lord. If they have persecuted me, they will also persecute you. If they have kept my saying, they will keep yours also
point_summary: Persecution of Christ's servants is not incidental but inevitable, as Jesus himself warned his disciples that a servant shares the same treatment as his Lord.

context: Let not your heart be troubled. You believe in God, believe also in me. In my Father's house are many mansions, if it were not so I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you. If I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again
point_summary: Jesus's promise of prepared heavenly mansions confirms that believers are strangers and pilgrims on earth not under judgment, like Cain, but because a better country awaits them.

context: receive you unto myself, that where I am, there ye may be also
point_summary: Christ's promise to prepare a heavenly dwelling contrasts the believer's pilgrim status on earth with Cain's cursed wandering, showing that our homelessness is hopeful, not punitive.

context: John chapter eight, as our manner has been here over these last four weeks, we're going to look at an example from the gospels
point_summary: John 8 is introduced as a Gospel example demonstrating that Jesus walked obediently, establishing the pattern believers are likewise called to follow.

context: beginning in verse number 27. The Bible says, they understood not that he spake to them of the father
point_summary: Obedience to the Father defined Christ's every word and action, as Jesus declares He speaks nothing of Himself but only what the Father taught Him.

context: then said Jesus unto them, when you have lifted up the son of man, then shall you know that I am he and that I do nothing of myself, but as my father hath taught me, I speak these things
point_summary: Christ's declaration in John 8:28 — that He does nothing of Himself but speaks only as the Father taught Him — is presented as the defining example of obedient dependence that believers are called to imitate.

context: it is finished
point_summary: Christ's declaration "I do always those things that please him" is presented as the model for the believer's call to continue in his word through a life of consistent obedience.

context: he that sent me is with me. The father hath not left me alone for I do always those things that please him
point_summary: Christ's declaration in John 8:29 — that He always did those things pleasing to the Father — is presented as the model of perfect obedience lived out in human flesh, never ceasing to be God yet fully man.

context: as he spake these words, many believed on him
point_summary: Belief prompted by Christ's words leads immediately to his call for continuing in that word — a life of consistent obedience that proves genuine discipleship.

context: If you continue in my word, then are you my disciples indeed
point_summary: Continuing in Christ's word—marked here as the defining evidence of true discipleship—carries the meaning of sustained, obedient conformity to God's teaching, not merely initial belief.

context: Verily, verily, I say unto you, if a man keep my saying, he shall never see death
point_summary: Keeping Christ's word carries the promise of never seeing death—a claim so staggering that his hearers accused him of having a devil, not grasping how infinitely greater he was than Abraham.

context: And then said the Jews unto him, now we know that thou hast a devil. Abraham is dead, and the prophets, and thou sayest, if a man keep my saying, he shall never taste of death
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus answered in verse 54 and said, if I honor myself, my honor is nothing. It is my father that honoreth me, of whom you say that he is your God, yet ye have not known him.
point_summary: Jesus's claim in John 8:54 that his honor comes from the Father, not himself, leads directly into his declaration "Before Abraham was, I am"—an unambiguous assertion of deity that his Jewish hearers understood and responded to with murderous rage.

context: Notice what they say in verse 57. Then said the Jews unto him, thou art not yet 50 years old, and hast thou seen Abraham?
point_summary: Jesus's declaration "before Abraham was, I am" is presented as an unambiguous claim to deity, confirmed by the Jews' immediate response of taking up stones to stone him.

context: And Jesus said unto them, verily, verily, I say unto you, before Abraham was, I am.
point_summary: Jesus's declaration "Before Abraham was, I am" is presented as an unambiguous claim to deity, confirmed by the Jews' immediate response of taking up stones to stone him.

context: This beginning of miracles did Jesus in the kingdom of Galilee manifest it forth his glory and his disciples believed on him
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: when Jesus said to Nicodemus in John chapter number 3, you must be born again
point_summary: Being born twice—physically and spiritually—means the second death has no power over you, making the new birth not optional but an absolute necessity as Christ declared.

context: you must be born again
point_summary: Being born again is not presented as optional — Jesus declared to Nicodemus that one *must* be born again or remain unable to see or enter the kingdom of God.

context: you can't see God if you're not born again
point_summary: Being born again is not presented as optional — Jesus declared to Nicodemus that it is an absolute necessity for seeing God and entering the kingdom of heaven.

context: Jesus says with regard to this in John 16 in verse number 21
point_summary: Childbirth pain, pronounced as judgment in Genesis 3, is illuminated by Jesus' own words in John 16:21, where sorrow in travail gives way to joy the moment the child is born.

context: a woman when she is in travail notice hath sorrow because her hour has come but as soon as she is delivered of the child she rememberth no more the anguish for joy that a man is born into the world
point_summary: Jesus' own words in John 16:21 are cited to confirm that the sorrow of childbirth is real yet temporary, swallowed by joy—illustrating the nature of the pain God multiplied as part of the curse in Genesis 3.

context: John chapter number 3
point_summary: John 3's "born again" teaching is cited as the New Testament counterpart to the spiritual death introduced in Genesis, showing that physical birth alone cannot restore what sin severed between God and man.

context: miracle from John chapter number nine when Jesus healed the man that was born blind
point_summary: The miracle of John 9—where religious leaders were physically sighted yet spiritually blind—illustrates the partial, temporary spiritual blindness that has come upon Israel during the present church age.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Heaven is a real place with only one way of entry — Jesus Christ alone, as his own words declare and Peter's proclamation in Acts 4 confirms.

context: for God so loved the world
point_summary: God's global redemptive love, summarized in "God so loved the world," grounds the church's participation in worldwide missionary work.

context: our focus is to be on the fields that are white unto harvest. Lift up your eyes. The Lord says this and he tells us that the harvest, listen, it's plenteous. The labors, they're few
point_summary: Distraction, not only disobedience, can blind believers to evangelistic urgency, making Christ's call to "lift up your eyes" on fields "white unto harvest" a necessary corrective for those too busy to see the lost.

context: For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life
point_summary: God's declaration in John 3:16 was spoken by Jesus to Nicodemus—the most religiously elite man in Israel—demonstrating that even supreme religious devotion cannot secure salvation.

context: Jesus had to look at Nicodemus and say, if you're not born again
point_summary: Even the most religiously accomplished person—like Nicodemus, a member of the elite 71-man Sanhedrin—still required new birth, because religion alone cannot save.

context: For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son
point_summary: Even the most religiously accomplished person — like Nicodemus, a member of the Sanhedrin — cannot be saved by religion alone but must be born again through faith in Christ.

context: that whosoever believeth in him should not perish but have everlasting life
point_summary: God's love expressed in giving his Son meets the world's greatest and most universal need—eternal life for "whosoever"—a need no religion, including Nicodemus's, can satisfy.

reference_text: this is the way, this is the truth and the light
context: this is the way, this is the truth and the light. This is Jesus
point_summary: The world's scattered, harassed condition is set against Christ alone as the shepherd-voice declaring "this is the way, this is the truth and the light," with John 14:6's threefold claim implicitly underlying the diagnosis of a world without a shepherd.

context: What about the woman in the well and going against Corrie? Going against cultural status and different things just to talk to her.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: if you know John 3, 16, you know enough right there. Just share John 3, 16 with him. For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten son
point_summary: Knowing John 3:16 is presented as sufficient theological equipment to share the gospel, countering the hindrance of feeling inadequately prepared.

context: that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life
point_summary: Knowing John 3:16 is sufficient gospel content to share with anyone, making lack of biblical knowledge an inexcusable hindrance to evangelism.

context: You can just use John 3, 16 to share the gospel
point_summary: Knowing John 3:16 is sufficient equipment to share the gospel with anyone, removing the hindrance of feeling inadequate or unprepared.

context: Say not ye there yet four months, and then cometh harvest? Behold, I say unto you, lift up your eyes and look on the fields, for they are white
point_summary: Missionary opportunity is not permanent — the "white" fields of John 4:35 signal a decisive moment of ripeness in which delay means irreversible harvest loss.

context: According to John chapter number 18 and verse 37,
point_summary: Christ's own declaration to Pilate — "To this end was I born, and for this cause came I into the world" — is cited as evidence that every step of Jesus' earthly life was purposefully directed toward Calvary.

context: To this end was I born, and for this cause came I into the world
point_summary: Christ's own words to Pilate — "To this end was I born, and for this cause came I into the world" — are cited as proof that Calvary was not an unforeseen tragedy but a death the Savior himself proclaimed and purposed.

context: John the Baptizer identified as the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world
point_summary: Abel's blood offering is read as a type of Christ, whom John the Baptist identified as the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world, establishing blood as the sole basis of acceptance before God.

context: in the gospel of John chapter 14 and verse 6, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man, and that includes you, and that includes me. No man, Jesus said, cometh unto the Father, but by
point_summary: Human effort and bloodless religion are rejected by God, while salvation comes exclusively through Christ—the one who declared himself the sole way to the Father.

context: Jesus said, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No man cometh unto the Father but by me.
point_summary: Jesus' self-identification as "the way, the truth, and the life" grounds the sermon's central claim that the truth Christians are called to pass to the next generation is God's truth, not man's.

context: Jesus said this, sanctify them through thy word. Thy word is truth.
point_summary: Jesus' prayer that the Father sanctify believers through His word—"Thy word is truth"—grounds the sermon's call to pass God's objective, divinely preserved truth to the next generation.

context: we need to understand thy word is truth
point_summary: Sanctification through Scripture—"thy word is truth" from John 17:17—is invoked to contrast the Bible's absolute reliability with the misinformation readily available through internet searches and AI tools.

reference_text: Jesus loves me, this I know
context: It blesses my heart to hear little Nova sing Jesus loves me, this I know
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: if you love me, keep my commandments
point_summary: Obedience to Christ's command—not mere profession of love—is the scriptural basis for financially and prayerfully supporting world evangelism as a concrete act of keeping His commandments.

context: the Lord Jesus Christ, the Bible tells us in the book of John in the eighth chapter that he always did that which pleased the father. We understand as we begin to look through, he says that at the end of verse number 29, for I do always those things that please him.
point_summary: Christ's perfect testimony—"I do always those things that please him"—establishes him as the supreme example whose steps believers are called to follow.
